metadata.description: Database komprehensif penyakit unggas (ayam, kalkun, itik, dll) yang mencakup informasi detail tentang penyakit viral, bacterial, parasitic, fungal, nutritional, metabolic, cardiovascular, reproductive, toxicological, environmental, dan management disorders.
metadata.source: Compiled from veterinary poultry disease references
metadata.intended_use: Diagnostic support, educational purposes, and disease surveillance in poultry farming

diseases.name: Avian Influenza

symptomsEnhanced.name: Egg shell(s), Misshapen (abnormal size or shape)

diseases.description: Avian Influenza (AI) is a highly contagious viral disease caused by Type A Orthomyxovirus strains, characterized by variations in hemagglutinin (HA) and neuraminidase (N) surface antigens. The disease manifests in two primary forms: Low Pathogenic Avian Influenza (LPAI) causing mild respiratory disease and production losses, and Highly Pathogenic Avian Influenza (HPAI or 'fowl plague') resulting in catastrophic mortality with viscerotropic and pansystemic infection. HPAI is particularly devastating, causing rapid death, severe respiratory and nervous signs, and is zoonotic with the potential to infect humans. The disease occurs worldwide and has significant economic impact through production losses, trade disruptions, and high costs for control and eradication. Sporadic outbreaks of HPAI result in severe losses, while endemic LPAI acts as an erosive disease reducing liveability and egg/broiler quality.
clinicalSigns: Sudden death (particularly in HPAI)
Acute and precipitous decline in egg production
Rapidly ascending mortality with respiratory and nervous signs
Subcutaneous hemorrhages and edema of the head
Cyanosis (blue discoloration) of the comb and wattles
Vesicle formation on comb and wattles
Swelling of face and wattles
Respiratory rales and difficulty breathing
Torticollis (twisted neck) and other nervous signs
Variable morbidity depending on strain pathogenicity
Depression and lethargy
Green diarrhea
LPAI: Low morbidity and mortality with production decline
HPAI: High morbidity (up to 100%) and mortality
transmission: Caused by Type A Orthomyxovirus with varying hemagglutinin (HA) and neuraminidase (N) antigens
Wild birds serve as natural reservoirs and transmit infection to domestic flocks
Transmission occurs through contact with infected wild birds, particularly waterfowl
Spread via contaminated feed, water, equipment, and vehicles (fomites)
Wind dispersal of virus-laden dust and feathers over considerable distances
Direct contact between infected carriers and susceptible flocks
Particularly common in regions with extensive live bird distribution and multi-age operations
Virus is relatively resistant to environmental exposure
Can infect birds in imperfectly decontaminated facilities
Movement of infected flocks, equipment, and personnel facilitates spread
Rapid multiplication in susceptible populations, especially commercial operations with substandard biosecurity
diagnosis: Rapid presumptive diagnosis using solid-state antigen capture assay (Directigen®)
RT-PCR assay for confirmation of AI virus
Virus isolation from tissues using specific pathogen-free embryos
Avian-cell tissue culture systems for virus isolation
Serotyping using serum-virus neutralization procedures
Agar-gel immunodiffusion test (AGID) to detect group-specific AI antibody
ELISA procedure for antibody detection indicating exposure or vaccination
Clinical signs: acute mortality, respiratory/nervous signs, drop in egg production
Necropsy findings: subcutaneous hemorrhages, edema of head, vesicles on comb/wattles
Hemorrhages in serosa of viscera and mucosa of intestinal/respiratory tracts
LPAI: tracheitis, pulmonary edema, airsacculitis if secondary bacterial infection present
treatment: No effective antiviral treatment available for AI virus
Supportive care may be provided for LPAI cases
HPAI typically requires culling and disposal of entire flock
Antibiotics may be used to control secondary bacterial infections
Isolation and quarantine of affected flocks
Prevention of spread through strict biosecurity measures
control: In exotic HPAI outbreaks: rapid diagnosis, slaughter and disposal of affected flocks
Quarantine and surveillance with disposal of antibody-positive flocks
Restriction on movement of flocks and products from infection foci
Strict biosecurity to limit virus dissemination among commercial farms
Vaccination using autogenous inactivated vaccine or recombinant vector products in endemic areas
HA-homologous inactivated vaccine to reduce susceptible bird population
Prevention of contact between domestic poultry and wild waterfowl
All-in-all-out management with thorough decontamination between flocks
Control of access by wild birds to feed storage and poultry areas
Proper disposal of dead birds and contaminated materials
Absolute separation of swine and poultry to prevent virus recombination
In endemic regions: long-term vaccination and intensified biosecurity commitment

diseases.name: Newcastle Disease

symptomsEnhanced.name: Egg shell(s), Misshapen (abnormal size or shape)

diseases.description: Newcastle Disease is a highly contagious and fatal viral disease affecting all species of birds, caused by virulent strains of avian paramyxovirus type 1. It presents in various pathotypes (lentogenic, mesogenic, velogenic) with velogenic strains causing severe systemic, respiratory, and neurological disease.
clinicalSigns: Torticollis (wry neck) and circling
Gasping, coughing, and respiratory distress
Greenish watery diarrhea
Leg and wing paralysis
Misshapen eggs and reduced production
Sudden death in velogenic strains
transmission: Inhalation of aerosolized virus from infected birds
Ingestion of feces-contaminated food or water
Mechanical transmission via fomites, equipment, and personnel
Wild birds serving as reservoirs
diagnosis: Virus isolation in embryonated chicken eggs
Reverse transcription-PCR (RT-PCR)
Hemagglutination inhibition (HI) test for antibody detection
Clinical signs and characteristic lesions (hemorrhages in proventriculus and cecal tonsils)
treatment: No specific treatment for the virus
Antibiotics may be used to control secondary bacterial infections
Flock depopulation is often required for velogenic outbreaks
control: Strict biosecurity to prevent introduction
Thorough cleaning and disinfection between flocks
Restricting visitor access and enforcing downtime
Quarantine and rapid reporting of suspected outbreaks
vaccineRecommendations: Live attenuated vaccines (e.g., LaSota, B1, Hitchner) via spray or drinking water for broilers and pullets
Inactivated oil-emulsion vaccines injected in layers and breeders before lay
Recombinant HVT-ND vaccines administered in ovo or subcutaneously at the hatchery

diseases.name: Infectious Bronchitis

symptomsEnhanced.name: Egg shell(s), Misshapen (abnormal size or shape)

diseases.description: Infectious Bronchitis (IB) is an acute, highly contagious viral respiratory disease of chickens caused by a coronavirus. It causes significant economic losses through respiratory disease, severe drops in egg production, and impaired egg quality, with some nephropathogenic strains causing kidney damage.
clinicalSigns: Coughing, sneezing, and tracheal rales
Nasal and ocular discharge
Severe drop in egg production
Thin-shelled, wrinkled, or misshapen eggs with watery whites
Kidney swelling and increased mortality in nephropathogenic strains
transmission: Airborne transmission via aerosolized respiratory droplets
Fecal-oral route through contaminated feces
Mechanical spread via contaminated equipment, vehicles, and personnel
diagnosis: Virus isolation in specific-pathogen-free embryonated eggs
RT-PCR detection from tracheal or cloacal swabs
Serology using ELISA or Hemagglutination Inhibition (HI)
Gross lesions showing serous or caseous exudate in trachea
treatment: No specific viral treatment available
Increasing brooder temperature to prevent chilling
Broad-spectrum antibiotics to reduce secondary bacterial infections (e.g., E. coli)
control: Strict biosecurity and all-in/all-out management
Thorough cleaning and disinfection of facilities
Limiting exposure to wild birds and maintaining proper ventilation
vaccineRecommendations: Live attenuated vaccines (e.g., Massachusetts, Connecticut, Arkansas serotypes) administered via spray or drinking water
Inactivated vaccines given to breeders and layers prior to the onset of production to ensure lasting immunity

diseases.name: Marek's Disease
diseases.description: Marek's Disease is a highly contagious viral neoplastic disease of chickens caused by a gallid alphaherpesvirus 2. It is characterized by the development of T-cell lymphomas in viscera, muscles, and skin, as well as demyelination of peripheral nerves leading to paralysis.
clinicalSigns: Asymmetric paralysis of one or both legs or wings
Drooping wings and classic 'splits' posture
Grey eye with irregular or pinpoint pupil causing blindness
Nodular skin lesions around feather follicles
Progressive weight loss, depression, and mortality
transmission: Inhalation of infectious virus shed in feather follicle dander
Virus is highly resilient and persists in contaminated dust and litter for months
Mechanical transmission via personnel, equipment, and wind
diagnosis: Clinical signs (unilateral paralysis) and gross necropsy findings (enlarged nerves and visceral tumors)
Histopathology of affected nerves showing lymphoid infiltration
PCR detection of Marek's disease virus DNA from tissues
Differentiation from Avian Leukosis Virus using molecular techniques
treatment: No treatment available; affected birds usually die or must be culled
control: Strict biosecurity and sanitation of the hatchery environment
Complete cleaning and disinfection of poultry houses between flocks
Use of genetically resistant breeds of chickens
vaccineRecommendations: Universal vaccination is essential; administered in ovo at 18 days of incubation or subcutaneously at day of hatch
Common vaccines include HVT (turkey herpesvirus), SB-1, and CVI988/Rispens strains depending on field challenge severity

diseases.name: Infectious Bursal Disease
diseases.description: Infectious Bursal Disease (IBD), also known as Gumboro disease, is an acute, highly contagious viral disease of young chickens caused by a birnavirus. It targets the bursa of Fabricius, leading to immunosuppression and increased susceptibility to secondary infections.
clinicalSigns: Watery diarrhea leading to pasty vents
Severe depression, lethargy, and huddling
Ruffled feathers and ruffled appearance
Tremors and unsteady gait
Sudden spike in mortality typically peaking at 3-5 days
transmission: Fecal-oral route through ingestion of contaminated feces
Highly resistant virus can survive in the environment for months
Mechanical transmission via personnel, equipment, and darkling beetles (Alphitobius diaperinus)
diagnosis: Gross lesions on the bursa of Fabricius (swollen, edematous, hemorrhagic, or later atrophied)
Histopathology showing lymphoid depletion in the bursa
RT-PCR for viral RNA detection
Serological profiling using ELISA to assess flock immunity or exposure
treatment: No specific treatment available
Supportive care including vitamin and electrolyte supplementation in drinking water
Controlling secondary infections due to immunosuppression
control: Stringent cleaning and disinfection using effective virucidal agents (e.g., formaldehyde, iodophors)
Extended downtime between flocks
Pest control program to eliminate darkling beetles
vaccineRecommendations: Live attenuated vaccines (mild, intermediate, or hot strains) administered via drinking water to chicks
Inactivated oil-emulsion vaccines administered to breeders to provide high levels of maternal antibodies to progeny
Recombinant HVT-IBD vector vaccines administered in ovo or at hatch

diseases.name: Fowl Cholera
diseases.description: Fowl Cholera is a contagious bacterial disease of domesticated and wild avians caused by Pasteurella multocida. It can manifest as an acute septicemic disease with sudden high mortality, or as a chronic localized infection causing swelling of the face, wattles, or joints.
clinicalSigns: Sudden death without prior warning signs in acute cases
Cyanosis (purple/dark red discoloration) of the comb and wattles
Swelling of the wattles and face
Greenish or yellowish diarrhea
Respiratory difficulty and mucoid discharge from the mouth
Lameness and swollen joints in chronic cases
transmission: Direct contact with infected or carrier birds
Ingestion of water or feed contaminated with feces or oral discharges
Rodents, cats, and wild birds can act as mechanical or biological vectors
Contaminated equipment and footwear
diagnosis: Bacterial culture and isolation of Pasteurella multocida from bone marrow, heart blood, or liver
PCR assays for rapid identification
Microscopic examination of blood or tissue smears showing bipolar-staining Gram-negative rods
Clinical signs and gross lesions (petechial hemorrhages on epicardium)
treatment: Antibiotics such as sulfonamides, penicillin, or tetracyclines administered in water or feed
Antimicrobial susceptibility testing is crucial as resistance is common
Treatment may halt mortality, but the disease can recur upon withdrawal
control: Strict biosecurity including robust rodent control programs
Exclusion of wild birds, pets, and other animals from poultry houses
Prompt removal and disposal of carcasses
Complete depopulation, cleaning, and disinfection of infected premises
vaccineRecommendations: Inactivated bacterins (often multivalent) administered to breeders and layers prior to lay
Live attenuated vaccines administered via wing-web puncture in endemic areas (use with caution)

diseases.name: Coccidiosis
diseases.description: Coccidiosis is a ubiquitous and economically devastating parasitic disease of poultry caused by protozoa of the genus Eimeria. It affects the intestinal tract, causing tissue damage, poor nutrient absorption, dehydration, blood loss, and susceptibility to secondary infections like Necrotic Enteritis.
clinicalSigns: Bloody droppings or blood-tinged diarrhea
Watery diarrhea and soiled vents
Lethargy, depression, and huddling behavior
Ruffled feathers and pale combs
Significant weight loss, stunted growth, and poor feed conversion
transmission: Fecal-oral route through ingestion of infectious sporulated oocysts from the litter
Mechanical transmission via contaminated footwear, equipment, insects, and rodents
Oocysts are highly resistant to environmental conditions and common disinfectants
diagnosis: Gross necropsy identifying characteristic lesions in specific regions of the intestinal tract (e.g., cecal cores for E. tenella)
Microscopic examination of intestinal mucosal scrapings to identify Eimeria species
Fecal flotation to detect and quantify oocysts
treatment: Administration of anticoccidial drugs in drinking water (e.g., amprolium, toltrazuril, sulfonamides)
Supplemental vitamins (A and K) to assist in tissue recovery and reduce hemorrhage
control: Prophylactic use of anticoccidial feed additives (ionophores or synthetic chemicals) using shuttle or rotation programs
Proper ventilation and drinker management to maintain dry litter
Stringent biosecurity and thorough cleaning between flocks
vaccineRecommendations: Live virulent or attenuated coccidiosis vaccines administered at the hatchery via coarse spray or edible gel to induce controlled early immunity, commonly used in broilers and breeders

diseases.name: Colibacillosis
diseases.description: Colibacillosis refers to any localized or systemic infection caused entirely or partly by avian pathogenic Escherichia coli (APEC). It is one of the most common bacterial diseases in poultry, often occurring as a secondary infection following viral respiratory challenges or environmental stress.
clinicalSigns: Depression, lethargy, and ruffled feathers
Respiratory distress and gasping
Watery diarrhea and soiled vents
Swollen abdomen (ascites or peritonitis)
High mortality and sudden death
Swollen legs or joints in some localized forms
transmission: Inhalation of E. coli-contaminated dust
Ingestion of contaminated water or feed
Fecal contamination of hatching eggs leading to omphalitis in chicks
Opportunistic infection following mucosal damage by mycoplasma or viruses
diagnosis: Isolation and identification of E. coli from typical lesions (e.g., heart blood, liver, air sacs)
Antimicrobial susceptibility testing of isolates
Gross pathology showing fibrinous perihepatitis, pericarditis, and airsacculitis
treatment: Antibiotic therapy based on antimicrobial susceptibility testing (AST) due to widespread resistance
Supportive care and optimization of environmental conditions (ventilation, temperature)
control: Control of primary respiratory diseases (IB, ND, Mycoplasma)
Improvement of air quality, reduction of dust and ammonia levels
Strict hatchery sanitation and proper egg handling
Water sanitation programs
vaccineRecommendations: Live mutated E. coli vaccines administered via spray to provide cross-protection
Autogenous inactivated bacterins formulated against specific pathogenic serotypes on problem farms

diseases.name: Infectious Coryza
diseases.description: Infectious Coryza is an acute respiratory disease of chickens caused by the bacterium Avibacterium paragallinarum. It is characterized by severe inflammation of the upper respiratory tract, leading to significant economic losses due to a rapid drop in egg production and poor growth.
clinicalSigns: Marked swelling of the face and infraorbital sinuses
Foul-smelling, purulent nasal and ocular discharge
Sneezing and respiratory distress
Adherence of eyelids due to sticky exudate
Significant reduction in feed consumption and egg production
transmission: Direct contact with clinically infected birds or asymptomatic carriers
Inhalation of infectious airborne droplets
Ingestion of contaminated drinking water
Fomites and contaminated equipment
diagnosis: Bacterial culture of sinus exudate requiring specific growth conditions (V-factor/NAD)
PCR detection for rapid and specific diagnosis
Clinical presentation of acute facial edema and malodorous nasal discharge
treatment: Administration of antibiotics in drinking water (e.g., erythromycin, oxytetracycline, sulfonamides)
Treatment alleviates clinical signs but does not eliminate carrier status in recovered birds
control: Implementation of strict all-in/all-out management practices
Depopulation of infected flocks followed by thorough cleaning and disinfection
Absolute separation of susceptible younger birds from older recovering flocks
vaccineRecommendations: Inactivated bacterins containing locally relevant serovars administered subcutaneously or intramuscularly to pullets 3-4 weeks prior to moving to laying houses

diseases.name: Fowl Pox
diseases.description: Fowl Pox is a slow-spreading viral disease of poultry caused by an avipoxvirus. It manifests in two forms: the cutaneous (dry) form characterized by nodular lesions on unfeathered skin, and the diphtheritic (wet) form characterized by fibrinous necrotic lesions in the respiratory and digestive mucosa.
clinicalSigns: Nodular crusty scabs on the comb, wattles, and face (dry pox)
Yellowish diphtheritic patches in the mouth, pharynx, and trachea (wet pox)
Swelling of the face and eyes with discharge
Difficulty breathing and swallowing
Weight loss and drop in egg production
transmission: Mechanical transmission by biting insects, primarily mosquitoes
Direct contact with infected birds through skin abrasions
Inhalation or ingestion of virus-laden dander or scabs (especially wet form)
diagnosis: Clinical signs and characteristic gross lesions (scabs or diphtheritic membranes)
Histopathology revealing intracytoplasmic inclusion bodies (Bollinger bodies)
Virus isolation on the chorioallantoic membrane (CAM) of embryonated eggs
PCR detection of avipoxvirus DNA
treatment: No specific antiviral treatment
Removal of environmental stressors
Application of topical antiseptics to skin lesions (optional in small flocks)
Broad-spectrum antibiotics to control secondary bacterial infections in severe cases
control: Mosquito and biting insect control programs
Elimination of standing water around poultry houses
Thorough cleaning and disinfection of the environment
vaccineRecommendations: Live fowl pox or pigeon pox vaccines administered via wing-web puncture
Typically administered to pullets at 10-14 weeks of age or during an outbreak to halt the spread

diseases.name: Infectious Laryngotracheitis
diseases.description: Infectious Laryngotracheitis (ILT) is an acute viral respiratory disease of chickens caused by a gallid alphaherpesvirus. It is characterized by severe respiratory distress, gasping, and expectoration of bloody exudate, leading to high mortality and production losses.
clinicalSigns: Gasping and severe dyspnea with extended neck
Coughing up blood or blood-stained mucus
Blood spots on breast feathers and walls
Nasal discharge and conjunctivitis
Limp neck and lethargy in severe cases
transmission: Direct contact with infected or carrier birds
Inhalation of airborne virus from respiratory secretions
Fomites including contaminated equipment, clothing, and footwear
Latent carriers shedding virus during periods of stress
diagnosis: Histopathology of trachea demonstrating syncytial cells and intranuclear inclusion bodies
PCR detection of viral DNA from tracheal swabs
Virus isolation on chorioallantoic membrane (CAM) of embryos
Gross lesions of hemorrhagic tracheitis
treatment: No specific treatment for the herpesvirus
Administration of broad-spectrum antibiotics to control secondary bacterial infections
Immediate vaccination in the face of an outbreak can help limit spread in unaffected houses
control: Strict biosecurity and access control
Rapid depopulation of infected flocks and deep burial or composting
Extended downtime and thorough disinfection
Avoiding mixing of vaccinated/recovered birds with naive flocks
vaccineRecommendations: Tissue Culture Origin (TCO) or Chick Embryo Origin (CEO) live vaccines applied via eye drop or drinking water
Recombinant vector vaccines (e.g., rHVT-LT or rFPV-LT) administered in ovo or at day of age to avoid vaccine reactions

diseases.name: Avian Encephalomyelitis

symptomsEnhanced.name: Gait, Reluctance to stand or walk

diseases.description: Avian Encephalomyelitis (AE), often referred to as epidemic tremor, is an infectious viral disease caused by a Tremovirus (Picornaviridae). It primarily targets the central nervous system of young poultry, resulting in distinctive neurological signs, and causes transient drops in egg production and hatchability in adult breeders.
clinicalSigns: Fine, rapid tremors of the head and neck
Progressive ataxia (incoordination) and reluctance to move
Sitting on hocks or partial to complete leg paralysis
Cloudy eyes leading to blindness in some recovered birds
Temporary drop in egg production and significant reduction in hatchability in breeders
transmission: Vertical transmission from infected breeder hens through eggs to progeny (main route for clinical outbreaks in chicks)
Horizontal transmission via the fecal-oral route in flocks
Direct contact with infected feces in hatcheries or growing facilities
diagnosis: Clinical presentation of epidemic tremors in young chicks
Histopathology of the brain, spinal cord, and proventriculus revealing nonpurulent encephalomyelitis
Serology using ELISA or agar-gel immunodiffusion (AGID) on unvaccinated flocks
Viral detection using RT-PCR on brain or pancreatic tissue
treatment: No specific antiviral treatment is available
Humane culling of severely affected chicks as recovery is poor and survivors may become blind or unthrifty
control: Securing chicks from AE-free or properly vaccinated breeder flocks
Strict biosecurity to prevent the introduction of the virus into susceptible flocks
Maintaining robust cleaning and disinfection protocols
vaccineRecommendations: Live attenuated vaccines administered to breeder and layer flocks between 10 to 15 weeks of age via drinking water or wing-web to ensure maternal antibody transfer and prevent production drops.

diseases.name: Avian Leukosis
diseases.description: Avian Leukosis encompasses a variety of neoplastic diseases caused by Avian Leukosis Viruses (ALV), primarily retroviruses. Lymphoid leukosis is the most common manifestation, resulting in the slow development of B-cell lymphomas in internal organs. The disease generally manifests in sexually mature birds, leading to significant chronic mortality and production losses.
clinicalSigns: Progressive weakness, emaciation, and severe weight loss
Enlarged abdomen due to massive tumor infiltration in the liver ('big liver disease')
Pale, shriveled comb indicating systemic decline
Decreased egg production and general unthriftiness
Palpable enlarged bursa of Fabricius through the vent
transmission: Vertical transmission (hen to egg) is the primary route for maintaining the virus across generations
Horizontal transmission via direct contact, heavily contaminated feces, and vaccines grown in contaminated eggs
Infection is most critical during the first few weeks of life
diagnosis: Gross necropsy showing grayish-white nodular or diffuse tumors in the liver, spleen, bursa of Fabricius, and kidneys
Histopathology to differentiate from Marek's disease (ALV tumors are strictly B-cell lymphomas and uniformly lack nerve involvement)
ELISA testing to detect p27 viral antigen in albumen or meconium
RT-PCR for specific ALV subgroup identification
treatment: No treatment or cure is available for ALV infections.
control: Eradication at the primary breeder level by testing and culling virus-shedding hens
Strict hatchery sanitation and rearing chicks in isolated, clean environments
Utilization of genetically resistant chicken lines developed by primary breeding companies
vaccineRecommendations: There are no commercial vaccines available for Avian Leukosis. Control relies entirely on eradication and genetic selection.

diseases.name: Chronic Respiratory Disease
diseases.description: Chronic Respiratory Disease (CRD) is a globally significant bacterial infection caused by Mycoplasma gallisepticum (MG). While MG alone causes mild respiratory signs, the disease frequently becomes severe when complicated by secondary pathogens like E. coli or viral infections (e.g., ND, IB), leading to complex airsacculitis and severe economic losses.
clinicalSigns: Tracheal rales, coughing, and frequent sneezing
Serous to purulent nasal and ocular discharge
Foamy conjunctivitis and swelling of the facial sinuses
Decreased feed consumption leading to poor weight gain and feed conversion
Noticeable drops in egg production in layer and breeder flocks
transmission: Vertical transmission from infected breeder hens to progeny through eggs
Horizontal transmission via direct contact with respiratory aerosols
Spread via contaminated fomites, personnel, and airborne dust over short distances
diagnosis: Serological monitoring using Rapid Plate Agglutination (RPA), HI, or ELISA
PCR detection directly from tracheal or choanal cleft swabs for rapid and specific identification
Bacterial culture using specialized Mycoplasma media (long incubation required)
Gross necropsy showing cloudy air sacs, often with caseous exudate if E. coli is involved
treatment: Administration of targeted antibiotics (e.g., tylosin, tiamulin, tetracyclines, tilmicosin) via drinking water or feed
Treatment can alleviate clinical signs and reduce transmission but does not clear the carrier state
control: Eradication and maintenance of MG-free primary and multiplier breeder flocks
Strict biosecurity to prevent lateral introduction
Optimizing environmental factors (proper ventilation to minimize dust and ammonia)
Single-age (all-in/all-out) farm management
vaccineRecommendations: Live attenuated vaccines (e.g., F strain, ts-11, 6/85) are used in commercial layers in endemic multi-age sites to displace wild-type strains and protect production
Inactivated bacterins may be used to reduce egg transmission in breeders

diseases.name: Pullorum Disease
diseases.description: Pullorum Disease is a highly fatal, acute systemic disease primarily affecting young chicks and poults, caused by the bacterium Salmonella enterica serovar Pullorum. Eradicated in many commercial sectors globally, it remains a severe threat in backyard or non-commercial flocks.
clinicalSigns: White, chalky, pasty diarrhea adhering to the vent area
Huddling near heat sources, drooping wings, and severe lethargy
Labored breathing and gasping
Sudden death with mortality peaking around 2 to 3 weeks of age
Survivors remain asymptomatic carriers with misshapen reproductive organs
transmission: Vertical transmission (transovarian) is the most critical route, infecting chicks prior to hatch
Horizontal transmission in hatcheries via inhalation of contaminated fluff and dust
Fecal-oral transmission through contaminated feed, water, and cannibalism
diagnosis: Isolation and identification of Salmonella Pullorum from affected organs (liver, spleen, unabsorbed yolk sac)
Serological testing using Rapid Whole Blood Agglutination (RWBA) on the farm to identify carriers
Necropsy lesions showing white nodules in the heart, liver, lungs, and cecal cores
treatment: Treatment is generally prohibited or discouraged in food-producing animals because it does not eliminate the carrier state, posing a continuous threat to the industry.
control: Strict adherence to national eradication programs (e.g., NPIP in the US)
Mandatory testing and slaughter of positive breeder flocks
Meticulous hatchery sanitation and fumigation
Biosecurity measures to prevent introduction from wild or backyard birds
vaccineRecommendations: Vaccines are strictly not recommended as they interfere with serological eradication testing protocols.

diseases.name: Fowl Typhoid
diseases.description: Fowl Typhoid is a severe, systemic bacterial disease primarily of mature growing and adult poultry, caused by Salmonella enterica serovar Gallinarum. It causes acute septicemia, high mortality, and significant production losses.
clinicalSigns: Profuse greenish-yellow or mucoid diarrhea
Pale, shrunken comb and wattles indicative of anemia
High fever, intense thirst, and severe lethargy
Sudden spike in mortality
Significant drop in feed consumption and egg production
transmission: Horizontal transmission is prominent through ingestion of feces-contaminated feed and water
Mechanical spread via personnel, fomites, and notably the poultry red mite (Dermanyssus gallinae)
Vertical transmission occurs but is less efficient compared to Pullorum disease
diagnosis: Bacterial culture and isolation of Salmonella Gallinarum from liver, spleen, or bone marrow
Gross pathology revealing a characteristic swollen, friable, bronze-colored liver and enlarged spleen
Serological assays, though cross-reactivity with S. Pullorum occurs
treatment: Antibiotics (e.g., amoxicillin, sulfonamides) can reduce mortality but do not clear the infection.
Regulatory frameworks in many jurisdictions mandate depopulation instead of treatment.
control: Testing and culling of infected flocks, integrated into national eradication frameworks
Rigorous biosecurity, including aggressive control of red mites and rodents
Thorough cleaning, disinfection, and extended downtime for infected facilities
vaccineRecommendations: In endemic regions where allowed, live attenuated vaccines (e.g., 9R strain) are administered to layers to mitigate clinical disease, though their use complicates serological eradication testing.

diseases.name: Necrotic Enteritis
diseases.description: Necrotic Enteritis is a critical enterotoxemic disease, primarily affecting fast-growing broilers, caused by the proliferation of Clostridium perfringens (Types A or C) and its alpha/NetB toxins. It typically occurs as a secondary condition following mucosal damage from coccidiosis or abrupt dietary shifts.
clinicalSigns: Sudden onset of high mortality without prior warning
Severe depression, immobility, and huddled posture
Ruffled feathers and anorexia
Dark, blood-tinged, or mucosal necrotic diarrhea
Subclinical form presents as wet litter, poor feed conversion, and reduced growth
transmission: Ingestion of robust C. perfringens spores from contaminated litter, feces, or soil
Disease manifestation requires predisposing factors (e.g., Eimeria damage, high non-starch polysaccharide diets, immunosuppression) to allow excessive bacterial proliferation.
diagnosis: Gross necropsy reveals distended, friable small intestines lined with a characteristic tan-to-yellow pseudomembrane ('Turkish towel' appearance)
Gram stain of mucosal scrapings showing massive numbers of large, Gram-positive rods
Bacterial culture to confirm C. perfringens and multiplex PCR for toxin typing (NetB)
treatment: Prompt administration of targeted antibiotics (e.g., bacitracin, penicillin, lincomycin, or tylosin) via drinking water
control: Effective prevention and control of coccidiosis (Eimeria maxima in particular)
Dietary management avoiding abrupt changes and using high-quality, digestible proteins
Inclusion of prebiotics, probiotics, organic acids, or exogenous enzymes in the feed to maintain gut health
Maintaining dry, well-managed litter
vaccineRecommendations: Commercial vaccines are limited and rarely used. Control is predominantly achieved through holistic gut health management and anticoccidial programs.

diseases.name: Paratyphoid
diseases.description: Paratyphoid infection (Salmonellosis) refers to enteric and systemic diseases caused by motile Salmonella serotypes, most notably S. Enteritidis and S. Typhimurium. While adult poultry are often asymptomatic carriers, the disease causes severe mortality in young chicks and represents a critical food safety and public health risk due to its zoonotic nature.
clinicalSigns: In chicks: severe watery diarrhea, pasty vent, huddling near heat sources
Lethargy, closed eyes, and drooping wings
Unabsorbed yolk sac and omphalitis in young birds
In adults: generally asymptomatic, though occasionally causes mild diarrhea or a drop in egg production
transmission: Vertical transmission occurs either through true ovarian infection or surface contamination of the eggshell
Horizontal transmission via the fecal-oral route, contaminated feed, and water
Vectors such as rodents, darkling beetles, and wild birds play a major role in persistence
diagnosis: Bacterial culture and isolation from tissues (liver, spleen, ceca), environmental drag swabs, or boot swabs
Serotyping and molecular typing to identify specific zoonotic serovars
Serological monitoring using ELISA to assess flock status
treatment: Antibiotics may decrease clinical mortality in chicks but are strictly discouraged as they promote antimicrobial resistance and fail to clear the intestinal carrier state, leading to silent shedding.
control: Comprehensive biosecurity focused on robust rodent and pest control
Heat treatment or pelleting of feed to eliminate pathogens
Use of competitive exclusion products (probiotics) to colonize the neonatal gut
Rigorous cleaning and chemical disinfection of facilities between flocks
vaccineRecommendations: Inactivated bacterins and live attenuated Salmonella vaccines are heavily utilized in breeders and commercial layers to stimulate immunity, reduce intestinal colonization, and prevent egg contamination.

diseases.name: Infectious Synovitis

symptomsEnhanced.name: Gait, Reluctance to stand or walk

diseases.description: Infectious Synovitis is an acute to chronic disease of chickens and turkeys caused by Mycoplasma synoviae (MS). It primarily targets the synovial membranes of joints and tendon sheaths causing lameness, but can also cause subclinical respiratory disease and eggshell apex abnormalities in layers.
clinicalSigns: Lameness, reluctance to walk, and stiff gait
Swelling of the hock joints and footpads containing viscous, creamy to caseous exudate
Development of breast blisters due to prolonged sternal resting
Pale comb, stunted growth, and general unthriftiness
In layers, MS can cause altered eggshell quality (eggshell apex abnormality)
transmission: Vertical transmission from infected breeders to chicks
Horizontal transmission via respiratory aerosols and direct contact within the flock
Fomite transmission plays a minor but significant role
diagnosis: Serology using RPA or ELISA for flock screening
PCR detection from choanal cleft swabs or joint exudate for rapid confirmation
Bacterial culture utilizing specialized Mycoplasma media
Gross pathology showing creamy exudate in joint spaces and tendon sheaths
treatment: Antibiotics such as tetracyclines, tiamulin, or tylosin via water or feed to alleviate clinical signs
Treatment does not completely clear the infection, and relapses may occur upon withdrawal
control: Procurement of chicks from officially MS-free primary breeder flocks
Rigorous biosecurity to prevent lateral introduction
Single-age farm management to break the cycle of infection
vaccineRecommendations: Live, temperature-sensitive vaccines (e.g., MS-H strain) are utilized in commercial layers and breeders in endemic areas to prevent colonization by wild-type strains and protect production.

diseases.name: Aspergillosis
diseases.description: Aspergillosis, often called 'brooder pneumonia', is a mycotic infection affecting the respiratory system, most commonly caused by Aspergillus fumigatus. It generally presents as an acute disease causing significant mortality in young chicks, or as a chronic affliction in older birds under severe environmental stress.
clinicalSigns: Silent gasping and accelerated breathing (dyspnea) without typical respiratory rales
Lethargy, anorexia, and progressive emaciation
Cloudy eyes and caseous exudate beneath the eyelids
Neurological signs (torticollis, ataxia) if fungal elements disseminate to the brain
Elevated mortality in the first 1-2 weeks of life
transmission: Inhalation of overwhelming numbers of fungal spores from contaminated incubators or hatchers
Exposure to moldy, wet litter or heavily contaminated feed
It is an environmental pathogen and is not contagious directly from bird to bird
diagnosis: Gross necropsy revealing characteristic yellowish-white caseous nodules or green-gray fungal plaques in the lungs, air sacs, and trachea
Microscopic examination (wet mounts) of lesions showing branching fungal hyphae
Fungal culture on Sabouraud dextrose agar
treatment: Antifungal treatment is not practical, economical, or approved for commercial poultry.
Culling of severely affected birds to prevent suffering is standard practice.
control: Strict hatchery sanitation and rigorous incubator fumigation protocols
Use of clean, dry, mold-free litter and proper storage of feed
Maintaining excellent house ventilation to reduce environmental spore loads and moisture
vaccineRecommendations: No commercial vaccines are available for Aspergillosis.

diseases.name: Blackhead Disease
diseases.description: Blackhead Disease, medically termed Histomoniasis, is a severe protozoal disease caused by Histomonas meleagridis. While it primarily devastates turkey flocks, it also affects chickens, which often act as subclinical carriers. The disease is characterized by profound necrotic damage to the ceca and the liver.
clinicalSigns: Distinctive sulfur-yellow or bright yellow diarrhea
Severe lethargy, huddling, and drooping wings
Significant weight loss and stunting
Cyanosis (darkening or purplish discoloration) of the head parts, giving the disease its common name
High mortality, especially in turkeys and occasionally in young pullets
transmission: Ingestion of embryonated eggs of the cecal worm (Heterakis gallinarum) which harbor the Histomonas protozoa
Ingestion of earthworms that act as transport hosts for the cecal worm
Direct lateral transmission ('cloacal drinking') can occur rapidly within turkey flocks
diagnosis: Gross necropsy showing pathognomonic lesions: target-like, depressed necrotic areas on the liver and inflamed ceca filled with hard, caseous cores
Microscopic identification of the protozoan in histologic sections of the liver or ceca
PCR detection for definitive confirmation
treatment: Historically treated with nitroimidazoles, but these are now largely banned for use in food-producing animals in many countries. Currently, no approved, effective therapeutic drugs are widely available.
control: Strict spatial separation of chickens (reservoir hosts) from turkeys
Routine deworming programs to eliminate Heterakis gallinarum populations
Rearing birds on wire, slatted floors, or concrete to prevent access to soil, earthworms, and nematode eggs
Frequent litter turnover and aggressive pest control
vaccineRecommendations: No vaccines are currently available for Histomoniasis.

diseases.name: Reticuloendotheliosis
diseases.description: Reticuloendotheliosis is a viral disease caused by a retrovirus (REV), leading to significant immunosuppression, runting syndrome, and lymphoid neoplasia in various avian species. It often presents subclinically but causes severe economic impact through poor performance and vaccine failures.
clinicalSigns: Stunted or severely delayed growth (runting syndrome)
Abnormal feather development (nakanuke or 'moth-eaten' feathers)
Pale combs and general lethargy
Progressive weight loss and unthriftiness
Occasional development of visceral tumors similar to Marek's disease
transmission: Horizontal transmission via direct contact, feces, or mechanically by biting insects
Vertical transmission from infected breeder hens to progeny through the egg
Iatrogenic transmission via contaminated live virus vaccines
diagnosis: Virus isolation from whole blood or tumors
PCR detection of proviral DNA in affected tissues
Histopathological differentiation of tumors (REV tumors can involve both B and T cells)
Serology (ELISA) to establish flock exposure status
treatment: No treatment or cure is available for the virus.
Supportive care is generally ineffective for clinically affected birds.
control: Eradication of the virus in primary breeder flocks
Strict vector control programs (mosquitoes and other biting insects)
Rigorous quality control to ensure all poultry vaccines are free from REV contamination
vaccineRecommendations: There are no commercial vaccines available against Reticuloendotheliosis virus.

diseases.name: Avian Tuberculosis
diseases.description: Avian Tuberculosis is a chronic, slowly spreading bacterial infection caused by the Mycobacterium avium complex. It is characterized by the formation of granulomatous lesions in visceral organs and leads to progressive wasting. While largely eradicated in modern commercial operations, it persists in backyard and free-range flocks.
clinicalSigns: Progressive and severe weight loss (emaciation or 'going light') despite maintained appetite initially
Marked weakness and lethargy
Pale, shriveled combs and wattles
Persistent watery diarrhea
Occasional lameness due to bone marrow or joint involvement
transmission: Fecal-oral route through ingestion of contaminated soil, water, or feed
The mycobacteria are highly resilient and can survive in soil for years
Mechanical transmission via equipment and personnel boots
diagnosis: Gross necropsy revealing irregular, grayish-white to yellow caseous nodules (granulomas) primarily in the liver, spleen, and intestines
Acid-fast staining (Ziehl-Neelsen) of crushed tissue smears showing red bacillary organisms
Bacterial culture (requires prolonged incubation) and PCR for definitive confirmation
treatment: Treatment is strictly not recommended due to the zoonotic potential of M. avium, the chronic nature of the disease, and the high risk of environmental shedding.
control: Immediate depopulation of clinically affected flocks
Thorough cleaning and prolonged resting of contaminated environments (exposure to sunlight and drying)
Preventing flock access to historically contaminated soil or areas frequented by wild birds
vaccineRecommendations: No commercial vaccines are available or recommended for Avian Tuberculosis.

diseases.name: Listeriosis
diseases.description: Listeriosis is an infectious bacterial disease caused by Listeria monocytogenes. In poultry, it typically presents as a sporadic, acute septicemia in young birds or as a localized encephalomyelitis causing pronounced neurological signs. It is of high public health importance due to its zoonotic risk through contaminated food.
clinicalSigns: Neurological signs including torticollis (wry neck), head tremors, and circling
Severe lethargy, depression, and ataxia
Sudden death in the acute septicemic form
Partial or complete paralysis of legs or wings
transmission: Ingestion of the ubiquitous bacteria from contaminated soil, poor-quality silage, or decaying organic matter
Fecal-oral transmission through contaminated feed or drinking water
Rodents act as important mechanical vectors and biological reservoirs
diagnosis: Bacterial culture and isolation of L. monocytogenes from the brain, liver, or spleen
Histopathology revealing microabscesses or necrosis in the brainstem and myocardium
PCR assays for rapid confirmation
treatment: Administration of broad-spectrum antibiotics (e.g., high doses of penicillins or tetracyclines) via drinking water
Effectiveness depends heavily on early intervention before severe neurological damage occurs
control: Meticulous sanitation and elimination of decaying vegetation or spoiled feed around the farm
Aggressive rodent control programs
Strict biosecurity to prevent introduction
vaccineRecommendations: There are no commercial vaccines utilized for Listeriosis in poultry.

diseases.name: Erysipelas
diseases.description: Erysipelas is an acute, systemic bacterial infection caused by Erysipelothrix rhusiopathiae. While traditionally a major threat to turkeys and swine, it increasingly causes severe outbreaks in laying hens, particularly in alternative or free-range housing systems, leading to high mortality.
clinicalSigns: Sudden death of birds that appeared healthy hours prior
Cyanosis (dark purple discoloration) of the comb, wattles, and facial skin
Severe lethargy, depression, and ruffled feathers
General reddening or blotchy skin lesions
Significant drop in egg production in layer flocks
transmission: Entry of bacteria through skin abrasions, wounds, or cannibalism
Ingestion of contaminated soil, water, or infected carcasses
Mechanical transmission by biting insects, particularly the poultry red mite (Dermanyssus gallinae)
diagnosis: Gram stain of blood or tissue impression smears revealing characteristic Gram-positive, slender, pleomorphic rods
Bacterial culture and isolation from liver, spleen, or heart blood
Gross pathology showing enlarged, friable liver and spleen, and petechial hemorrhages in muscles
treatment: Immediate administration of penicillins (highly efficacious) via drinking water or intramuscular injection
Erythromycin or tetracyclines may be used based on susceptibility testing
control: Aggressive management and eradication of poultry red mites
Prompt removal and safe disposal of dead birds to prevent cannibalism
Thorough cleaning, disinfection, and spelling of contaminated ranges
vaccineRecommendations: Inactivated bacterins are effectively used in high-risk layer or breeder flocks and turkeys. They are typically administered via injection prior to the onset of lay or exposure to contaminated ranges.

diseases.name: Bumblefoot

symptomsEnhanced.name: Gait, Reluctance to stand or walk

diseases.description: Bumblefoot, medically known as plantar pododermatitis, is a localized bacterial infection and inflammatory reaction primarily involving the footpad. It is most frequently caused by opportunistic Staphylococcus aureus infections entering through compromised skin, strongly correlated with suboptimal housing conditions.
clinicalSigns: Pronounced swelling, heat, and redness of the footpad
Presence of a hard, black, necrotic core or scab on the plantar surface
Obvious lameness and altered gait to avoid bearing weight
Reluctance to walk or access feed and water, leading to weakness
Swelling may extend to the joints and tendons in severe, advanced cases
transmission: Not contagious bird-to-bird
Occurs when environmental bacteria (S. aureus, E. coli) invade the foot through puncture wounds, cuts, or abrasions caused by poor litter conditions or inappropriate roosts.
diagnosis: Straightforward clinical inspection of the plantar foot surface revealing the characteristic necrotic lesion and swelling
Bacterial culture of the purulent or caseous exudate beneath the scab for definitive pathogen identification
treatment: Surgical debridement to carefully remove the necrotic core and caseous material, followed by flushing with antiseptic (e.g., chlorhexidine)
Application of topical antibacterial dressings and bandaging the foot
Systemic antibiotics (e.g., penicillins, erythromycin) in cases with severe joint involvement
control: Optimization of litter management to maintain dry, friable conditions
Ensuring roosts and perches are smooth, appropriately sized, and lack sharp edges
Managing nutrition to prevent obesity and ensure proper biotin and zinc levels
vaccineRecommendations: No specific vaccines are used to prevent Bumblefoot.

diseases.name: Staphylococcosis Infection
diseases.description: Staphylococcosis encompasses a range of systemic or localized diseases caused predominantly by Staphylococcus aureus. In poultry, it most frequently manifests as bacterial arthritis, tenosynovitis, osteomyelitis, or acute septicemia, heavily impacting welfare and causing significant condemnations at processing.
clinicalSigns: Pronounced swelling, heat, and pain in the hock joints and footpads
Severe lameness and reluctance to stand
Drooping wings and general lethargy
Sudden mortality in the acute septicemic form without localized swelling
Necrotic skin lesions or gangrenous dermatitis when skin is compromised
transmission: S. aureus is ubiquitous in the environment and normal skin flora
Infection occurs via breaches in mucosal barriers, skin abrasions, or respiratory exposure
Often acts secondary to immunosuppressive viruses (IBD, CAV) or environmental stress
diagnosis: Bacterial culture and isolation of S. aureus from affected joints, bone marrow, or heart blood
Gross pathology revealing purulent or caseous exudate in joint spaces and tendon sheaths
Differentiation from Mycoplasma synoviae and Reovirus infections
treatment: Antibiotic therapy (e.g., penicillins, erythromycin, tetracyclines) administered in water or feed
Treatment efficacy is often poor in chronic cases because antibiotics struggle to penetrate established caseous joint exudate
Antimicrobial susceptibility testing (AST) is strongly recommended
control: Meticulous hatchery sanitation to prevent neonatal omphalitis
Removal of sharp objects and maintaining dry litter to prevent skin breaches
Effective control of primary immunosuppressive diseases
vaccineRecommendations: Commercial vaccines are generally not utilized, though autogenous bacterins may occasionally be used in high-value breeder flocks facing endemic challenges.

diseases.name: Ornithobacteriosis
diseases.description: Ornithobacteriosis is an acute, contagious respiratory disease caused by the bacterium Ornithobacterium rhinotracheale (ORT). It primarily causes severe exudative pneumonia, pleuritis, and airsacculitis, leading to increased mortality, reduced growth, and high condemnation rates, often exacerbated by viral co-infections.
clinicalSigns: Sneezing, coughing, and significant respiratory distress (dyspnea)
Nasal discharge and facial edema
Foamy exudate in the eyes
Lethargy, reduced feed intake, and poor weight gain
Sudden increases in flock mortality
transmission: Horizontal spread via inhalation of infectious respiratory aerosols and direct contact
Fomites and contaminated equipment
Vertical transmission from breeders to progeny is suspected and actively researched
diagnosis: Gross pathology revealing severe, yogurt-like fibrinous exudate in air sacs and pneumonia
Bacterial culture on blood agar supplemented with gentamicin under microaerophilic conditions
PCR detection directly from tracheal swabs or tissues for rapid confirmation
ELISA testing to assess flock exposure
treatment: Antibiotics (e.g., amoxicillin, chlortetracycline, macrolides) administered in water
Efficacy is highly variable as ORT rapidly develops resistance to multiple antimicrobial classes; AST is essential.
control: Rigorous biosecurity and all-in/all-out production cycles
Optimization of environmental ventilation to control dust and ammonia
Stringent control of primary viral respiratory pathogens (ND, IB, aMPV)
vaccineRecommendations: Inactivated multivalent bacterins are frequently administered to breeder flocks via injection to provide maternal immunity to progeny and protect against production drops.
Live attenuated vaccines may be utilized via spray in commercial broilers in highly endemic areas.

diseases.name: Swollen Head Syndrome
diseases.description: Swollen Head Syndrome (SHS) is an acute, complex respiratory disease primarily triggered by Avian Metapneumovirus (aMPV) and frequently complicated by secondary bacterial invaders, most notably E. coli. It leads to severe cellulitis of the facial and periorbital tissues, predominantly affecting broilers and broiler breeders.
clinicalSigns: Marked swelling (edema) of the periorbital tissues, head, and face
Adherence of eyelids leading to partial or complete eye closure
Sneezing, coughing, and nasal discharge
Torticollis or head tilt due to inner ear involvement or cerebral extension
Severe lethargy, reluctance to move, and depression
transmission: Highly contagious horizontal spread via respiratory aerosols and direct contact
Mechanical transmission via contaminated personnel, equipment, and drinking water
diagnosis: Clinical presentation of facial cellulitis and edema
RT-PCR detection of Avian Metapneumovirus from choanal or tracheal swabs early in the disease
Bacterial culture of subcutaneous facial exudate (usually yielding E. coli)
ELISA to detect seroconversion against aMPV in convalescent flocks
treatment: No specific treatment exists for the viral component (aMPV).
Broad-spectrum antibiotics administered in drinking water to mitigate the secondary E. coli infection and reduce mortality
control: Strict biosecurity and improvement of house ventilation to minimize ammonia and dust
Maintaining high-quality litter management
Controlling concurrent respiratory challenges (e.g., IBV, Mycoplasma)
vaccineRecommendations: Live attenuated aMPV vaccines administered via spray or drinking water to broilers and pullets
Inactivated oil-emulsion vaccines given via injection to breeders prior to lay to ensure long-lasting immunity

diseases.name: Avian Cellulitis
diseases.description: Avian Cellulitis, often called 'inflammatory process' (IP), is a localized subcutaneous bacterial infection, overwhelmingly caused by Avian Pathogenic E. coli (APEC). It is primarily a subclinical disease in broilers that is only discovered at the processing plant, leading to massive economic losses due to carcass condemnations or downgrading.
clinicalSigns: Often subclinical in the field with no obvious behavioral changes
Thickened, red, or brownish discoloration of the skin, typically over the abdomen or thighs
Scab formations or scratches visible on the skin surface
In severe cases, generalized lethargy and abdominal enlargement
Slightly elevated flock mortality in the late grow-out phase
transmission: Bacteria enter the subcutaneous space exclusively through breaks in the skin (scratches, cuts)
Environmental E. coli from the litter contaminates the wounds
High incidence is directly correlated with aggressive behavior, panic, overcrowding, and poor feathering
diagnosis: Visual inspection at processing reveals characteristic yellow, caseous, fibrinous plaques in the subcutaneous tissues
Bacterial culture of the subcutaneous exudate confirming E. coli
On-farm monitoring of scratch incidence
treatment: Treatment is rarely initiated because the disease is usually subclinical and discovered too close to processing (withdrawal periods prohibit antibiotic use).
If detected early in a flock with a high incidence of scratches, targeted antibiotics may be considered.
control: Optimizing stocking density and improving feathering through nutrition
Minimizing panic and scratching by controlling lighting programs, avoiding sudden noises, and ensuring adequate feeder/drinker space
Maintaining excellent litter quality to reduce the bacterial load
vaccineRecommendations: Specific vaccines for cellulitis are not routinely used, though general E. coli vaccines (live or inactivated) may offer some cross-protection against APEC strains.

diseases.name: Gangrenous Dermatitis
diseases.description: Gangrenous Dermatitis (GD) is a devastating, acute disease of fast-growing poultry characterized by severe necrosis of the skin and underlying tissues. It is caused by infections with Clostridium septicum, Clostridium perfringens type A, and/or Staphylococcus aureus, highly exacerbated by profound immunosuppression.
clinicalSigns: Sudden spike in mortality with birds found dead in good flesh
Skin over the breast, abdomen, or wings becomes dark red, purplish, or greenish-black
Affected skin is often wet, weeping, devoid of feathers, and crepitant (spongy due to gas pockets)
Severe lethargy, ataxia, and depression in surviving birds
Rapid putrefaction of carcasses post-mortem
transmission: Ubiquitous bacteria from the litter and environment enter through compromised skin (scratches) or a damaged intestinal mucosa
Does not transmit directly from bird to bird, but environmental contamination from dead birds exacerbates the outbreak.
diagnosis: Characteristic gross lesions (necrotic, gas-filled subcutaneous tissues with serosanguineous fluid)
Gram stain of tissue impression smears showing abundant large Gram-positive rods (Clostridium) and/or cocci (Staphylococcus)
Anaerobic bacterial culture to confirm Clostridium species
treatment: Immediate administration of targeted broad-spectrum antibiotics (e.g., penicillins, amoxicillin, tetracyclines) via drinking water
Prompt removal of mortality to reduce environmental spore load
control: Rigorous prevention of immunosuppressive viral diseases, critically Infectious Bursal Disease (IBD) and Chicken Infectious Anemia (CAV)
Optimization of litter management to reduce moisture and bacterial buildup
Minimizing trauma and scratches by optimizing stocking density and lighting
In-feed interventions (probiotics, organic acids) to maintain gut integrity
vaccineRecommendations: No specific commercial vaccines exist for Gangrenous Dermatitis. Control relies heavily on robust vaccination programs against IBD and CAV in breeder flocks.

diseases.name: Osteomyelitis

symptomsEnhanced.name: Gait, Reluctance to stand or walk

diseases.description: Osteomyelitis is a localized or systemic bacterial infection of the bone marrow and bone tissue, most commonly caused by Staphylococcus aureus, Escherichia coli, or Enterococcus species. It leads to severe pain, bone deformities, and chronic lameness, heavily impacting animal welfare and production.
clinicalSigns: Severe, progressive lameness and altered gait
Reluctance to stand or walk, leading to poor access to feed and water
Noticeable localized swelling and heat on the legs (especially the proximal tibiotarsus or proximal femur)
Bone deformities in chronic cases
General weakness and poor flock uniformity
transmission: Hematogenous spread (via the bloodstream) from primary entry points such as respiratory mucosa, compromised intestinal lining, or skin abrasions
Direct inoculation through penetrating wounds or severe bumblefoot lesions extending into the bone
diagnosis: Gross pathology revealing focal areas of caseous necrosis, purulent exudate, or hemorrhage in the bone marrow (most easily seen by longitudinally splitting the proximal tibiotarsus or femur)
Bacterial culture from the affected bone marrow for definitive identification
Histopathology showing necrosis and inflammatory cell infiltration of the bone tissue
treatment: Treatment is often unrewarding due to the inability of antibiotics to effectively penetrate avascular necrotic bone tissue.
In early stages or valuable breeder birds, prolonged administration of targeted broad-spectrum antibiotics (e.g., based on AST) may be attempted.
Humane culling is generally recommended for severely lame birds.
control: Maintenance of optimal litter quality to prevent skin lesions or footpad dermatitis (bumblefoot)
Minimizing early stressors and controlling primary immunosuppressive diseases
Careful handling to prevent micro-fractures in growing bones which can become focal points for infection
vaccineRecommendations: No specific vaccines are available for Osteomyelitis. Control focuses entirely on management and preventing primary portals of entry.

diseases.name: Spondylitis (Vertebral Osteomyelitis)

symptomsEnhanced.name: Gait, Reluctance to stand or walk

diseases.description: Spondylitis, often referred to as 'Kinky Back' in its clinical manifestation, is a bacterial osteomyelitis specifically affecting the vertebrae. In broilers, it is predominantly caused by Enterococcus cecorum, which colonizes the free thoracic vertebra (T6), causing abscessation that compresses the spinal cord and leads to paralysis.
clinicalSigns: Classic posture: birds sitting on their hocks with feet raised off the ground and tails pointed down
Progressive hind limb paresis leading to complete, irreversible paralysis
Reluctance to walk and marked weakness
Secondary dehydration and starvation due to inability to reach feeders and drinkers
transmission: Early oral ingestion of pathogenic E. cecorum from the environment
Bacteria translocate across the gut mucosa into the bloodstream (bacteremia)
Bacteria selectively colonize the osteochondrotic clefts of rapidly growing vertebrae (usually T6) due to mechanical stress
diagnosis: Clinical presentation of symmetrical hind-limb paralysis in fast-growing birds
Sagittal sectioning of the spinal column during necropsy reveals distinct caseous abscesses or necrotic tracts inside the T6 vertebra, bulging into the spinal canal
Bacterial culture of the vertebral lesion confirming E. cecorum or, less commonly, S. aureus
treatment: Therapeutic treatment with antibiotics is ineffective once neurological signs (paralysis) manifest, as spinal cord damage is irreversible.
Immediate euthanasia of paralyzed birds is necessary for welfare.
control: Rigorous cleaning and disinfection to lower environmental bacterial load for incoming day-old chicks
Slowing the early growth rate slightly (e.g., through lighting or feed restriction programs) to reduce mechanical stress on the thoracic vertebrae
Supporting gut health to prevent bacterial translocation
vaccineRecommendations: Autogenous E. cecorum vaccines are sometimes used in broiler breeder flocks to provide maternal immunity to progeny, though efficacy varies.

diseases.name: Avian Chlamydiosis
diseases.description: Avian Chlamydiosis is an infectious systemic disease caused by the obligate intracellular bacterium Chlamydia psittaci. It causes significant respiratory and systemic signs. It is highly zoonotic, causing 'psittacosis' (parrot fever) in humans, representing a serious occupational hazard for farm workers and processing plant staff.
clinicalSigns: Serous to purulent ocular and nasal discharge
Respiratory distress, gasping, and sneezing
Yellowish-green to bright green watery diarrhea
Severe lethargy, anorexia, and progressive weight loss
Decreased egg production in layers
transmission: Inhalation of infectious aerosols generated from dried feces or respiratory secretions
Direct contact with infected birds or contaminated fomites
The bacterium produces environmentally resilient elementary bodies that survive for months in dust and organic matter
diagnosis: PCR detection of C. psittaci from conjunctival, choanal, or cloacal swabs
Gross necropsy showing severe fibrinous pericarditis, perihepatitis, and airsacculitis (resembling systemic E. coli)
Specialized tissue staining (e.g., Gimenez or Macchiavello stains) to demonstrate intracellular inclusion bodies
Serological monitoring using ELISA
treatment: Tetracyclines (chlortetracycline or doxycycline) are the drugs of choice, administered continuously in feed or water for extended periods (often 30-45 days) to clear the intracellular pathogen.
Treatment must be carefully managed to ensure therapeutic blood levels and mitigate zoonotic risk.
control: Strict exclusion of wild birds (especially pigeons, doves, and waterfowl) from poultry houses and feed storage
Rigorous biosecurity, mandatory quarantine for new flock additions, and thorough wet-cleaning to prevent dust aerosolization
Use of appropriate personal protective equipment (PPE) by workers
vaccineRecommendations: There are no commercial vaccines available for Avian Chlamydiosis.

diseases.name: Roundworm Infection
diseases.description: Roundworm infection in poultry is primarily caused by Ascaridia galli, the largest nematode affecting birds. It inhabits the small intestine, absorbing nutrients and causing localized tissue damage. High worm burdens lead to unthriftiness, severe drops in feed efficiency, and can cause fatal intestinal impaction.
clinicalSigns: Presence of large, yellowish-white macroscopic worms (up to 11 cm) in fresh droppings
Progressive weight loss and stunted growth despite normal feed intake
Watery diarrhea, sometimes containing undigested food
Pale comb and wattles indicating anemia and general weakness
Reduced egg production in mature laying hens
transmission: Direct life cycle: ingestion of embryonated eggs from contaminated litter, soil, or feed
Earthworms can act as mechanical transport hosts, concentrating infective eggs
Eggs are extremely resistant to environmental conditions and common disinfectants, surviving in soil for over a year
diagnosis: Gross necropsy revealing large adult nematodes within the lumen of the small intestine
Fecal flotation demonstrating characteristic smooth, thick-shelled oval eggs
Visual confirmation of adult worms passed in feces
treatment: Administration of approved anthelmintics (e.g., fenbendazole, levamisole, piperazine, flubendazole) via drinking water or feed
Treatment should be carefully dosed to avoid rapid massive die-offs of worms, which can cause acute intestinal blockage.
control: Routine, strategic deworming programs based on fecal monitoring
Thorough mechanical clean-out of litter between flocks and sanitation with specific ovicidal disinfectants or heat treatment
Rearing birds on slatted floors or wire to separate them from feces
Pasture rotation for free-range flocks
vaccineRecommendations: No vaccines are available against Ascaridia galli.

diseases.name: Tapeworm Infection
diseases.description: Tapeworm infections (Cestodiasis) in poultry are caused by various flatworms, predominantly Raillietina and Choanotaenia species. They attach to the intestinal mucosa using a scolex, leading to localized enteritis and competition for nutrients. Infections are highly prevalent in backyard and free-range systems where intermediate hosts are abundant.
clinicalSigns: Presence of white, motile, rice-like segments (proglottids) in fresh droppings
Progressive weight loss and unthriftiness despite maintaining a good appetite
Watery diarrhea
Pale comb and general weakness due to nutrient deprivation
Suboptimal egg production
transmission: Strictly indirect life cycle requiring an intermediate host (e.g., darkling beetles, houseflies, ants, slugs, or earthworms)
Poultry become infected exclusively by hunting and ingesting the intermediate host that carries the encysted tapeworm larva (cysticercoid)
diagnosis: Visual identification of shed proglottids on the surface of fresh feces
Gross necropsy revealing adult tapeworms attached to the small intestinal wall
Fecal flotation is unreliable for tapeworms because eggs are contained within shed segments rather than dispersed evenly in the feces
treatment: Administration of specific anticestodal drugs such as praziquantel, niclosamide, or fenbendazole
Note: Treatment must strict adhere to local regulations regarding use and withdrawal periods in food-producing birds.
control: Vigorous insect and pest control programs inside and outside the poultry house to eliminate intermediate hosts
Proper storage and frequent removal of manure to disrupt insect breeding cycles
Restricting flock access to areas with heavy insect or snail populations
vaccineRecommendations: No vaccines exist for tapeworm infections.

diseases.name: Capillariasis
diseases.description: Capillariasis is a severe parasitic disease caused by hairworms (Capillaria spp.), which are microscopic thread-like nematodes. Depending on the species, they embed deeply into the mucosa of the crop, esophagus, or lower intestinal tract, causing severe inflammation, hemorrhage, and fatal emaciation.
clinicalSigns: Severe emaciation and rapid weight loss ('going light')
Profuse watery or bloody diarrhea (if intestinal species like C. obsignata)
Very pale comb and wattles indicating significant anemia
Ruffled feathers, weakness, and huddling
Crop stasis, regurgitation, or difficulty swallowing (if crop/esophageal species like C. contorta)
transmission: Direct life cycle: Ingestion of embryonated eggs from the litter or soil (e.g., C. obsignata)
Indirect life cycle: Ingestion of earthworms serving as essential intermediate hosts (e.g., C. annulata)
Eggs are extremely hardy and accumulate in contaminated environments over time
diagnosis: Fecal flotation identifying characteristic barrel-shaped eggs with prominent bipolar plugs
Gross necropsy requiring careful scraping of the affected mucosa (crop or intestine) followed by microscopic examination to visualize the slender, hair-like adult worms
treatment: Administration of broad-spectrum anthelmintics such as fenbendazole, levamisole, or flubendazole
Treatment may need to be repeated due to the deep embedding of worms in the mucosa
control: Routine fecal monitoring and prophylactic deworming schedules
Excellent litter management and dry housing environments
For outdoor flocks, restricting access to wet areas heavily populated by earthworms
vaccineRecommendations: No commercial vaccines exist for Capillariasis.

diseases.name: Gapeworm Infection
diseases.description: Gapeworm infection, or Syngamosis, is a respiratory parasitic disease caused by the nematode Syngamus trachea. Adult worms permanently attach in copula (forming a 'Y' shape) to the tracheal mucosa, obstructing the airway and extracting blood. It frequently affects outdoor poultry, pheasants, and young birds.
clinicalSigns: Pronounced 'gaping' behavior: extreme gasping for air with mouth open and neck fully extended
Frequent, violent head shaking to clear the tracheal obstruction
Coughing and wet gurgling respiratory sounds
Progressive weakness, hypoxia, and weight loss
Death primarily occurs in young birds due to asphyxiation
transmission: Direct ingestion of infective embryonated eggs or hatched larvae from the soil
More commonly, ingestion of earthworms, snails, and slugs which act as highly efficient transport (paratenic) hosts, storing infective larvae in their tissues for years
diagnosis: Direct observation by shining a bright light down the throat of the bird to visualize the distinctive red, Y-shaped worms attached to the tracheal wall
Fecal flotation or examination of tracheal swabs to identify characteristic operculated eggs
Gross necropsy confirming worms and hemorrhagic tracheitis
treatment: Administration of anthelmintics such as fenbendazole or flubendazole in drinking water or feed
Severely affected birds may require manual removal of the worms using a specialized loop or forceps, though this is risky and stressful
control: Preventing flock access to intermediate hosts by keeping birds on concrete, wire, or indoors
Frequent rotation of outdoor ranges and avoiding wet, heavily shaded areas where earthworms and slugs thrive
Prophylactic deworming in highly endemic areas
vaccineRecommendations: No vaccines are available for Gapeworm.

diseases.name: Cryptosporidiosis
diseases.description: Cryptosporidiosis in poultry is an infectious disease caused by small protozoan parasites, primarily Cryptosporidium baileyi (affecting the respiratory and digestive tracts) and C. meleagridis (primarily enteric). It is an opportunistic pathogen that causes significant morbidity, especially in birds with compromised immune systems.
clinicalSigns: Intestinal form: Persistent watery diarrhea, marked stunting of growth, and poor feed conversion
Respiratory form (C. baileyi): Sneezing, coughing, dyspnea, and swollen sinuses (resembling viral bronchitis)
Severe lethargy, depression, and ruffled feathers
Increased mortality if complicated by secondary bacterial infections (e.g., E. coli)
transmission: Fecal-oral route through ingestion of infectious, thick-walled oocysts from contaminated litter, feed, or water
Inhalation of aerosolized oocysts directly infecting the respiratory tract
Oocysts are fully infectious upon excretion and are highly resistant to standard chemical disinfectants
diagnosis: Microscopic examination of fecal smears or mucosal scrapings utilizing specialized acid-fast staining to visualize small red oocysts
Immunofluorescence assays (IFA) or ELISA on fecal samples
Histopathology revealing spherical organisms attached to the apical border of epithelial cells in the trachea, bursa, or intestines
treatment: No effective, specific anti-cryptosporidial drugs are currently approved or highly successful in poultry.
Treatment focuses entirely on supportive care, optimizing the environment, and treating concurrent secondary bacterial infections.
control: Implementation of extreme hygiene and biosecurity measures
Thorough mechanical cleaning of houses followed by extended downtime and drying
Chemical disinfection requires specific agents like 5-10% ammonia solutions, formalin, or hydrogen peroxide to effectively inactivate oocysts
vaccineRecommendations: No commercial vaccines are available for Cryptosporidium in poultry.

diseases.name: Giardiasis
diseases.description: Giardiasis is an intestinal infection caused by the flagellated protozoan Giardia spp. While relatively rare as a primary clinical disease in commercial poultry, it occasionally affects backyard flocks or pet birds, adhering to the small intestine and causing malabsorption and chronic digestive upset.
clinicalSigns: Persistent, foul-smelling, watery diarrhea
Chronic weight loss and poor body condition
Stunted growth in younger birds
Dull, unkempt plumage and general weakness
Often asymptomatic in adult, immunocompetent birds
transmission: Direct fecal-oral route via ingestion of infectious cysts from contaminated drinking water, puddles, or wet feed
Cysts can survive for extended periods in cool, moist environmental conditions
diagnosis: Direct microscopic examination of warm, fresh fecal smears to visualize the motile, flagellated trophozoites ('falling leaf' motility)
Fecal flotation utilizing zinc sulfate to identify the characteristic cysts
Commercial ELISA or IFA tests designed to detect Giardia antigen in feces
treatment: Administration of antiprotozoal medications such as metronidazole, ronidazole, or fenbendazole
Note: The use of metronidazole and related nitroimidazoles is strictly prohibited in food-producing poultry in many jurisdictions.
control: Providing clean, sanitized, and closed drinking water systems
Preventing flock access to stagnant ground water or puddles
Routine sanitation of feeders and drinkers, and maintaining dry litter conditions
vaccineRecommendations: No vaccines are available for Giardiasis in poultry.

diseases.name: Baylisascariasis
diseases.description: Baylisascariasis is a fatal neurological disease caused by the aberrant migration of the larval stage of Baylisascaris procyonis, the raccoon roundworm. Poultry act as accidental paratenic hosts, experiencing severe tissue damage (Neural Larva Migrans) as the larvae travel through the brain and spinal cord.
clinicalSigns: Sudden onset of severe incoordination and staggering (ataxia)
Head tilt and severe torticollis (wry neck)
Progressive paralysis of the legs and wings
Lethargy, weakness, and altered mental state
Rapid progression to death as central nervous system damage worsens
transmission: Ingestion of highly resilient, embryonated B. procyonis eggs from the environment
Infection is strictly linked to exposure to areas contaminated by raccoon feces (latrines)
The disease is not transmitted from bird to bird
diagnosis: Clinical presentation of acute neurological signs in birds with access to areas frequented by raccoons

Histopathology of the brain and spinal cord post-mortem is required for definitive diagnosis, revealing severe eosinophilic encephalitis and the physical presence of large nematode larvae in the neural tissue
treatment: Treatment is unrewarding and ineffective once neurological signs have appeared due to irreversible brain damage.
Humane euthanasia of affected birds is strongly recommended.
control: Absolute exclusion of raccoons from poultry houses, runs, and feed storage areas
Identification and extremely careful removal and destruction (e.g., burning) of raccoon feces/latrines in the vicinity
Preventing foraging in heavily wooded or contaminated soil
vaccineRecommendations: No vaccines exist for Baylisascariasis.

diseases.name: Toxoplasmosis
diseases.description: Toxoplasmosis is a parasitic disease caused by the protozoan Toxoplasma gondii. While clinical disease in poultry is relatively rare, birds can serve as intermediate hosts, harboring tissue cysts that pose a serious zoonotic risk to humans and other mammals upon consumption of undercooked meat.
clinicalSigns: Often asymptomatic in healthy flocks
In severe cases or immunocompromised birds: incoordination (ataxia) and altered gait
Severe lethargy, depression, and generalized weakness
Respiratory distress or gasping if lungs are involved
Occasional sudden death without clear premonitory signs
transmission: Ingestion of infectious oocysts from soil, water, or feed contaminated with feces from infected felines (the definitive hosts)
Mechanical transmission by earthworms or insects accessing contaminated feces
diagnosis: Serology (ELISA, agglutination tests) to detect specific antibodies
Histopathology of brain and muscle tissues demonstrating protozoal cysts
PCR testing for definitive identification of T. gondii DNA
treatment: Treatment is generally not pursued in commercial poultry due to food safety regulations and the inability of drugs to completely clear tissue cysts.
control: Strict biosecurity to prevent feral or domestic cats from accessing poultry houses, runs, feed, and water sources
Aggressive rodent control programs to eliminate the prey base that attracts cats
Proper storage of feed to prevent fecal contamination
vaccineRecommendations: No commercial vaccines are available for Toxoplasmosis in poultry.

diseases.name: Sarcocystosis
diseases.description: Sarcocystosis is a parasitic infection caused by Sarcocystis species, with poultry acting as intermediate hosts. It is typically an incidental finding characterized by rice-like cysts in skeletal muscles but can occasionally cause severe pulmonary and systemic disease in acute infections.
clinicalSigns: Usually subclinical and asymptomatic in mild infections
In acute, massive infections: severe weakness and lethargy
