Respiratory distress and gasping due to pulmonary edema during the acute phase
Progressive weight loss and unthriftiness
Sudden mortality in severe outbreaks
transmission: Ingestion of sporocysts shed in the feces of definitive hosts (such as dogs, cats, opossums, or wild carnivores)
Definitive hosts acquire the parasite by consuming raw, infected avian muscle tissue
diagnosis: Gross observation of macroscopic, whitish, rice-grain-like cysts (sarcocysts) in the breast or leg muscles during necropsy or processing
Histopathology of affected muscle tissue to visualize the internal structure of the cysts
PCR for accurate species identification
treatment: No practical or approved treatment exists for commercial poultry once cysts have formed in the muscles.
control: Preventing carnivores, stray dogs, cats, and wild scavengers from accessing poultry houses and ranges
Immediate and proper disposal of dead birds to break the predator-prey life cycle
Securing feed and water sources from fecal contamination
vaccineRecommendations: No commercial vaccines are available.

diseases.name: Yeast Infection (Candidiasis)
diseases.description: Candidiasis, commonly known as 'thrush' or 'sour crop', is a fungal infection of the upper digestive tract caused by Candida albicans. It is frequently an opportunistic infection that arises secondary to prolonged antibiotic use, poor sanitation, or severe environmental stress which disrupts the normal gut flora.
clinicalSigns: Thickened, whitish, pseudomembranous lesions or plaques in the mouth and throat
Enlarged, fluid-filled crop with severely delayed emptying
Foul-smelling, sour odor emanating from the beak
Reduced appetite leading to progressive weight loss and unthriftiness
Ruffled feathers, listlessness, and occasionally watery diarrhea
transmission: Candida albicans is a normal inhabitant of the digestive tract and environment
Disease occurs non-contagiously when the normal bacterial flora is disrupted (e.g., by antibiotics) allowing the yeast to overgrow
Ingestion of heavily contaminated drinking water or moldy feed
diagnosis: Gross lesions during necropsy showing a 'terry-cloth' or 'turkish towel' appearance of the crop mucosa
Microscopic examination of crop scrapings showing budding yeast cells and pseudohyphae
Fungal culture on Sabouraud dextrose agar for confirmation
treatment: Immediate withdrawal of any broad-spectrum growth-promoting antibiotics to allow normal gut flora to recover
Administration of copper sulfate in drinking water (must be carefully dosed to avoid toxicity)
Addition of specific antifungals like nystatin to the feed in severe flock outbreaks
control: Rigorous cleaning and regular sanitation of water lines, tanks, and drinkers
Judicious use of antibiotics only when strictly necessary
Inclusion of organic acids or probiotics in the water or feed to promote a healthy acidic gut microbiome
vaccineRecommendations: No vaccines are available for Candidiasis.

diseases.name: Favus (Ringworm)
diseases.description: Favus, or avian ringworm, is a chronic dermatophyte fungal infection primarily caused by Microsporum gallinae. It typically affects the unfeathered areas of the head, causing unsightly crusty lesions. While it generally causes low mortality, it is highly contagious and possesses zoonotic potential.
clinicalSigns: Distinctive white, powdery, or flour-like spots developing on the comb and wattles
Spots coalesce to form thick, wrinkled, grayish-white crusts
Skin becomes excessively dry, scaly, and wrinkled
If the infection spreads to feathered areas (neck and body), feathers become brittle and break off at the base
Typically does not cause systemic illness unless lesions become massive or secondary bacterial infections occur
transmission: Direct contact with clinically infected birds
Fomite transmission via contaminated housing, equipment, rubbing against wire, or environment where resilient fungal spores can persist for months
Entry facilitated by minor abrasions or scratches on the comb
diagnosis: Clinical appearance of the powdery comb lesions
Microscopic examination of skin scrapings cleared with potassium hydroxide (KOH) demonstrating fungal hyphae
Fungal culture on Sabouraud dextrose agar for definitive identification
treatment: Topical application of antifungal creams or solutions (e.g., miconazole, clotrimazole, or chlorhexidine) applied daily until lesions resolve (most practical for exhibition or pet birds)
Treatment is often not economically viable for large commercial flocks, and the disease may be self-limiting in healthy birds
control: Immediate isolation or culling of affected birds to prevent slow, steady spread
Thorough cleaning and aggressive disinfection of the environment using effective fungicidal agents
Minimizing injuries to combs by providing adequate feeder space and addressing aggressive behavior
vaccineRecommendations: No commercial vaccines exist for Favus in poultry.

diseases.name: Lice Infestation
diseases.description: Pediculosis in poultry is an infestation by chewing lice (most commonly Menacanthus stramineus). These obligate ectoparasites live entirely on the bird, feeding on skin scales, feather parts, and occasionally blood, causing severe irritation, skin damage, and significant stress leading to production losses.
clinicalSigns: Visual presence of fast-moving, flat, straw-colored insects near the vent, under the wings, or on the breast skin
Clusters of whitish nits (eggs) heavily cemented to the base of feather shafts, especially around the vent
Excessive preening, scratching, and restless behavior
Dull, broken, or 'moth-eaten' plumage
Skin reddening and scabs from irritation and scratching
Decreased egg production and general unthriftiness in severe infestations
transmission: Direct bird-to-bird contact is the primary route
Lice cannot survive long off the host, but fomite transmission via shared nesting boxes or dust-bathing areas can occur
Introduction via wild birds or introducing new, infested stock into the flock
diagnosis: Direct visual examination of the bird's skin by parting the feathers, quickly revealing adult lice moving away from the light
Identification of characteristic egg clusters (nits) at the feather base
treatment: Application of approved insecticidal dusts, sprays, or dips (e.g., pyrethroids, spinosad) directly to the birds, ensuring penetration to the skin
Treatment must be repeated in 7-10 days to kill newly hatched nymphs, as insecticides generally do not kill the resilient eggs (nits)
control: Routine physical examination of flocks to catch infestations early
Strict quarantine and prophylactic treatment of any new birds introduced to the premises
Providing access to dry dust baths supplemented with diatomaceous earth or wood ash to encourage natural grooming and parasite control
vaccineRecommendations: No vaccines are available for lice infestation.

diseases.name: Northern Fowl Mite Infestation
diseases.description: The Northern Fowl Mite (Ornithonyssus sylviarum) is one of the most destructive, blood-sucking ectoparasites of poultry. Unlike red mites, it spends its entire life cycle on the host bird. Heavy infestations cause severe anemia, dramatic drops in egg production, and significant welfare compromise.
clinicalSigns: Matted, soiled, dark-colored feathers around the vent area due to accumulating mite excrement and dried blood
Tiny black, red, or grey crawling mites clearly visible on the skin when feathers are parted
Darkened, thickened, scabby, and highly irritated skin around the vent
Pale comb and wattles indicative of severe anemia
Significant scratching, restlessness, weight loss, and reduced egg production
transmission: Rapid direct transmission from bird to bird
Mechanical transmission by personnel handling the birds, contaminated equipment, and egg flats
Mites can survive off the host in the environment for up to 2-3 weeks under cool, humid conditions
diagnosis: Direct visual examination by parting the feathers around the vent to reveal rapidly moving mites and characteristic skin lesions
Workers may report mites crawling on their arms and hands after handling birds or eggs
treatment: Application of approved acaricides (e.g., permethrin, carbaryl, spinosad) directly onto the birds using high-pressure sprays, ensuring the chemical penetrates the feathers to reach the skin around the vent
Dusting the birds and nesting areas with diatomaceous earth
control: Exclusion of wild birds (e.g., sparrows, pigeons) from poultry houses, as they are major carriers
Strict biosecurity regarding the movement of equipment, flats, and personnel between houses
Routine, scheduled flock inspections to detect infestations before they spread widely
vaccineRecommendations: No commercial vaccines are available for Northern Fowl Mites.

diseases.name: Tropical Fowl Mite Infestation
diseases.description: The Tropical Fowl Mite (Ornithonyssus bursa) is a blood-feeding ectoparasite very similar in appearance and impact to the Northern Fowl Mite, but it is primarily endemic to warmer, tropical, and subtropical regions. It causes severe skin irritation, blood loss, and decreased flock productivity.
clinicalSigns: Intense scratching, agitation, and restlessness
Presence of tiny, fast-moving reddish-black mites on the skin and feathers, particularly around the vent
Dark, crusted, reddened skin and dirty, matted feathers
Pale comb and wattles resulting from significant blood loss (anemia)
Unthriftiness, weight loss, and noticeable drops in egg production
transmission: Direct contact with infested birds
Highly associated with the presence of wild birds (e.g., sparrows, starlings) nesting in or around the poultry house, which serve as natural reservoirs
Mites can survive in the environment and nests for short periods
diagnosis: Visual identification of mites directly on the bird's body
Microscopic examination to accurately differentiate it from the Northern Fowl Mite or Red Mite, based on specific morphological features (e.g., the position of the anus on the anal plate)
treatment: Direct application of approved acaricidal sprays, dips, or dusts (e.g., pyrethroids) to the birds, ensuring thorough coverage of the vent and abdomen
Treatment of the immediate housing environment, nests, and cracks where mites may briefly hide
control: Eradication and physical exclusion of wild bird nests in, on, and around the poultry facilities
Maintaining rigorous biosecurity protocols and conducting routine flock inspections
Use of diatomaceous earth in dust baths
vaccineRecommendations: No commercial vaccines are available.

diseases.name: Scaly Leg Mite Infestation

symptomsEnhanced.name: Gait, Reluctance to stand or walk

diseases.description: Scaly Leg Disease is caused by the microscopic burrowing mite Knemidocoptes mutans. These mites tunnel deep under the epidermal scales of the lower legs and feet, feeding on keratin and tissue fluid. This causes severe inflammation, massive crusting, and eventually crippling bone and joint deformities.
clinicalSigns: Scales on the legs and toes become raised, thickened, and heavily crusted
Presence of white, powdery, or scab-like exudate beneath and between the scales
Severe itchiness leading to pecking at the legs
In advanced, chronic cases: severe swelling, lameness, joint deformities, and potential loss of digits
Decreased mobility affecting access to feed and water
transmission: Spreads very slowly via direct bird-to-bird contact
Prolonged contact in shared environments, primarily spreading along wooden roosts
Older birds and backyard or free-range flocks are most commonly affected
diagnosis: Highly characteristic clinical appearance of severely thickened, crusty leg scales
Deep skin scraping of the affected area examined under a microscope to identify the short-legged, spherical mites
treatment: Physical treatment involves soaking the legs in warm soapy water, gently scrubbing dead scales, and coating the legs entirely in petroleum jelly or mineral oil to suffocate the mites
Systemic or topical application of antiparasitics like ivermectin or moxidectin (off-label use requires strict veterinary guidance regarding meat and egg withdrawal periods)
control: Culling of severely deformed, chronic carriers
Thorough cleaning, treating, or replacing old wooden roosts with smooth materials, as mites harbor in wood crevices
Regular inspection of the flock's legs to catch early infestations
vaccineRecommendations: No vaccines exist for scaly leg mites.

diseases.name: Flea Infestation
diseases.description: Flea infestations in poultry are most commonly caused by the Sticktight Flea (Echidnophaga gallinacea). Unlike typical fleas that move freely, female sticktight fleas permanently embed their mouthparts into the unfeathered skin of the bird's head, causing severe irritation, blood loss, and potential secondary infections.
clinicalSigns: Dark brown or black, wart-like nodules clustered heavily on the comb, wattles, and around the eyes (these are the embedded fleas)
Constant scratching of the head and face against objects
Reddening and inflammation of the affected skin
Pale comb and wattles indicating anemia, general weakness, and reduced production
In extreme cases, fleas clustering around the eyes can cause swelling leading to blindness
transmission: Direct contact with infested environments, particularly sandy, dry, and shaded soil
The female flea drops eggs from the host into the litter or soil, where larvae develop and pupate before the adult fleas jump onto a new host
Introduction by other animals, including dogs, cats, and wild birds
diagnosis: Straightforward visual identification of dark brown to black fleas firmly embedded in the unfeathered skin of the head and face
Fleas are notoriously difficult to brush off or detach manually
treatment: Careful application of an approved flea powder, ointment, or suffocating agent (like petroleum jelly) directly onto the attached fleas to kill them in place
Systemic treatments such as ivermectin may be considered under strict veterinary supervision
Forceful manual removal should be avoided as mouthparts left in the skin can cause severe infection
control: Complete removal and burning of infested litter
Treating the floor and surrounding soil with appropriate premises insecticides or insect growth regulators to break the environmental flea life cycle
Preventing dogs and cats from resting in or accessing the poultry area
vaccineRecommendations: No vaccines are available for flea infestations.

diseases.name: Flystrike
diseases.description: Flystrike, or cutaneous myiasis, is a devastating and extremely painful condition where flies (such as blowflies or flesh flies) lay eggs in open wounds, soiled feathers, or natural body openings. The rapidly hatching maggots consume living and necrotic tissue, leading to severe toxemia, shock, and rapid death if left untreated.
clinicalSigns: Visible maggots or clusters of fly eggs in wounds, the vent area, or matted plumage
Foul odor of rotting tissue emanating from the bird
Severe depression, lethargy, and reluctance to move
Birds may isolate themselves and display signs of severe shock and weakness
Rapid systemic decline and sudden death due to secondary septicemia and toxemia
transmission: Not contagious from bird to bird
Occurs when adult flies are attracted to pre-existing wounds (e.g., from cannibalism or predator attacks) or heavily soiled, diarrheic vent feathers
Warm, humid weather severely exacerbates the incidence
diagnosis: Direct visual confirmation of maggots actively feeding in tissue cavities or under matted plumage upon close inspection
treatment: Immediate physical removal of all maggots and eggs using forceps, followed by aggressive flushing of the wound with dilute antiseptics (e.g., chlorhexidine or iodine)
Careful trimming of surrounding soiled or matted feathers
Application of topical wound sprays containing mild insecticides or fly repellents
Administration of systemic broad-spectrum antibiotics to prevent septicemia
control: Prompt identification and treatment of any wounds or injuries to prevent fly attraction
Addressing underlying causes of diarrhea to ensure vent feathers remain clean
Aggressive fly control programs in and around the poultry house using baits, traps, and proper manure management
vaccineRecommendations: No vaccines exist for flystrike.

diseases.name: Rickets

symptomsEnhanced.name: Gait, Reluctance to stand or walk

diseases.description: Rickets is a metabolic bone disease affecting young, growing poultry, characterized by the failure of bone mineralization. It is caused by an absolute deficiency or improper ratio of dietary Calcium, Phosphorus, and/or Vitamin D3, often secondary to malabsorption conditions like enteritis.
clinicalSigns: Soft, rubbery bones and beak that bend easily
Severe lameness, reluctance to walk, and resting on hocks
Enlarged hock joints and bowing of the legs
Stunted growth and poor feathering
In layers, may present as thin-shelled eggs or cage layer fatigue (osteoporosis)
transmission: Non-contagious. This is strictly a nutritional deficiency or metabolic failure issue.
Can occur flock-wide due to feed mill mixing errors or concurrent enteric disease (e.g., malabsorption syndrome).
diagnosis: Clinical presentation of soft, flexible long bones (tibia/femur) and rubbery beak
Gross necropsy revealing thickened, irregular epiphyseal growth plates in long bones and 'rachitic rosary' (nodules on ribs)
Analysis of feed samples for precise Calcium, Phosphorus, and Vitamin D3 levels
treatment: Immediate administration of water-soluble Vitamin D3 (which provides the fastest absorption)
Correction of the calcium/phosphorus ratio in the feed
Treatment of any underlying enteric diseases causing malabsorption
control: Rigorous quality control during feed formulation and milling to prevent mixing errors
Routine monitoring of flock gut health to ensure adequate nutrient absorption
vaccineRecommendations: No vaccines are applicable for this nutritional disorder.

diseases.name: Curly Toe Paralysis

symptomsEnhanced.name: Gait, Reluctance to stand or walk

diseases.description: Curly Toe Paralysis is a specific neurological condition caused by a dietary deficiency of Riboflavin (Vitamin B2). It leads to peripheral nerve demyelination, most notably affecting the sciatic nerve, resulting in characteristic leg and toe deformities in young chicks.
clinicalSigns: Inward curling of the toes on one or both feet (pathognomonic sign)
Birds resting on their hocks with the toes curled underneath them
Progressive lameness and reluctance to move
Stunted growth, emaciation, and weakness
Decreased hatchability and increased embryonic mortality in breeder flocks
transmission: Non-contagious. Results from feeding rations lacking adequate riboflavin.
Can also occur in day-old chicks hatched from breeders with riboflavin-deficient diets.
diagnosis: Classic clinical presentation of curled toes in young birds
Gross pathology showing distinct enlargement and swelling of the sciatic nerve
Histopathology revealing demyelination and axonal degeneration of peripheral nerves
Rapid recovery following therapeutic riboflavin administration
treatment: Immediate oral administration of Riboflavin via drinking water.
While mild to moderate cases recover rapidly, severe chronic cases with permanent nerve damage may not reverse.
control: Ensuring commercial feeds are adequately supplemented with vitamin premixes containing Vitamin B2
Careful management of breeder diets to ensure egg yolk carries sufficient vitamins for the embryo
vaccineRecommendations: No vaccines are available or applicable.

diseases.name: Slipped Tendon

symptomsEnhanced.name: Gait, Reluctance to stand or walk

diseases.description: Slipped Tendon, scientifically known as Perosis or chondrodystrophy, is an anatomical disorder affecting the intertarsal joint of fast-growing birds. It is primarily caused by deficiencies in trace minerals (Manganese, Zinc) or vitamins (Choline, Biotin, Folic acid, Niacin), leading to the gastrocnemius tendon slipping out of its condylar groove.
clinicalSigns: Noticeable enlargement, flattening, and swelling of the hock joint
Lateral or medial rotation of the lower leg
The Achilles (gastrocnemius) tendon visibly displacing from its groove at the back of the hock
Severe lameness and inability to bear weight on the affected leg
Secondary starvation and dehydration due to immobility
transmission: Non-contagious. Results from complex nutritional deficiencies or imbalances (e.g., high dietary calcium inhibiting manganese absorption)
Strongly associated with the rapid growth rates of modern broiler genetics
diagnosis: Physical examination of the leg reveals the dislocated tendon, which can sometimes be manually palpated or visualized outside the condylar groove
Gross pathology showing flattened condyles and shallow trochlear grooves on the distal tibiotarsus
treatment: Treatment is generally unrewarding once the tendon has fully slipped and bone deformity has occurred.
Humane culling is often the necessary response for severely affected individuals to prevent welfare issues.
control: Meticulous verification of micro-nutrient premixes (Manganese, Choline, Biotin, Zinc) in the feed
Managing early growth curves in broilers through lighting programs to allow skeletal development to keep pace with muscle mass
vaccineRecommendations: No vaccines are available for this condition.

diseases.name: Fatty Liver Disease
diseases.description: Fatty Liver Hemorrhagic Syndrome (FLHS) is a severe metabolic disorder almost exclusively affecting high-producing caged laying hens. It is characterized by excessive lipid deposition in the liver, rendering it extremely friable and prone to rupture, leading to fatal internal bleeding.
clinicalSigns: Sudden death of seemingly healthy, high-producing hens
Birds are typically overweight with significant abdominal fat pads
Pale comb and wattles immediately prior to or upon death (due to acute hemorrhage)
Unexplained drops in flock egg production
Enlarged, soft abdomen
transmission: Non-contagious. Results from a positive energy balance (consuming more calories than required for maintenance and production), restricted movement (caged systems), and the high estrogen levels of laying hens which naturally stimulate hepatic lipogenesis.
diagnosis: Necropsy is definitive: massive blood clots found in the abdominal cavity originating from liver rupture
The liver is pale yellow, extremely enlarged, and greasy or friable to the touch
Subcutaneous and abdominal fat deposits are excessive
treatment: Immediate dietary intervention is required.
Addition of lipotropic agents to the feed or water (e.g., Choline chloride, Inositol, Vitamin B12, and Vitamin E) to facilitate fat transport out of the liver
Replacing high-carbohydrate energy sources with fat/oil sources to reduce de novo lipogenesis in the liver
control: Strict monitoring of body weight and body condition scores of pullets and layers
Avoiding overfeeding energy-dense diets; using precision feeding based on environmental temperature and production phase
Minimizing stress, which can trigger acute liver rupture
vaccineRecommendations: No vaccines are available for this metabolic syndrome.

diseases.name: Vitamin A Deficiency
diseases.description: Vitamin A Deficiency is a nutritional disease causing widespread squamous metaplasia (keratinization) of mucous membranes in the respiratory, digestive, and reproductive tracts. This breakdown of mucosal barriers heavily predisposes the bird to severe secondary bacterial infections.
clinicalSigns: Watery eyes that rapidly progress to an accumulation of cheesy, caseous material beneath the eyelids (often blinding the bird)
Nasal discharge and sticky eyelids
Pustule-like lesions inside the mouth, pharynx, and esophagus
Stunted growth, ruffled feathers, and weakness
Severe drop in egg production and hatchability, and blood spots in eggs
transmission: Non-contagious. Results from feed mixing errors, prolonged feed storage causing vitamin oxidation, or poor gut absorption.
Can occur in chicks if the breeder hen's diet was deficient.
diagnosis: Clinical presentation of ocular caseous exudate combined with pathognomonic white, pustular lesions in the upper digestive tract
Histopathology showing classic squamous metaplasia of glandular epithelium
Analysis of feed and liver tissue for Vitamin A content
treatment: Immediate administration of water-dispersible stabilized Vitamin A at high therapeutic doses (e.g., 10,000 IU/liter of water) for rapid absorption
Treatment of secondary bacterial infections (like Coryza or Colibacillosis) if present
control: Ensuring commercial feeds contain stabilized forms of Vitamin A and adequate antioxidants
Proper storage of feed to prevent prolonged heat and moisture exposure which destroys vitamins
Avoiding the feeding of old, rancid, or oxidized ingredients
vaccineRecommendations: No vaccines are available.

diseases.name: Vitamin E Deficiency
diseases.description: Vitamin E Deficiency causes significant oxidative stress and manifests in three distinct syndromes in poultry depending on concurrent deficiencies (like Selenium): Encephalomalacia ('Crazy Chick Disease'), Exudative Diathesis (severe edema), and Nutritional Muscular Dystrophy (white muscle disease).
clinicalSigns: Encephalomalacia: Severe ataxia, backward walking, head retraction (stargazing), tumbling, and paralysis in chicks (often days 15-30)
Exudative Diathesis: Massive greenish-blue fluid accumulation under the skin of the breast and abdomen
Muscular Dystrophy: Locomotor weakness, unthriftiness, and pale streaking in breast muscles
Increased mortality
transmission: Non-contagious. Triggered by inadequate dietary Vitamin E or Selenium.
Strongly exacerbated by feeding high levels of polyunsaturated fatty acids (like fish oils) without sufficient antioxidants, leading to rancidity and vitamin destruction.
diagnosis: Clinical neurological signs or gross subcutaneous edema
Necropsy for Encephalomalacia reveals swollen, hemorrhagic, or necrotic lesions on the cerebellum
Necropsy for Muscular Dystrophy reveals white, degenerative streaks in the breast and leg muscles
Feed analysis confirming high peroxide values (rancidity) or low Vitamin E/Selenium
treatment: Immediate administration of Vitamin E and Selenium preparations in drinking water
Rapidly replacing suspected rancid feed with fresh, properly supplemented rations
control: Use of adequate antioxidants (e.g., ethoxyquin, BHT) in feed containing high fat levels
Proper formulation ensuring balanced Vitamin E and Selenium levels
Storing feed in cool, dry conditions to prevent fat oxidation
vaccineRecommendations: No vaccines exist for this nutritional deficiency.

diseases.name: Thiamine Deficiency
diseases.description: Thiamine (Vitamin B1) Deficiency causes profound disruptions in carbohydrate metabolism, leading to severe neuromuscular disease known as polyneuritis. It occurs sporadically but progresses rapidly to paralysis and death if not treated.
clinicalSigns: Loss of appetite and severe lethargy
Progressive neuromuscular signs: ataxia and leg paralysis
Classic 'stargazing' posture: the bird sits on its hocks with the head drawn rigidly back over the spine (opisthotonos)
General body tremors
Rapidly fatal if untreated
transmission: Non-contagious. Caused by missing vitamin premix in the diet.
Can also be induced by feeding high levels of thiaminase-containing ingredients (certain raw fish) or prolonged overdosing of amprolium (a coccidiostat that acts as a thiamine antagonist).
diagnosis: The classic 'stargazing' posture is highly indicative
Dramatic and rapid recovery (within hours) following a therapeutic injection or oral dose of thiamine is the most practical diagnostic tool
Histopathology showing peripheral nerve degeneration
treatment: Immediate administration of Thiamine via drinking water or, in severe cases, by intramuscular injection.
Treatment yields rapid and near-complete recovery if administered before irreversible damage occurs.
control: Ensuring proper vitamin premix inclusion during feed milling
Strict adherence to dosage instructions when using amprolium for coccidiosis treatment
Avoiding the feeding of moldy feed or raw fish products that destroy Thiamine
vaccineRecommendations: No vaccines apply.

diseases.name: Biotin Deficiency
diseases.description: Biotin is an essential B-complex vitamin vital for skin health, bone development, and metabolism. Its deficiency leads to severe dermatitis and contributes to bone malformations, significantly impacting welfare and growth, particularly in turkeys and broilers.
clinicalSigns: Severe dermatitis on the footpads and toes (dry, rough, cracking, and bleeding fissures) which closely mimics environmental bumblefoot
Crusty, scabby lesions around the corners of the beak, eyes, and vent
Shortening and bending of bones leading to perosis (slipped tendon)
Poor, brittle feathering
Decreased hatchability with parrot-beak deformities in embryos
transmission: Non-contagious dietary issue.
Caused by feed mixing errors or interference with absorption (e.g., severe enteritis, or feeding diets high in raw egg whites which contain avidin, a biotin binder).
diagnosis: Observation of the classic combination of footpad dermatitis and lesions around the beak
Differentiation from contact dermatitis (bumblefoot) by checking for concurrent facial lesions and slipped tendons
Clinical response to water-soluble Biotin supplementation
treatment: Supplementation of Biotin in the drinking water.
While skin lesions generally resolve with treatment, severe bone deformities (like slipped tendons) are permanent.
control: Ensuring commercial diets are adequately supplemented with synthetic Biotin, as the bioavailability of Biotin in common grains (like wheat and barley) is highly variable.
Maintaining gut health to ensure optimal vitamin synthesis by intestinal flora.
vaccineRecommendations: No vaccines available.

diseases.name: Choline Deficiency
diseases.description: Choline is essential for cell membrane structure, lipid transport (lipotropic agent), and neurotransmitter synthesis. Its deficiency in growing poultry profoundly impacts bone development and liver function, leading to anatomical deformities and fat accumulation.
clinicalSigns: High incidence of perosis (slipped tendon) characterized by flattened hocks and lateral twisting of the lower legs
Stunted growth and poor feed conversion
Lameness and reluctance to walk
In adult layers, it strongly exacerbates Fatty Liver Hemorrhagic Syndrome due to the inability to mobilize fats from the liver
transmission: Non-contagious. Results from inadequate inclusion of choline in the diet.
Particularly critical in starter diets as young chicks lack the ability to synthesize sufficient choline internally.
diagnosis: Clinical presentation of perosis and stunting
Necropsy revealing slipped tendons and excessive fat accumulation in the liver
Ruling out other causes of perosis (Manganese, Biotin, Folic acid) via feed analysis
treatment: Administration of Choline chloride via drinking water or feed.
Treatment prevents further cases, but anatomical damage (slipped tendons) in already affected birds is largely irreversible.
control: Routine supplementation of Choline chloride in all commercial starter and grower diets.
Supplementing layer diets to assist in liver fat metabolism and prevent Fatty Liver Disease.
vaccineRecommendations: No vaccines available.

diseases.name: Niacin Deficiency
diseases.description: Niacin (Vitamin B3 or Nicotinic Acid) is critical for cellular energy metabolism. Deficiency causes systemic issues characterized by bone malformations, severe inflammation of the digestive tract, and poor growth. Turkeys and waterfowl are particularly susceptible due to higher metabolic requirements.
clinicalSigns: Severe bowing of the legs and enlargement of the hock joint, closely resembling perosis (but the Achilles tendon rarely slips)
Inflammation of the mouth, tongue, and upper esophagus (stomatitis)
Poor, brittle feathering and stunted growth
Persistent watery diarrhea
In ducks, it presents classically as 'bowed leg' disease with severe lameness
transmission: Non-contagious. Results from feeding grain-based diets (e.g., corn) where niacin is tightly bound and biologically unavailable, without proper synthetic supplementation.
diagnosis: Clinical observation of bowed legs, particularly in susceptible species like ducks and turkeys, combined with stomatitis
Feed analysis confirming inadequate available niacin levels
Rapid clinical improvement of early-stage lameness following niacin supplementation
treatment: Immediate supplementation of Niacin (nicotinic acid or nicotinamide) in the drinking water.
Early intervention can reverse leg bowing, but advanced skeletal deformities will remain permanent.
control: Ensuring commercial diets are fortified with highly bioavailable synthetic niacin
Paying specific attention to the higher niacin requirements when formulating feed for waterfowl and turkeys
vaccineRecommendations: No vaccines available.

diseases.name: Folic Acid Deficiency
diseases.description: Folic acid is essential for DNA synthesis and cellular division. Its deficiency severely impacts rapidly dividing cells, leading to macrocytic anemia, poor feathering, and embryonic mortality. Turkeys are particularly susceptible, often developing characteristic cervical paralysis.
clinicalSigns: Pale comb and mucous membranes due to megaloblastic (macrocytic) anemia
Stunted growth, poor feed conversion, and severe lethargy
Brittle, depigmented, and structurally compromised feathers
In turkeys: distinct cervical paralysis (stiff, extended neck)
High embryonic mortality (often around day 20) and poor hatchability in breeder flocks
transmission: Non-contagious. Results from inadequate dietary supplementation or poor intestinal absorption.
diagnosis: Blood smear evaluation revealing characteristic macrocytic (enlarged) red blood cells
Observation of cervical paralysis in poults combined with anemia
Feed analysis and dramatic clinical response to folic acid supplementation
treatment: Immediate oral or in-water supplementation of folic acid
Injectable folic acid can be used for severely paralyzed or anemic birds to initiate rapid recovery
control: Routine verification of vitamin premixes in rations, ensuring adequate margins of safety for folic acid, particularly in turkey starter and breeder diets.
vaccineRecommendations: No vaccines apply to this condition.

diseases.name: Pantothenic Acid Deficiency
diseases.description: Pantothenic acid (Vitamin B5) is a vital component of Coenzyme A, necessary for energy metabolism. Deficiency manifests primarily as severe skin lesions (dermatitis) and nervous system degeneration, severely reducing the growth and livability of chicks.
clinicalSigns: Crusty, scaly, and thick dermatitis appearing around the beak, eyelids, and vent
Peeling and severe cracking of the skin on the bottom of the feet, causing intense pain and lameness
Ragged, broken, and dull plumage
Stunted growth and progressive weakness
Subcutaneous hemorrhage and high embryonic mortality in breeders
transmission: Non-contagious. Strict nutritional deficiency resulting from unsupplemented or poorly formulated diets.
diagnosis: Observation of characteristic facial and footpad dermatitis
Must be carefully differentiated from Biotin deficiency (which has similar lesions) through feed analysis and therapeutic response trials
Histopathology of the spinal cord showing myelin degeneration
treatment: Rapid administration of calcium pantothenate in drinking water or feed
Skin lesions generally resolve within one to two weeks following treatment
control: Ensuring commercial diets are properly fortified with synthetic pantothenic acid, as natural levels in grains are often insufficient or degraded during processing.
vaccineRecommendations: No vaccines available.

diseases.name: Pyridoxine Deficiency
diseases.description: Pyridoxine (Vitamin B6) is crucial for amino acid metabolism and the synthesis of neurotransmitters. Its deficiency causes extreme hyper-excitability and severe, spasmodic neurological disorders in poultry, leading to exhaustion and death.
clinicalSigns: Extreme hyper-excitability and jerky, uncoordinated movements
Aimless running, flapping of wings, and walking backward
Violent convulsions and seizures, often triggered by sudden noises or handling
Severe depression and exhaustion following convulsive episodes
Significant depression of appetite leading to stunted growth
transmission: Non-contagious. Results directly from dietary insufficiency.
diagnosis: Clinical presentation of violent, jerky nervous signs and seizures without the typical 'stargazing' seen in thiamine deficiency
Rapid resolution of neurological signs following therapeutic administration of pyridoxine
Feed analysis
treatment: Immediate administration of Pyridoxine in the drinking water.
Most birds will recover completely within days if treated early before terminal exhaustion.
control: Routine inclusion of adequate Vitamin B6 in dietary premixes, paying attention to the higher requirements of high-protein diets.
vaccineRecommendations: No vaccines available.

diseases.name: Zinc Deficiency
diseases.description: Zinc is an essential trace mineral required for bone development, feathering, and immune function. Deficiency causes skeletal malformations and severe integumentary issues. It is often induced by high dietary calcium or phytates which bind zinc and render it unavailable.
clinicalSigns: Shortening and thickening of the long bones (legs and wings)
Enlargement of the hock joint, predisposing the bird to slipped tendons
Poor feathering characterized by weak, brittle shafts and lack of typical color/sheen
Scaly dermatitis on the lower legs and feet
Decreased hatchability and weak chicks in breeder flocks
transmission: Non-contagious. Results from absolute dietary deficiency or, more commonly, poor bioavailability due to binding agents (phytate) or excess calcium/phosphorus in the feed.
diagnosis: Observation of poor feathering combined with shortened, thick leg bones
Tissue analysis (bone or liver) confirming low zinc concentrations
Detailed feed analysis checking the ratio of Zinc to Calcium and the presence of phytates
treatment: Supplementation of the diet with highly bioavailable zinc sources (e.g., zinc sulfate or organic zinc chelates)
Adjusting dietary calcium levels to prevent competitive binding
control: Careful feed formulation utilizing phytase enzymes to release bound zinc from plant ingredients
Use of organic trace mineral chelates which are more readily absorbed in the gut
vaccineRecommendations: No vaccines exist for zinc deficiency.

diseases.name: Avian Osteoporosis
diseases.description: Avian Osteoporosis, commonly presenting as 'Cage Layer Fatigue', is a critical metabolic bone disease in high-producing laying hens. It occurs when calcium output for eggshell formation exceeds dietary absorption, forcing the hen to extensively deplete structural calcium from her medullary and cortical bones, leading to fragility and fractures.
clinicalSigns: Acute lameness or complete inability to stand, with birds resting on their sides or hocks
Bones that are extremely brittle and prone to folding or snapping fractures during handling
Increase in thin-shelled, shell-less, or cracked eggs prior to the onset of lameness
Spinal fractures leading to rear body paralysis
Affected birds are often active and alert but physically unable to access feed/water
transmission: Non-contagious. Highly associated with intense genetic selection for high egg production combined with lack of exercise (caged systems) and inadequate pre-lay calcium loading.
diagnosis: Clinical presentation of paralyzed, high-producing hens in cages
Gross necropsy revealing incredibly thin, fragile cortical bones (tibia, femur, ribs) that snap easily without resistance
Normal blood calcium levels initially, dropping only in terminal stages
treatment: Moving affected birds from cages to the floor, allowing easy access to feed and water
Administration of large-particle calcium (oyster shell/limestone) and Vitamin D3
Many birds recover fully if structural fractures haven't severed the spinal cord
control: Optimizing the pullet pre-lay diet with increased calcium to build robust medullary bone reserves prior to the first egg
Providing large-particle calcium sources in the layer diet to ensure slow release in the gut during the night when shell formation occurs
Utilizing furnished cages or aviary systems to promote bone-strengthening exercise
vaccineRecommendations: No vaccines apply to this metabolic disease.

diseases.name: Low Blood Calcium Level
diseases.description: Low blood calcium (Hypocalcemia) is an acute metabolic crisis, most commonly seen in high-producing layers or young pullets just coming into lay. Unlike osteoporosis which takes time to develop, acute hypocalcemia disrupts nerve transmission and muscle contraction, leading to sudden paralysis or tetany.
clinicalSigns: Sudden onset of severe weakness, lethargy, and inability to stand
Flaccid paralysis or muscle fasciculations (tremors)
Production of thin-shelled, shell-less, or completely absent eggs
In severe cases, birds may exhibit seizures, coma, and rapid death
Often triggered by sudden heat stress which alters blood pH and calcium binding
transmission: Non-contagious. Results from an acute imbalance between calcium intake, mobilization from bone, and the high demand for eggshell gland secretion.
diagnosis: Clinical signs of acute paralysis in active laying hens
Immediate, dramatic recovery (often within minutes) following intravenous or oral administration of liquid calcium
Blood biochemistry confirming low ionized calcium levels
treatment: Emergency administration of liquid calcium supplements (calcium gluconate) orally or via injection
Addition of water-soluble Vitamin D3 to enhance gut absorption
control: Ensuring diets have the correct Calcium to Phosphorus ratio
Using a mix of fine and coarse limestone/oyster shell to maintain steady blood calcium levels throughout the night
Supplementing electrolytes and Vitamin C during heat stress to mitigate blood pH changes
vaccineRecommendations: No vaccines available.

diseases.name: Gout

symptomsEnhanced.name: Gait, Reluctance to stand or walk

diseases.description: Gout in poultry is a severe metabolic condition resulting from kidney damage or severe dehydration, causing an inability to excrete uric acid. This leads to toxic accumulations of white urate crystals either inside the joints (Articular Gout) or coating the internal organs (Visceral Gout/Avian Urolithiasis).
clinicalSigns: Articular Gout: Severe lameness, reluctance to walk, and distinct, painful swelling of the toe and hock joints. White to yellowish nodules (tophi) may be visible under the skin of the feet.
Visceral Gout: Severe lethargy, depression, pale combs, and sudden death without prominent external signs.
Whitish, watery diarrhea due to excessive urate excretion attempts
transmission: Non-contagious itself, but can be triggered flock-wide by nephrotoxic factors: infectious agents (e.g., nephropathogenic Infectious Bronchitis Virus), mycotoxins (ochratoxin), severe water deprivation, or feeding high-calcium layer diets to growing pullets.
diagnosis: Gross necropsy is definitive: Visceral gout reveals a striking white, chalky coating (urate crystals) over the heart, liver, and air sacs. Kidneys are swollen and pale.
Articular gout reveals thick, white, toothpaste-like fluid inside the swollen joints
Blood biochemistry showing extremely elevated uric acid levels
treatment: Treatment is generally ineffective once extensive urate deposition has occurred.
Immediate flushing of the flock with fresh, clean water and electrolytes
Adding urine acidifiers (like ammonium chloride) to the water may help dissolve kidney stones in early cases
control: Ensuring constant, unrestricted access to fresh drinking water
Strict avoidance of feeding high-calcium layer rations to young growing birds
Vaccination against nephropathogenic strains of Infectious Bronchitis Virus
vaccineRecommendations: Vaccinate against Infectious Bronchitis (IBV) to prevent viral kidney damage that precipitates gout.

diseases.name: Amyloidosis
diseases.description: Amyloidosis is a chronic, degenerative metabolic condition characterized by the abnormal deposition of insoluble amyloid proteins in various organs, most notably the liver, spleen, and joints. It typically occurs as a secondary response to prolonged, chronic inflammation, bacterial infections, or stress in older birds.
clinicalSigns: Progressive, severe weight loss and generalized unthriftiness
Noticeably enlarged, distended abdomen due to hepatomegaly (liver enlargement) or fluid accumulation (ascites)
Pale comb and wattles indicating chronic illness
Lethargy, weakness, and eventual death due to organ failure
Can present as chronic joint swelling if amyloid deposits in the articular spaces
transmission: Non-contagious. It is an internal pathophysiological response.
Highly correlated with chronic, poorly managed bacterial infections (e.g., chronic Staphylococcosis or prolonged bumblefoot) that trigger continuous acute-phase protein production.
diagnosis: Gross necropsy reveals a massive, pale, firm, and waxy liver (often mistaken for fatty liver, but firm rather than greasy) and enlarged spleen
Definitive diagnosis requires histopathology with Congo Red staining, which highlights the amyloid deposits in bright orange-red under polarized light
treatment: No treatment exists to reverse or remove amyloid deposits once they have formed in the organs.
Humane culling of affected, suffering birds is the standard approach.
control: Aggressive management and prompt treatment of any primary chronic inflammatory conditions or bacterial infections in the flock
Maintaining excellent housing environments to reduce chronic physical stress and injuries
vaccineRecommendations: No vaccines exist for Amyloidosis.

diseases.name: Atherosclerosis
diseases.description: Atherosclerosis is a cardiovascular disease characterized by the thickening and hardening of arterial walls due to the buildup of lipid (fat) and cholesterol plaques. While rarely a primary clinical concern in commercial broilers due to their short lifespan, it is increasingly seen in older breeder flocks, laying hens, and turkeys.
clinicalSigns: Often entirely subclinical until a fatal cardiovascular event occurs
Sudden death in otherwise healthy-looking older birds due to aortic rupture or heart failure
In chronic, progressive cases: lethargy, generalized weakness, and reluctance to exercise
Mild cyanosis (purplish comb) or panting when stressed due to compromised blood flow
transmission: Non-contagious. Primarily a metabolic and age-related degenerative disease.
Exacerbated by high-energy, high-fat diets, lack of exercise, and genetic predisposition.
diagnosis: Typically diagnosed post-mortem
Gross necropsy revealing thickened, rigid, and yellowish walls of the aorta, brachiocephalic arteries, or coronary arteries
Histopathology showing lipid-laden macrophages and cholesterol clefts within the arterial intima
treatment: No practical or economically viable treatment exists for poultry.
Management must focus entirely on prevention.
control: Implementing strict feed restriction programs and body weight management for broiler breeders to prevent obesity
Formulating diets with balanced lipid profiles, potentially increasing omega-3 fatty acids and reducing saturated fats
Promoting physical activity in housing systems
vaccineRecommendations: No vaccines available.

diseases.name: Ascites Syndrome
diseases.description: Ascites Syndrome (Pulmonary Hypertension Syndrome) is a major metabolic and cardiovascular disease of fast-growing broilers. It occurs when the bird's rapid muscle growth outpaces the oxygen-supplying capacity of its relatively small lungs and heart. This hypoxia triggers pulmonary hypertension, right ventricular heart failure, and massive fluid leakage into the abdomen.
clinicalSigns: Distended, tight, and visibly enlarged abdomen ('waterbelly')
Severe respiratory distress, panting, and gasping even at normal temperatures
Pronounced cyanosis (dark purple discoloration) of the comb, wattles, and skin
Lethargy, reluctance to move, and stunted growth in the later stages
High flock mortality, often peaking around 4 to 5 weeks of age
transmission: Non-contagious. It is a metabolic failure driven by genetics, rapid growth rates, and environmental stressors.
Exacerbated by poor ventilation (high ammonia/dust), low temperatures requiring high energy for heat, or high altitudes (low oxygen tension).
diagnosis: Clinical observation of 'waterbelly' and cyanosis in fast-growing broilers
Necropsy reveals large volumes of clear, amber-colored fluid in the abdominal cavity
The heart is severely enlarged, particularly the right ventricle, which will be dilated, flabby, and thin-walled
Liver may be swollen and congested
treatment: No medical treatment can reverse the heart failure or fluid accumulation once it has occurred.
Humane culling of clinically affected birds is required.
control: Slowing the early growth rate (days 7-21) using strict lighting programs or feed restriction to allow cardiovascular development to catch up
Optimizing house ventilation to ensure maximum oxygen availability and minimum ammonia levels
Maintaining proper brooding temperatures to prevent cold stress, which drastically increases oxygen demand
vaccineRecommendations: No vaccines exist for this metabolic syndrome.

diseases.name: Omphalitis (Yolk Sac Infection)
diseases.description: Omphalitis, or yolk sac infection, is a prominent infectious condition of neonatal poultry characterized by inflammation of the navel and unabsorbed yolk sac. It is primarily caused by opportunistic environmental bacteria (such as E. coli, Staphylococcus, or Pseudomonas) invading through an unhealed navel, leading to severe early chick mortality.
clinicalSigns: Enlarged, mushy, or discolored abdomen in young chicks
Unhealed, inflamed, or scabbed navel
Severe lethargy, huddling near heat sources, and drooping wings
Pasty vents and foul-smelling droppings
High mortality within the first 3 to 7 days of life
transmission: Non-contagious from chick to chick
Infection occurs in the hatchery or immediately upon placement via environmental contamination of the unhealed navel
Strongly associated with poor hatchery sanitation, improper incubation temperatures, and highly contaminated breeder eggs
diagnosis: Clinical presentation of early neonatal mortality and mushy abdomens
Necropsy reveals an enlarged, unabsorbed yolk sac containing putrid, discolored, or caseous fluid
Bacterial culture of the yolk sac content for specific pathogen identification
treatment: Treatment is generally unrewarding due to the severe systemic nature of the infection in a fragile neonate.
Flock-level antibiotic treatment via drinking water may be utilized based on antimicrobial susceptibility, but culling severely affected chicks is standard.
control: Rigorous sanitation and fumigation of hatching eggs immediately after collection
Strict hatchery hygiene, optimizing incubator temperature and humidity to ensure proper navel closure
Rapidly discarding heavily soiled or floor eggs
vaccineRecommendations: No direct vaccines exist for Omphalitis, though vaccinating breeders against E. coli can provide some maternal immunity.

diseases.name: Runting-Stunting Syndrome
diseases.description: Runting-Stunting Syndrome (RSS), or Malabsorption Syndrome, is a complex enteric disease of young broilers primarily associated with Astroviruses and Reoviruses. It causes severe damage to the intestinal villi, resulting in massive maldigestion, poor nutrient absorption, and severely compromised flock uniformity.
clinicalSigns: Extreme lack of flock uniformity with a high percentage of severely stunted birds
Pale, watery diarrhea frequently containing undigested feed particles
Poor feathering (helicopter disease) and pale shanks
Enlarged, pendulous abdomens due to gas and fluid accumulation in the gut
Generally low mortality but devastating economic impact due to poor feed conversion
transmission: Highly contagious horizontal transmission via the fecal-oral route
The viral agents are environmentally robust and persist in the litter and poultry house environment across consecutive flocks
diagnosis: Flock presentation of severe stunting and poor uniformity starting around 1-2 weeks of age
Necropsy revealing pale, dilated intestines with thin, transparent walls and undigested feed
Pancreatic atrophy and cystic lesions may be observed
Histopathology confirming villous atrophy in the small intestine
treatment: No specific antiviral treatment is available.
Supportive care through the addition of easily digestible nutrients, vitamins, and electrolytes to the water.
control: Complete clean-out of contaminated litter and aggressive disinfection protocols between flocks
Extended downtime to break the viral cycle
Optimization of early brooding temperatures to reduce stress
vaccineRecommendations: No highly effective commercial vaccines are available for the diverse Astrovirus strains; Reovirus vaccination in breeders provides some maternal protection.

diseases.name: Inclusion Body Hepatitis
diseases.description: Inclusion Body Hepatitis (IBH) is an acute viral disease of young poultry caused by Fowl Adenoviruses (FAdV). It is characterized by sudden, sharp spikes in mortality and massive hepatic necrosis. The disease is often exacerbated by concurrent immunosuppression from IBD or CAV.
clinicalSigns: Sudden, sharp onset of mortality often peaking within 3-5 days in broilers aged 2 to 5 weeks
Affected birds appear severely depressed, huddled, and lethargic just hours prior to death
Pale comb and wattles, with general weakness
Ruffled feathers and anorexia
Mortality can range from 2% to over 30% in severe outbreaks
transmission: Vertical transmission from infected breeder hens to progeny is the primary initiation route
Rapid horizontal transmission within the flock via the fecal-oral route
FAdV is highly resistant to many disinfectants and persists in the environment
diagnosis: Gross necropsy reveals a markedly enlarged, pale yellow, friable liver with extensive petechial or ecchymotic hemorrhages
Kidneys are typically enlarged and pale
Histopathology confirms massive hepatic necrosis and characteristic intranuclear inclusion bodies in hepatocytes
PCR to identify specific FAdV serotypes for vaccination planning
treatment: No specific treatment exists.
Supportive therapy utilizing liver tonics, vitamins, and reducing feed protein levels may help alleviate metabolic stress on the liver.
control: Rigorous control of immunosuppressive agents (IBD, CAV, Marek's) to prevent clinical manifestation of adenoviruses
Strict biosecurity and excellent hatchery hygiene
vaccineRecommendations: Inactivated multivalent FAdV vaccines are administered to breeder flocks to provide robust maternal antibodies to progeny, breaking the cycle of vertical transmission.

diseases.name: Egg Drop Syndrome
diseases.description: Egg Drop Syndrome (EDS '76) is a viral disease caused by a Duck Adenovirus. It uniquely targets the shell gland (uterus) of mature laying hens, causing a precipitous drop in egg production and profound deterioration of eggshell quality, without causing significant systemic illness or mortality in the birds themselves.
clinicalSigns: Sudden, dramatic drop in egg production (10% to 40%) in apparently healthy flocks
Production of pale eggs (loss of pigment in brown eggs)
Appearance of thin-shelled, soft-shelled, or completely shell-less eggs
Transient, mild diarrhea just prior to the drop in production
Failure of flocks to reach peak production if infected early
transmission: Vertical transmission through the egg; the virus remains latent until the hen reaches sexual maturity and begins laying
Horizontal transmission occurs slowly via contact with contaminated eggs, feces, and shared water
Waterfowl serve as natural asymptomatic reservoirs
diagnosis: Clinical presentation of perfectly healthy birds producing severely defective eggs
Hemagglutination Inhibition (HI) or ELISA testing demonstrating a sharp rise in specific antibodies
PCR detection of viral DNA from the shell gland or cloacal swabs
treatment: No specific treatment is available.
Flocks usually return to normal production levels within 4 to 10 weeks, although some individual birds may never recover normal shell quality.
control: Strict separation of commercial poultry from all waterfowl
Ensuring clean, chlorinated drinking water to prevent horizontal spread via shared water sources
Thorough cleaning and disinfection of egg trays and equipment
vaccineRecommendations: Inactivated oil-emulsion EDS vaccines are highly effective and are routinely administered via injection to pullets and breeders at 14-18 weeks of age prior to the onset of lay.

diseases.name: Turkey Coryza (Bordetellosis)
diseases.description: Turkey Coryza, or Bordetellosis, is an acute, highly contagious respiratory disease of young turkeys caused by Bordetella avium. The bacterium colonizes the ciliated epithelium of the trachea, paralyzing the mucociliary escalator and causing severe upper respiratory signs. It frequently predisposes the flock to devastating secondary E. coli infections.
clinicalSigns: Clear, frothy nasal and ocular discharge leading to crusted nares
Frequent sneezing, coughing, and tracheal rales
Repeated head shaking and wiping the beak on the wings (leaving soiled feathers)
Swelling of the submaxillary area and sinuses
Change in vocalization, progressing to a loss of voice in turkeys
transmission: Direct bird-to-bird contact and inhalation of infectious respiratory aerosols
Contaminated drinking water is a highly effective route of rapid horizontal spread
The bacterium can survive for weeks in wet litter and water lines
diagnosis: Bacterial culture and isolation of Bordetella avium from tracheal swabs or exudate using specific media (MacConkey agar)
PCR detection for rapid and highly sensitive confirmation
Gross pathology showing collapsed, flattened tracheal rings with heavy mucus accumulation
treatment: Bordetella avium is notoriously resistant to most antibiotics in vivo because it resides within the protective mucus layer of the trachea.
Antibiotics (e.g., penicillins, tetracyclines) are primarily administered to control secondary E. coli septicemia.
control: Stringent water line sanitation and chlorination
Optimizing ventilation to reduce environmental ammonia, which exacerbates mucosal damage
Thorough cleaning and prolonged downtime between flocks
vaccineRecommendations: Live attenuated temperature-sensitive vaccines can be administered via spray or drinking water to day-old poults, followed by a booster.

diseases.name: Cannibalism
diseases.description: Cannibalism is a severe behavioral anomaly driven by management and environmental stressors, rather than an infectious agent. It involves aggressive feather pecking, vent pecking, or tissue tearing among flock members. Once blood is drawn, the behavior spreads rapidly, causing high mortality and significant welfare concerns.
clinicalSigns: Severe, bloody wounds primarily located around the vent, tail base, back, and toes
Significant patches of missing or aggressively plucked feathers
Evisceration of the abdominal cavity stemming from vent pecking (often following a minor vent prolapse)
Highly nervous, hyperactive, and aggressive flock behavior
Sudden increases in flock mortality due to blood loss or secondary infections
transmission: Non-infectious. It is a socially transmitted behavioral habit.
Triggered by high stocking density, excessive light intensity, heat stress, inadequate feeder/drinker space, or nutritional deficiencies (particularly sodium or specific amino acids).
diagnosis: Direct observation of aggressive pecking behavior within the flock
Necropsy reveals severe external trauma, missing feathers, and often evisceration, while internal organs remain largely healthy
treatment: Immediate removal and isolation of injured birds
Application of anti-peck sprays or wound-healing tar to affected individuals
Immediate reduction of light intensity inside the poultry house to calm the flock
control: Proper beak trimming/treatment at the hatchery utilizing infrared technology
Optimizing stocking density and ensuring equitable access to feed and water
Providing environmental enrichments (e.g., pecking blocks) and ensuring diets are nutritionally balanced, particularly for salt and methionine.
vaccineRecommendations: No vaccines apply to behavioral/management issues.

diseases.name: Heat Stress
diseases.description: Heat stress occurs when high environmental temperatures and humidity overwhelm the bird's ability to dissipate core body heat. Because poultry lack sweat glands, they rely heavily on panting (evaporative cooling), which alters blood pH (respiratory alkalosis) and can rapidly lead to cardiovascular collapse and massive acute mortality.
clinicalSigns: Severe panting with open beaks and rapid respiratory rates
Wings held away from the body (drooping) to expose poorly feathered areas and dissipate heat
Severe lethargy, reluctance to move, and significantly reduced feed intake
Increased water consumption leading to wet droppings
Sudden, explosive spikes in mortality, especially during the hottest parts of the day
In layers: acute drop in production and thin-shelled eggs
transmission: Non-infectious. A direct result of severe environmental challenge, facility design flaws, or ventilation failure.
diagnosis: Direct correlation with extreme environmental temperatures and high humidity indexes
Necropsy of acute mortalities shows severely congested, dark breast muscles and generalized venous congestion
Absence of infectious disease markers
treatment: Immediate emergency interventions to lower ambient temperature: activating cooling pads, foggers, and maximizing tunnel ventilation
Providing cool, fresh drinking water supplemented with electrolytes and Vitamin C to combat respiratory alkalosis
Withdrawing feed during the hottest hours to reduce metabolic heat production
control: Ensuring optimal design and maintenance of tunnel ventilation and evaporative cooling systems
Adjusting stocking densities during hot seasons
Implementing midnight feeding or early morning feeding programs
vaccineRecommendations: Not applicable.

diseases.name: Botulism
diseases.description: Botulism, frequently termed 'limberneck', is a rapidly fatal, paralytic toxicosis caused by the ingestion of preformed neurotoxins produced by the anaerobic bacterium Clostridium botulinum. The toxin irreversibly blocks acetylcholine release at the neuromuscular junction, leading to flaccid paralysis.
clinicalSigns: Classic 'limberneck' presentation: complete flaccid paralysis of the neck muscles, causing the head to rest on the ground
Progressive paralysis starting in the legs and wings, leading to complete immobility
Feathers appear loose and easily pulled from the follicles
Eyes remain partially or completely closed
Death by asphyxiation due to respiratory muscle paralysis
transmission: Ingestion of the highly potent toxin from decaying organic matter, stagnant water, or dead carcasses (maggots feeding on toxic carcasses concentrate the toxin and are eagerly eaten by birds)
It is an intoxication, not a contagious infection
diagnosis: Highly characteristic clinical signs of flaccid paralysis without gross internal lesions
Definitive diagnosis requires detection of the botulinum toxin in the serum, crop contents, or tissues of affected birds via mouse bioassay or ELISA
treatment: There is no practical or approved treatment for large commercial flocks.
Supportive care and administration of botulinum antitoxin may be attempted in highly valuable individual birds, but success is limited once signs are severe.
control: Daily removal and deep burial or incineration of dead birds to prevent the flock from consuming toxic maggots
Elimination of stagnant water, decaying vegetation, and spoiled feed from the environment
Ensuring clean, chlorinated drinking water
vaccineRecommendations: Toxoid vaccines exist for specific high-risk operations (like valuable pheasant breeding), but are rarely used in commercial chickens.

diseases.name: Aflatoxicosis
diseases.description: Aflatoxicosis is an insidious toxicological disease caused by the ingestion of aflatoxins, highly potent hepatotoxins produced by Aspergillus flavus and A. parasiticus growing on feed grains. It causes severe liver damage, systemic immunosuppression, and massive economic losses due to compromised growth and vaccine failures.
clinicalSigns: Severe stunting, poor feed conversion, and lack of uniformity
Pale, icteric (jaundiced) comb and wattles
Increased susceptibility to bruising and subcutaneous hemorrhages due to impaired blood clotting
General lethargy, weakness, and unthriftiness
Significant drops in egg production and hatchability, alongside immunosuppression leading to secondary disease outbreaks
transmission: Strictly an intoxication via ingestion of mold-contaminated feed (often corn or peanuts).
Toxins can accumulate in storage silos under hot, humid conditions.
diagnosis: Necropsy reveals an enlarged, pale, friable, or yellowish liver (fatty change), with petechial hemorrhages and an enlarged gall bladder
Definitive diagnosis requires quantitative chemical analysis (HPLC or ELISA) of representative feed samples to confirm high aflatoxin levels
treatment: Immediate removal and safe disposal of the contaminated feed source
Supplementation of the new feed or water with elevated levels of vitamins (especially Vitamin K for blood clotting and Vitamin E) and lipotropic agents to support liver recovery
control: Rigorous quality control and screening of all incoming feed grains using rapid mycotoxin test kits
Maintaining low moisture levels in feed storage silos and bins
Routine inclusion of high-quality, broad-spectrum mycotoxin binders (e.g., aluminosilicates or yeast cell wall extracts) in the feed formulation
vaccineRecommendations: No vaccines available. Toxin ingestion actually heavily suppresses the efficacy of standard vaccination programs.

diseases.name: Salt Toxicity
diseases.description: Salt Toxicity, or Sodium Ion Toxicosis, occurs from consuming excessive sodium in the feed or water, or conversely, from severe water deprivation followed by sudden access to unlimited water. It causes catastrophic osmotic imbalances, leading to severe brain edema, neurological signs, and acute mortality.
clinicalSigns: Extreme thirst and excessive water consumption (if available)
Profuse, watery diarrhea and severely wet litter
Pronounced neurological signs: incoordination, staggering, and muscular weakness
Convulsions, seizures, and eventual coma
Sudden and widespread mortality across the flock
transmission: Non-infectious. Caused by errors in feed formulation, malfunction of water medicators, naturally highly saline well water, or severe mechanical failure of the drinking system.
diagnosis: Clinical presentation of explosive diarrhea and neurological signs alongside severe wet litter
Necropsy reveals severe edema (swelling) of the brain and generalized visceral congestion
Confirmation via analysis of sodium levels in the feed, drinking water, and brain tissue
treatment: If toxicity is due to feed or medicator errors: Immediate removal of the toxic source and provision of fresh, clean water
If toxicity is due to prolonged water deprivation: Water must be reintroduced gradually in small increments over several hours to prevent acute brain edema and fatal osmotic shock.
control: Strict quality control and verification of salt inclusion rates during feed milling
Routine testing of well water sources for mineral and sodium content
Ensuring constant maintenance and monitoring of water lines and medication dosers to prevent water deprivation
vaccineRecommendations: Not applicable.

diseases.name: Chicken Infectious Anemia (CAV)
diseases.description: Chicken Infectious Anemia is an acute viral disease of young chickens caused by a Gyrovirus (CAV). It is characterized by severe aplastic anemia, lymphoid depletion, and profound immunosuppression. Affected birds are highly susceptible to secondary infections, particularly Gangrenous Dermatitis and Inclusion Body Hepatitis.
clinicalSigns: Severe pallor (paleness) of the comb, wattles, and facial skin due to profound anemia
Lethargy, depression, and anorexia
Subcutaneous hemorrhages and blue wing disease (often complicated by secondary bacteria)
Stunted growth and poor flock uniformity
Spikes in mortality typically occurring around 10 to 24 days of age
transmission: Vertical transmission from infected, seronegative breeder hens to their progeny through the egg is the most clinically significant route
Horizontal transmission via the fecal-oral route or inhalation of contaminated dander
The virus is highly resistant to heat and most common disinfectants
diagnosis: Clinical signs of severe anemia in young chicks (hematocrit often <20%)
Gross necropsy revealing pale bone marrow (fatty and yellow instead of red), severe atrophy of the thymus, and hemorrhages in the mucosa
PCR detection of viral DNA from the thymus or bone marrow
ELISA to monitor breeder flock seroconversion
treatment: No specific antiviral treatment exists.
Broad-spectrum antibiotics may be administered to control devastating secondary bacterial infections.
Optimal management to reduce environmental stress.
control: The primary control method is ensuring that breeder flocks seroconvert (develop antibodies) prior to the onset of egg production to prevent vertical transmission and provide maternal immunity to chicks.
Maintaining strict biosecurity and reducing concurrent immunosuppressive diseases (like IBD).
vaccineRecommendations: Live attenuated CAV vaccines are routinely administered via drinking water or wing-web injection to breeder pullets between 10 and 15 weeks of age.

diseases.name: Ulcerative Enteritis (Quail Disease)
diseases.description: Ulcerative Enteritis is an acute, highly fatal bacterial infection primarily affecting quail, but also chickens and turkeys. It is caused by Clostridium colinum. In chickens, it typically occurs as a secondary complication to coccidiosis, IBD, or severe stress, leading to deep mucosal ulcers and liver necrosis.
clinicalSigns: Sudden onset of high mortality, often without prior clinical signs in acute outbreaks
Profuse, watery, and sometimes bloody diarrhea
Severe lethargy, huddling with half-closed eyes, and ruffled feathers
Extreme emaciation in birds that survive the acute phase
transmission: Fecal-oral route through ingestion of highly resilient bacterial spores from contaminated litter, feed, or water
Mechanical transmission by flies and carrier birds
Spores can persist in the environment for months to years
diagnosis: Gross necropsy reveals pathognomonic deep, button-like ulcers in the lower small intestine and ceca, often leading to perforation and peritonitis
Liver may show diffuse yellow necrotic spots or large areas of necrosis
Isolation of C. colinum under strict anaerobic conditions
treatment: Immediate administration of antibiotics such as bacitracin, streptomycin, or penicillins via drinking water
Treatment must be aggressive and rapid to halt mortality
control: Effective prevention and control of predisposing factors, particularly coccidiosis
Raising birds on wire floors (especially quail) to break the fecal-oral cycle
Thorough clean-out, disinfection, and downtime between flocks
vaccineRecommendations: No commercial vaccines are available. Autogenous bacterins are rarely used due to variable efficacy.

diseases.name: Avian Intestinal Spirochetosis
diseases.description: Avian Intestinal Spirochetosis (AIS) is a gastrointestinal disease of laying hens and broiler breeders caused by anaerobic spirochetes of the genus Brachyspira (e.g., B. pilosicoli, B. intermedia). It colonizes the ceca and colon, causing chronic diarrhea, delayed onset of lay, and prolonged drops in egg production.
clinicalSigns: Chronic, frothy, yellowish to caramel-colored diarrhea
Fecal staining of eggshells and feathers around the vent
Delayed onset of lay or a 5-10% sustained drop in egg production
Poor feed conversion, mild weight loss, and uneven flock body weights
Increased moisture in the litter leading to secondary ammonia issues
transmission: Fecal-oral route via ingestion of contaminated feces, water, or feed
Wild birds, rodents, and insects (flies) can act as mechanical vectors and biological reservoirs
diagnosis: Clinical signs of frothy droppings and dirty eggs in adult flocks
Microscopic examination of fresh cecal smears using phase-contrast microscopy or silver stains to visualize motile spirochetes
Anaerobic culture on specialized selective media
PCR to differentiate pathogenic Brachyspira species from non-pathogenic ones
treatment: Administration of antibiotics such as tiamulin or lincomycin via drinking water
Relapses are common after antibiotic withdrawal if environmental contamination remains high
control: Strict biosecurity to exclude wild birds and rodents
Rigorous fly control programs
Thorough cleaning and chemical disinfection (spirochetes are susceptible to common disinfectants but protected by organic matter)
vaccineRecommendations: No commercial vaccines are currently available for AIS.

diseases.name: Spotty Liver Disease
diseases.description: Spotty Liver Disease (SLD) is an emerging bacterial disease primarily affecting free-range laying hens, caused by Campylobacter hepaticus. It is characterized by acute drops in egg production and increased flock mortality during the peak lay period, with pathognomonic lesions on the liver.
clinicalSigns: Sudden drop in egg production, often falling by 10% to 25% within a few days
Acute, sporadic mortality in otherwise healthy-looking hens
Mild lethargy, pale combs, and reduced feed intake
Clinical signs often coincide with environmental stressors (e.g., hot/humid weather, diet changes)
transmission: Fecal-oral transmission is the primary route, heavily associated with exposure to contaminated soil, feces, and water in free-range environments
Rodents and wild birds may act as vectors
diagnosis: Gross necropsy is highly distinctive: the liver is enlarged and covered in 1-2 mm greyish-white miliary necrotic spots (focal hepatic necrosis)
Bacterial culture of C. hepaticus from liver or bile requires specific microaerophilic conditions and prolonged incubation
PCR of liver tissue or bile for rapid and definitive confirmation
treatment: Antibiotics such as chlortetracycline, lincomycin, or tilmicosin administered in water or feed often provide rapid clinical improvement.
However, recrudescence (relapse) of the disease is very common once antibiotic therapy ceases.
control: Improving range management and drainage to eliminate wet areas
Rigorous biosecurity and rodent control
Optimizing gut health through organic acids or probiotics to reduce pathogen colonization in the ceca
vaccineRecommendations: No commercial vaccines are currently available, though experimental bacterins are under development.

diseases.name: Trichomoniasis (Canker)
diseases.description: Trichomoniasis, commonly called Canker (in pigeons) or Frounce (in raptors), is an upper digestive tract disease caused by the flagellated protozoan Trichomonas gallinae. While primarily a disease of pigeons and doves, it occasionally spills over into free-range poultry or backyard flocks, causing severe caseous lesions in the mouth and crop.
clinicalSigns: Difficulty swallowing and severe reduction in feed intake
Accumulation of thick, yellow, caseous (cheesy) necrotic plaques in the mouth, pharynx, esophagus, and crop
Foul odor from the beak
Progressive weight loss and emaciation
Gasping or respiratory distress if the lesions block the glottis
transmission: Direct transmission via shared drinking water or feed contaminated with the oral secretions of infected wild birds (especially pigeons and doves)
The protozoa cannot survive long in dry environments but persist well in stagnant water
diagnosis: Visual observation of the characteristic yellow diphtheritic lesions in the oral cavity
Microscopic examination of a warm saline wet mount of a lesion scraping to visualize the highly motile, flagellated protozoa
Must be differentiated from Fowl Pox (wet form) and Candidiasis
treatment: Nitroimidazole drugs (e.g., ronidazole, metronidazole, carnidazole) are highly effective.
Note: These drugs are strictly banned for use in food-producing poultry in many countries. Affected birds in commercial settings are typically culled.
control: Absolute exclusion of wild pigeons, doves, and their droppings/secretions from poultry feed and water sources
Frequent cleaning, disinfection, and chlorination of drinking water systems
Eliminating standing water puddles in free-range environments
vaccineRecommendations: No vaccines are available for Trichomoniasis.

diseases.name: Ammonia Toxicity (Keratoconjunctivitis)
diseases.description: Ammonia Toxicity, specifically causing Ammonia Blindness (Keratoconjunctivitis), is an environmental management issue. High levels of atmospheric ammonia (gas) released from wet litter dissolve in the moisture of the eyes and respiratory tract, causing severe caustic burns, inflammation, and predisposing birds to respiratory pathogens.
clinicalSigns: Photophobia (sensitivity to light) leading birds to keep eyes closed
Severe tearing and frothy ocular discharge
Rubbing of the eyes against the wings, causing soiled feathers
Cloudiness or ulceration of the cornea, progressing to blindness in severe cases
Reduced feed intake and stunted growth due to pain and inability to find feeders
transmission: Non-infectious. It is entirely dependent on the housing environment and litter management.
Ammonia levels exceeding 25-50 ppm consistently cause mucosal damage.
diagnosis: Detection of strong ammonia odors upon entering the poultry house (human threshold is around 20 ppm)
Clinical signs of conjunctivitis and corneal ulceration in a large portion of the flock
Absence of other specific respiratory pathogens (though secondary E. coli infections are common)
treatment: Immediate correction of ventilation to exhaust ammonia gas and bring in fresh air
Addition of fresh, dry bedding over wet areas
Flock usually recovers if corneal ulceration has not progressed to permanent scarring
control: Optimizing ventilation rates, especially during winter months when houses are closed tightly to conserve heat
Proper drinker management to prevent leaks and maintain dry litter
Use of litter amendments (acidifiers like sodium bisulfate) to lower litter pH and inhibit the bacterial conversion of uric acid into ammonia gas
vaccineRecommendations: Not applicable.

diseases.name: Ochratoxicosis
diseases.description: Ochratoxicosis is a severe mycotoxin poisoning caused by the ingestion of Ochratoxin A, produced primarily by Aspergillus ochraceus and Penicillium verrucosum. It is an extremely potent nephrotoxin (kidney toxin) in poultry, far more toxic than aflatoxin, leading to renal failure, immunosuppression, and poor performance.
clinicalSigns: Severe, persistent watery diarrhea leading to very wet litter
Significant increase in water consumption (polydipsia)
Profound depression, ruffled feathers, and severe stunting of growth
Decreased egg production and poor eggshell quality
