High mortality in severe toxicosis due to acute renal failure and visceral gout
transmission: Ingestion of contaminated feed ingredients (especially grains stored under cool, damp conditions)
It is an intoxication, not an infectious disease.
diagnosis: Necropsy reveals massively enlarged, pale, and swollen kidneys
Visceral gout (urate deposition on internal organs) may be present due to kidney failure
Definitive diagnosis requires quantitative chemical analysis of the feed for Ochratoxin A
treatment: Immediate removal of the toxic feed
Providing ample fresh water, electrolytes, and Vitamin C to support kidney flushing and reduce oxidative stress
Adding urine acidifiers may help mitigate urate deposition
control: Stringent quality control of feed ingredients, particularly assessing moisture content during storage
Use of broad-spectrum mycotoxin binders in the feed; however, Ochratoxin A is notoriously difficult to bind effectively compared to aflatoxin
vaccineRecommendations: No vaccines available.

diseases.name: T-2 Toxicosis
diseases.description: T-2 Toxicosis is caused by the ingestion of T-2 toxin (a trichothecene mycotoxin) produced by Fusarium fungi. It is a highly caustic compound that causes severe localized tissue necrosis on contact (particularly in the mouth) and systemic immunosuppression, heavily impacting feed intake and growth.
clinicalSigns: Pathognomonic severe, crusty, yellowish-white necrotic lesions on the edges of the beak, hard palate, tongue, and corners of the mouth
Extreme reduction in feed consumption due to oral pain
Abnormal feathering, poor plumage, and feather loss
Stunted growth and poor weight gain
Thin-shelled eggs and drops in production in layer flocks
transmission: Ingestion of contaminated feed (Fusarium typically infects grains like corn and wheat in the field during cool, wet weather prior to harvest)
diagnosis: Visual observation of characteristic oral lesions (must be differentiated from Fowl Pox and Candidiasis)
History of rapid onset following a new batch of feed
Chemical analysis of feed for T-2 and related trichothecenes
treatment: Immediate replacement of the contaminated feed
Supplementation with vitamins and supportive care to assist in mucosal healing
Oral lesions generally resolve within one to two weeks after the toxin is removed
control: Screening of feed grains, especially those harvested during unseasonably wet, cool periods

Inclusion of effective mycotoxin deactivators or binders in the feed (note: standard aluminosilicate clay binders are often ineffective against T-2; enzymatic deactivators or specific yeast-wall extracts are required)
vaccineRecommendations: No vaccines available.

diseases.name: Transmissible Viral Proventriculitis
diseases.description: Transmissible Viral Proventriculitis (TVP) is an infectious disease of broilers primarily caused by Chicken Proventricular Necrosis Virus (CPNV), a birnavirus. It causes severe inflammation and necrosis of the glandular stomach (proventriculus), severely impairing digestion, increasing feed conversion ratios, and causing significant issues during processing due to easily ruptured intestines.
clinicalSigns: Poor growth, stunting, and severe lack of flock uniformity
Passage of poorly digested feed in the feces
General unthriftiness and mild lethargy
Increased processing plant condemnations due to fragile intestines tearing and contaminating carcasses with ingesta
Typically low mortality but high economic impact
transmission: Horizontal transmission via the fecal-oral route
Highly infectious within a flock, spreading rapidly
diagnosis: Gross necropsy reveals a markedly enlarged, swollen, and pale proventriculus with thickened walls
The glandular papillae inside the proventriculus are flattened, necrotic, or hemorrhagic
Histopathology showing necrosis of the oxynticopeptic cells and lymphocytic infiltration
RT-PCR detection of CPNV
treatment: No specific antiviral treatments exist.
Supportive care includes optimizing feed formulation (easier to digest proteins) and excellent water sanitation to prevent secondary bacterial overgrowth in the compromised gut.
control: Strict biosecurity and aggressive cleaning and disinfection of houses between flocks
Extended downtime to reduce viral load in the environment
vaccineRecommendations: No commercial vaccines are currently available.

diseases.name: Coronaviral Enteritis (Bluecomb Disease)
diseases.description: Coronaviral Enteritis, historically known as Bluecomb Disease, is an acute, highly infectious disease primarily affecting turkeys, caused by the Turkey Coronavirus (TCV). It leads to severe enteritis, dramatic loss of feed and water intake, and profound dehydration, causing significant mortality in young poults and severe economic losses in older birds.
clinicalSigns: Sudden onset of severe, frothy, greenish to brownish watery diarrhea
Profound depression, huddling, and almost complete cessation of eating and drinking
Severe dehydration leading to cyanosis (darkening) of the head and wattles ('bluecomb')
Rapid weight loss and emaciation
Mortality can be very high in young poults due to starvation and dehydration
transmission: Highly contagious through the fecal-oral route
Mechanical transmission by personnel, equipment, and wild birds (which may act as mechanical vectors)
Virus is shed in massive amounts in feces
diagnosis: Clinical presentation of explosive diarrhea and sudden feed refusal in turkeys
Gross necropsy revealing thin-walled, gas- and fluid-filled intestines with watery contents
Direct electron microscopy of intestinal contents, or specific RT-PCR testing for TCV
IFA or ELISA on convalescent serum
treatment: No antiviral treatment available.
Aggressive supportive therapy including warming the house and providing electrolytes and vitamins in the water to combat severe dehydration.
Antibiotics may be used to control secondary bacterial enteritis.
control: Strict biosecurity and controlled movement between turkey houses
Complete depopulation of infected flocks, followed by rigorous cleaning, disinfection, and a minimum 3-4 week downtime
Prevention of cross-contamination from older recovered flocks (which may shed virus) to young poults
vaccineRecommendations: No effective commercial vaccines are currently available. Control relies entirely on strict biosecurity and eradication.

diseases.name: Viral Arthritis (Reovirus)

symptomsEnhanced.name: Gait, Reluctance to stand or walk

diseases.description: Viral Arthritis, also known as Avian Tenosynovitis, is caused by Avian Orthoreoviruses. It primarily targets the joints and tendon sheaths of the legs in fast-growing broilers, causing severe inflammation, lameness, and occasionally actual rupture of the gastrocnemius tendon. It is also a contributor to malabsorption syndromes.
clinicalSigns: Noticeable swelling and heat in the hock joints and the tendon sheaths extending up the back of the leg
Severe lameness, reluctance to walk, and a stiff, stilted gait
Birds resting on their hocks and exhibiting poor growth due to inability to access feed
In chronic or severe cases, sudden inability to bear weight on one leg due to a ruptured Achilles tendon
Poor flock uniformity and general unthriftiness
transmission: Vertical transmission from infected breeder hens to their progeny
Horizontal transmission is highly efficient via the fecal-oral route and inhalation of contaminated dust
Reoviruses are ubiquitous and extremely resistant to environmental degradation and many disinfectants
diagnosis: Gross pathology revealing straw-colored or blood-tinged exudate in the hock joint and inflamed, thickened tendon sheaths
Fibrosis and hardening of tissues around the joint in chronic cases
Viral isolation from joint exudate or PCR testing
Must be differentiated clinically from Mycoplasma synoviae and Staphylococcal arthritis
treatment: No specific antiviral treatment is available.
Culling of severely lame birds is required for welfare reasons.
Broad-spectrum antibiotics may be used only if secondary bacterial arthritis is confirmed.
control: Rigorous cleaning and disinfection protocols, utilizing products specifically proven effective against non-enveloped viruses
Strict all-in/all-out management to prevent virus accumulation on the farm
vaccineRecommendations: A combination of live and inactivated reovirus vaccines must be administered to breeder flocks to provide robust maternal antibodies to progeny, which is the most effective prevention strategy.

diseases.name: Hemorrhagic Enteritis
diseases.description: Hemorrhagic Enteritis (HE) is an acute, fatal viral disease of young turkeys caused by Turkey Adenovirus 3 (Siadenovirus). It primarily affects poults between 4 and 12 weeks of age. The virus causes severe intestinal bleeding and profound immunosuppression, strongly predisposing survivors to secondary E. coli or Clostridial infections.
clinicalSigns: Sudden onset of high mortality in rapidly growing, healthy-looking poults
Passage of frank, bright red blood in the droppings
Birds may be found dead with blood oozing from the vent
Severe depression, pallor, and huddling in affected birds just prior to death
Transient but severe immunosuppression in the flock
transmission: Horizontal transmission via the fecal-oral route is explosive within a flock
The virus is highly stable in feces and moist litter, surviving for months or years in the environment
diagnosis: Gross necropsy is highly distinctive: the small intestine is distended and filled with massive amounts of fresh, unclotted blood
The spleen is markedly enlarged, friable, and mottled (often marbled appearance)
Histopathology reveals intranuclear inclusion bodies in the reticuloendothelial cells of the spleen and intestines
Agar gel immunodiffusion (AGID) or PCR for confirmation
treatment: No specific antiviral treatment.
Historically, administration of convalescent antiserum was used to halt outbreaks, though this is less common today.
Antibiotics are routinely administered via water to prevent secondary E. coli or clostridial septicemia in the immunosuppressed survivors.
control: Strict biosecurity and complete clean-out of litter between flocks to reduce viral buildup
Isolation of different age groups, as older birds can be asymptomatic carriers
vaccineRecommendations: Live cell-culture or splenic-derived vaccines are highly effective and routinely administered via drinking water to turkey poults at 3 to 4 weeks of age.

diseases.name: Riemerella anatipestifer Infection
diseases.description: Riemerella anatipestifer infection, historically called 'New Duck Disease', is a highly contagious, acute to chronic septicemic bacterial disease primarily devastating domestic ducks and geese, but occasionally affecting turkeys. It causes severe fibrinous polyserositis and pronounced neurological signs, leading to high mortality.
clinicalSigns: Ocular and nasal discharge
Mild coughing and sneezing
Severe neurological signs: ataxia (staggering), head tremors, and torticollis (wry neck)
Birds resting on their backs, paddling their legs
Greenish diarrhea and rapid weight loss
transmission: Respiratory route via inhalation of infectious aerosols
Through breaks in the skin, particularly footpad lesions (bumblefoot) aggravated by rough flooring
Contaminated water sources are a major vector for waterfowl
diagnosis: Gross necropsy reveals classic fibrinous exudate covering the heart (pericarditis), liver (perihepatitis), and air sacs, virtually indistinguishable from severe E. coli septicemia
Bacterial culture from blood, brain, or affected organs requires specialized media (blood or chocolate agar) in a microaerophilic environment
PCR for rapid, definitive species identification
treatment: Antibiotics such as penicillins, enrofloxacin, or ceftiofur are effective if administered early in the outbreak.
Antimicrobial susceptibility testing is crucial as multi-drug resistant strains are increasingly common.
control: Maintaining excellent litter and floor conditions to prevent footpad abrasions
Rigorous cleaning and chlorination of water sources
All-in/all-out management systems
vaccineRecommendations: Inactivated bacterins (often autogenous, matching local serotypes) are widely used and injected into breeders to protect progeny, or directly into young ducklings in highly endemic areas.

diseases.name: Leucocytozoonosis
diseases.description: Leucocytozoonosis is an infectious blood parasite disease caused by protozoa of the genus Leucocytozoon (e.g., L. caulleryi in chickens, L. smithi in turkeys). It infects blood cells and internal organs, causing severe hemolytic anemia, tissue damage, and immunosuppression, particularly impacting flocks raised outdoors near water.
clinicalSigns: Profound paleness of the comb and wattles due to severe anemia
Extreme lethargy, weakness, and anorexia
Labored breathing or gasping as red blood cell destruction reduces oxygen transport
Greenish droppings (biliverdinuria) resulting from liver damage and red blood cell breakdown
Sudden death in young, highly susceptible birds due to acute pulmonary hemorrhage
transmission: Strictly transmitted via the bite of infected insect vectors, primarily black flies (Simuliidae) and biting midges (Culicoides)
It cannot be transmitted directly from bird to bird
diagnosis: Microscopic examination of a Giemsa-stained blood smear revealing large, distinct, distorted host cells containing the gametocytes of the parasite
Gross necropsy showing extreme pallor of tissues, an enlarged spleen and liver, and pinpoint hemorrhages in the lungs and kidneys
treatment: Antimalarial/antiprotozoal drugs (e.g., sulfadimethoxine, pyrimethamine) may reduce mortality but rarely clear the infection completely.
Treatment is often impractical for large flocks and restricted by local drug regulations.
control: Rigorous vector control: eliminating standing water and managing vegetation around poultry houses to disrupt fly breeding sites
Using insect screening on poultry houses and applying approved insecticides
Keeping birds indoors during peak vector feeding times (dawn and dusk)
vaccineRecommendations: No commercial vaccines are currently available.

diseases.name: Avian Malaria
diseases.description: Avian Malaria is a mosquito-borne disease caused by protozoa of the genus Plasmodium (e.g., P. gallinaceum). It destroys red blood cells, causing varying degrees of anemia. While highly fatal in susceptible species like penguins or canaries, it generally causes mild, chronic, or subclinical disease in commercial poultry, unless the flock is heavily stressed.
clinicalSigns: Paleness of the comb and wattles (anemia)
General lethargy, weakness, and reluctance to move
Loss of appetite and progressive weight loss
Drops in egg production
In severe cases, neurological signs or respiratory distress due to clogged capillaries (exoerythrocytic schizonts in the brain or lungs)
transmission: Transmitted exclusively through the bite of infected mosquitoes (primarily Culex, Aedes, or Anopheles species)
Cannot spread directly from bird to bird
diagnosis: Microscopic examination of Giemsa-stained blood smears to detect intraerythrocytic stages (trophozoites, schizonts, or gametocytes) which displace the host cell nucleus
Gross pathology revealing a markedly enlarged, dark, or melanin-pigmented liver and spleen
PCR testing for definitive species identification
treatment: Antimalarial drugs (such as chloroquine or primaquine) can be effective but are rarely economical or approved for use in commercial food-producing poultry.
Treatment is mostly reserved for valuable breeder, zoo, or exhibition birds.
control: Aggressive mosquito control around the farm premises (eliminating standing water, applying larvicides)
Securing houses with fine mesh screens in highly endemic areas
Routine application of approved insect repellents or premise sprays
vaccineRecommendations: No vaccines are available for Avian Malaria.

diseases.name: Footpad Dermatitis

symptomsEnhanced.name: Gait, Reluctance to stand or walk

diseases.description: Footpad Dermatitis (FPD), or contact dermatitis, is a condition characterized by necrotic lesions on the plantar surface of the footpads. Unlike Bumblefoot (which is a deep bacterial infection), FPD is primarily a chemical burn and contact erosion caused by prolonged exposure to wet, poor-quality litter containing high levels of ammonia and uric acid. It is a major animal welfare and economic indicator in broilers.
clinicalSigns: Discoloration (darkening) and hyperkeratosis (thickening) of the skin on the central footpad and toes
Development of erosions, cracks, and hard, black necrotic scabs on the footpads
Reluctance to move, altered gait, and resting frequently to avoid bearing weight
Reduced feed intake resulting in lower body weights and poor flock uniformity
Increased downgrades of paws at the processing plant
transmission: Non-infectious. Completely dependent on environmental conditions, specifically litter moisture and chemistry.
diagnosis: Visual scoring of the footpads during routine flock checks or at the processing plant
Lesions are superficial but can become deep ulcers if ignored
Must be differentiated from Biotin deficiency (which usually includes beak lesions) and true Bumblefoot (S. aureus infection)
treatment: Topical treatments are impractical for large flocks.
Immediate remediation of the environment by adding fresh, dry bedding over wet spots and drastically improving ventilation.
control: Rigorous maintenance of drinker lines to prevent leaks and spills
Optimizing ventilation to remove moisture from the house, especially during winter
Formulating diets to prevent loose, wet droppings (e.g., balancing sodium/potassium levels and using highly digestible proteins)
Use of litter amendments to control ammonia release
vaccineRecommendations: Not applicable.

diseases.name: Gizzard Erosion
diseases.description: Gizzard Erosion and Ulceration Syndrome is characterized by the degradation, cracking, and bleeding of the koilin layer (the tough inner lining) of the gizzard. It severely impairs mechanical digestion. While Fowl Adenoviruses can cause it, a major non-infectious cause is Gizzerosine toxicity (a biogenic amine formed in overheated fishmeal) or severe mycotoxin exposure.
clinicalSigns: Poor feed conversion, stunted growth, and uneven flock sizes
Vomiting or regurgitation of dark, blood-stained fluid in severe cases
Passage of undigested feed in the feces
Pale comb due to internal blood loss from gizzard ulcers
Reduced feed intake due to digestive pain
transmission: Toxicological forms are non-contagious, resulting from ingestion of Gizzerosine, copper sulfate overdose, or mycotoxins.
Viral forms (Fowl Adenovirus serotype 1) can be transmitted vertically or horizontally.
diagnosis: Gross necropsy is diagnostic: cutting open the gizzard reveals severe roughening, black/brown discoloration, fissures, and deep bleeding ulcers in the yellow koilin lining
Feed analysis for Gizzerosine (if high fishmeal diets are used) or mycotoxin screening
PCR for Adenovirus if a viral etiology is suspected
treatment: Immediate removal of the suspected toxic feed
Supportive care with vitamins (especially Vitamin K to mitigate bleeding) and electrolytes
Adding copper sulfate to water is strictly contraindicated as it will worsen the condition
control: Rigorous quality control of feed ingredients, particularly fishmeal, ensuring it has not been overheated during processing
Strict monitoring of mycotoxin levels in feed grains
Adenovirus control in breeders if the viral etiology is prevalent
vaccineRecommendations: No vaccines available for the toxicological causes. FAdV vaccines in breeders can prevent viral forms.

diseases.name: Cecal Worm Infection (Heterakiasis)
diseases.description: Heterakiasis is caused by the cecal worm Heterakis gallinarum. While the worm itself generally causes only mild inflammation in the ceca and slight unthriftiness, it is critically important because its eggs serve as the essential mechanical vector and reservoir for Histomonas meleagridis, the devastating protozoan that causes Blackhead Disease.
clinicalSigns: Often entirely subclinical or asymptomatic in chickens
Heavy infestations may cause mild diarrhea, unthriftiness, and slight weight loss
Small, white, thread-like worms (up to 1.5 cm) may be visible in the droppings
If carrying Histomonas, clinical signs of Blackhead Disease (yellow diarrhea, cyanosis) will rapidly supersede the mild worm signs
transmission: Direct ingestion of infective embryonated eggs from contaminated soil or litter
Ingestion of earthworms (paratenic hosts) that have consumed the eggs and concentrated the larvae
The eggs are highly resistant to environmental degradation, surviving for years in soil
diagnosis: Gross necropsy revealing numerous small, white nematodes within the tips of the ceca
Fecal flotation identifying characteristic thick-shelled, oval eggs (very similar to Ascaridia eggs but slightly smaller)
treatment: Administration of approved anthelmintics (e.g., fenbendazole, levamisole) in the drinking water or feed
Routine deworming is critical not just for worm removal, but to prevent Blackhead outbreaks
control: Implementing strict, scheduled deworming programs based on fecal egg counts
Rearing birds on wire or concrete to break the soil-borne life cycle
Absolute separation of chickens from turkeys, as chickens are the primary asymptomatic reservoirs for the worm and its protozoal hitchhiker
vaccineRecommendations: No vaccines are available for Heterakiasis.

diseases.name: Threadworm Infection (Strongyloidiasis)
diseases.description: Strongyloidiasis is caused by Strongyloides avium, a tiny nematode that embeds deeply within the mucosa of the ceca and small intestine. It causes severe, localized hemorrhagic enteritis. Because the larvae can penetrate intact skin, infections can rapidly escalate in poor, wet litter conditions.
clinicalSigns: Persistent, watery, or blood-tinged diarrhea
Severe weakness, depression, and marked weight loss
Poor feed conversion and stunted growth in younger birds
In heavy infestations, acute mortality may occur due to severe intestinal damage
Footpad lesions may occasionally be noted if larvae penetrate the skin of the feet
transmission: Fecal-oral ingestion of infective third-stage larvae from contaminated litter or soil
Direct percutaneous transmission (larvae burrowing directly through the skin of the feet or vent resting on wet litter)
The parasite can complete a free-living life cycle in warm, moist litter, multiplying outside the bird
diagnosis: Gross necropsy showing thickened, inflamed, and hemorrhagic cecal walls
Microscopic examination of mucosal scrapings is required, as the adult worms are extremely small (hair-like) and burrow deeply into the tissue
Fresh fecal examination (Baermann technique) to isolate actively moving larvae, rather than typical eggs
treatment: Broad-spectrum anthelmintics (e.g., fenbendazole, levamisole) are effective.
Treatment must be coupled with immediate environmental remediation to prevent rapid reinfection.
control: Maintaining exceptionally dry litter, as the free-living stages and infective larvae require moisture to survive and migrate
Routine sanitation and thorough clean-out between flocks
Rearing birds on wire floors in highly problematic endemic scenarios
vaccineRecommendations: No vaccines available.

diseases.name: Mycoplasma meleagridis Infection
diseases.description: Mycoplasma meleagridis (MM) is an egg-transmitted bacterial infection specific to turkeys. While M. gallisepticum affects multiple species, MM strictly targets turkeys, causing severe airsacculitis in newly hatched poults, skeletal deformities (TS-65 syndrome), and massive drops in hatchability.
clinicalSigns: Respiratory distress, gasping, and mild snicking in young poults
Stunted growth, poor feathering, and general unthriftiness
High incidence of skeletal deformities, specifically bowing of the tarsometatarsus and cervical vertebrae issues
Significant decrease in hatchability and late-stage embryonic mortality
Asymptomatic in adult breeder flocks, aside from the reproductive impact
transmission: Vertical transmission from infected breeder hens to the egg is the primary mode of disease dissemination
Venereal transmission during natural mating or artificial insemination with contaminated semen
Horizontal transmission via respiratory aerosols in the hatchery or brooder house
diagnosis: Gross necropsy of affected poults or dead-in-shell embryos revealing severe, cloudy airsacculitis with caseous exudate
Serology (RPA, HI, or ELISA) to monitor flock status
PCR testing of tracheal or cloacal swabs for specific and rapid confirmation
Culture on specialized Mycoplasma media
treatment: Strategic antibiotic treatment (e.g., tylosin, tiamulin) can reduce clinical signs and respiratory lesions in poults but will not eliminate the carrier state.
Treatment of breeder flocks is used to temporarily reduce egg transmission rates but does not achieve eradication.
control: The gold standard is complete eradication through the establishment and maintenance of MM-free primary breeder flocks
Egg dipping or heating (temperature differential treatments) combined with antibiotics can be used to eliminate the pathogen from hatching eggs
Strict biosecurity to prevent lateral introduction
vaccineRecommendations: No commercial vaccines are available. Control relies entirely on national eradication programs and purchasing poults from certified MM-free sources.

diseases.name: Duck Viral Enteritis (Duck Plague)
diseases.description: Duck Viral Enteritis, commonly known as Duck Plague, is an acute, highly contagious, and fatal herpesvirus infection of waterfowl (ducks, geese, and swans). It causes severe vascular damage, leading to massive internal hemorrhages, mucosal eruptions in the gastrointestinal tract, and high mortality in susceptible flocks.
clinicalSigns: Sudden, high mortality often preceded by extreme lethargy and photophobia
Watery, profuse diarrhea that is often blood-stained
Severe ocular and nasal discharge, sometimes gluing the eyelids shut
Birds exhibiting drooping wings, ruffled feathers, and extreme thirst
Prolapse of the penis in mature drakes
transmission: Direct horizontal transmission via contact with infected birds or asymptomatic carriers
Indirect transmission through water contaminated with feces or oral secretions
Wild waterfowl serve as the primary natural reservoir and vector
diagnosis: Gross necropsy revealing pathognomonic eruptive lesions (diphtheritic plaques) in the esophagus, cloaca, and intestines
Petechial hemorrhages across visceral organs and free blood in the body cavities
PCR detection of Anatid alphaherpesvirus 1 from liver or spleen tissues
Virus isolation on duck embryo fibroblasts
treatment: No specific antiviral treatment is available.
In the face of an outbreak, immediate vaccination of unaffected birds in the flock can rapidly halt the spread due to viral interference.
control: Strict exclusion of wild waterfowl from commercial duck facilities and open water sources
Rigorous cleaning and chlorination of drinking water
Immediate depopulation and proper disposal of infected flocks in severe outbreaks
vaccineRecommendations: Live attenuated vaccines are highly effective and are routinely administered subcutaneously or intramuscularly to commercial ducks and breeder flocks.

diseases.name: Duck Viral Hepatitis
diseases.description: Duck Viral Hepatitis (DVH) is an acute, highly fatal, and rapidly spreading viral disease primarily affecting young ducklings under 6 weeks of age. Caused predominantly by Duck Hepatitis A Virus (a picornavirus), it induces massive hepatic necrosis and severe neurological spasms immediately prior to death.
clinicalSigns: Extremely rapid onset; ducklings may appear healthy and die within hours
Classic terminal spasms: ducklings fall on their sides, paddle their legs, and draw their heads sharply back (opisthotonos)
Severe lethargy, huddling, and reluctance to move
Mortality can approach 100% in highly susceptible flocks under 3 weeks of age
transmission: Horizontal transmission via the fecal-oral route or inhalation
The virus is highly contagious and spreads rapidly through contaminated water, feed, and equipment
Wild birds and rodents can act as mechanical vectors
diagnosis: Clinical presentation of acute mortality with opisthotonos in young ducklings
Gross necropsy revealing an enlarged, friable liver covered in extensive petechial and ecchymotic hemorrhages
RT-PCR detection of the virus from liver tissue for rapid confirmation
Virus isolation in duck embryos
treatment: No specific treatment exists for the viral infection.
In emergency situations during an outbreak, administration of hyperimmune serum or egg yolk antibodies can be highly effective in saving exposed, early-stage ducklings.
control: Strict biosecurity and complete isolation of different age groups, particularly protecting ducklings under 4 weeks of age
Thorough cleaning, disinfection, and extended downtime between flocks
vaccineRecommendations: Live attenuated vaccines are administered to breeder ducks to ensure robust maternal antibody transfer to progeny. If breeders are unvaccinated, ducklings can be vaccinated directly at 1 day of age.

diseases.name: Turkey Viral Hepatitis
diseases.description: Turkey Viral Hepatitis is a highly contagious, subclinical to mildly acute viral disease specific to turkey poults, caused by a distinct picornavirus. While mortality is typically low, it causes focal hepatic necrosis and can severely impair early performance and flock uniformity.
clinicalSigns: Often subclinical, with infections passing unnoticed in robust flocks
Mild to severe depression, lethargy, and huddling in affected poults
Occasional watery diarrhea and sudden death in a small percentage of the flock
Decreased feed consumption and uneven growth rates
transmission: Horizontal transmission via the fecal-oral route is the primary method of spread
The virus is exceptionally stable in the environment, surviving in feces and litter for extended periods
Mechanical transmission via contaminated equipment and personnel
diagnosis: Gross necropsy revealing an enlarged liver dotted with numerous greyish-white necrotic foci, and an enlarged, pale pancreas
Histopathology confirming focal necrosis and mononuclear infiltration in the liver
Virus isolation from liver tissue via yolk sac inoculation in embryonated turkey eggs
PCR testing for specific viral identification
treatment: No specific antiviral treatment is available.
Supportive care including vitamins and high-quality nutrition to mitigate the impact on early growth.
control: Strict all-in/all-out production cycles
Thorough cleaning, chemical disinfection, and prolonged downtime of brooding facilities to reduce environmental viral loads
vaccineRecommendations: No commercial vaccines are currently available or routinely required for Turkey Viral Hepatitis.

diseases.name: Quail Bronchitis
diseases.description: Quail Bronchitis is a highly contagious and severe respiratory disease primarily affecting bobwhite quail, caused by a Fowl Adenovirus (FAdV serotype 1). It causes acute respiratory distress, tracheal lesions, and high mortality in young quail, while causing drops in egg production in adults.
clinicalSigns: Acute onset of sneezing, coughing, and rattling respiratory sounds
Severe gasping and dyspnea, often with the beak open
Huddling, severe lethargy, and rapidly progressing weakness
Mortality can exceed 50% in quail chicks under 4 weeks of age
Adults are typically asymptomatic but may experience a transient drop in egg production
transmission: Highly infectious horizontal transmission via inhalation of respiratory aerosols and ingestion of contaminated feces
Vertical transmission from infected breeders through the egg to progeny is suspected and plays a role in endemic cycles
The virus is highly resilient in the environment
diagnosis: Clinical presentation of acute respiratory disease and high mortality in young quail
Gross necropsy revealing excessive mucus in the trachea, airsacculitis, and occasionally pale, enlarged livers with necrotic foci
Virus isolation from the trachea or lungs using embryonated eggs
PCR to detect FAdV-1
treatment: No specific antiviral treatment is available.
Increasing brooder temperatures slightly and reducing environmental stressors may help decrease mortality.
Antibiotics are ineffective against the virus but may be used to control secondary bacterial infections.
control: Strict isolation of quail flocks from chickens (which can act as asymptomatic carriers of FAdV-1)
Rigorous biosecurity and all-in/all-out management
Eradication of infected breeder flocks if vertical transmission is problematic
vaccineRecommendations: No commercial vaccines are widely available specifically for Quail Bronchitis.

diseases.name: Avian Nephritis
diseases.description: Avian Nephritis is an acute viral infection primarily affecting young chickens, caused by the Avian Nephritis Virus (ANV), an astrovirus. It targets the kidneys, causing mild to severe interstitial nephritis, diarrhea, and growth retardation. It often contributes significantly to the Runting-Stunting Syndrome complex.
clinicalSigns: Outbreaks of watery diarrhea resulting in wet litter
Stunted growth, uneven flock sizes, and poor feed conversion
Mild depression and lethargy in young chicks (usually under 3 weeks of age)
Mild, transient increases in early chick mortality
Often subclinical, presenting only as a failure to meet performance targets
transmission: Fecal-oral transmission is the primary route, with the virus shed in high concentrations in the feces
Vertical transmission from infected breeders is possible but horizontal spread is more clinically significant
The virus is robust and persists in contaminated poultry environments
diagnosis: Gross necropsy revealing pale, swollen kidneys and visceral gout in severe cases
Histopathology of the kidneys demonstrating characteristic interstitial nephritis and degeneration of the proximal convoluted tubules
RT-PCR detection of ANV from kidney tissue or cloacal swabs
ELISA for flock serological profiling
treatment: No specific antiviral treatment is available.
Supportive care is essential: providing high-quality, easily digestible feed, electrolytes, and ensuring ad libitum water access to help flush the kidneys.
control: Strict all-in/all-out production cycles combined with thorough cleaning and disinfection to lower environmental viral loads
Ensuring maternal antibody transfer by allowing breeder flocks to seroconvert naturally prior to lay
vaccineRecommendations: No commercial vaccines are available for Avian Nephritis Virus.

diseases.name: Big Liver and Spleen Disease
diseases.description: Big Liver and Spleen Disease (BLS) is a viral infection primarily affecting broiler breeders and commercial layers, caused by Avian Hepatitis E Virus (a hepevirus). It is characterized by sudden drops in egg production, slightly elevated mortality, and massive enlargement of the liver and spleen.
clinicalSigns: Sudden, unexplained drop in egg production (often 10% to 20%) spanning several weeks
Slight increase in flock mortality (often 1-2% above normal)
Enlarged abdomen due to severe hepatosplenomegaly
Pale comb and wattles, with general lethargy in severely affected birds
Decreased hatchability in breeder flocks
transmission: Fecal-oral route via ingestion of contaminated feces, water, or feed
Horizontal transmission is rapid within a flock
Vertical transmission from breeders to progeny is suspected but the definitive mechanism remains under investigation
diagnosis: Gross necropsy reveals a massively enlarged, pale, and friable liver, often with subcapsular hemorrhages or a mottled appearance, and an enlarged spleen
Must be carefully differentiated from Avian Leukosis, Marek's Disease, and Fatty Liver Hemorrhagic Syndrome
RT-PCR detection of Avian Hepatitis E Virus from liver tissue or bile
ELISA to detect specific antibodies in flock serum
treatment: No specific treatment exists for the viral infection.
Supportive care, including vitamin supplementation, may help mitigate production losses and support liver recovery.
control: Strict biosecurity and excellent sanitation protocols
Thorough cleaning and disinfection of breeder houses between flocks to prevent environmental carryover
vaccineRecommendations: No commercial vaccines are currently available for BLS.

diseases.name: Turkey Meningoencephalitis
diseases.description: Turkey Meningoencephalitis is a severe, mosquito-borne viral disease of turkeys caused by the Israel Turkey Meningoencephalitis Virus (ITV), a flavivirus. It is characterized by progressive neurological degradation, severe paralysis, and high mortality, predominantly affecting turkeys raised in endemic regions (primarily the Middle East and parts of Africa).
clinicalSigns: Progressive incoordination (ataxia) leading to severe staggering
Drooping of the wings and progressive paralysis of the legs
Torticollis (wry neck) and severe head tremors
Profound lethargy, anorexia, and drop in egg production
High mortality in unprotected flocks, particularly during peak mosquito seasons
transmission: Transmitted exclusively through the bite of infected mosquitoes (primarily Culex species)
Not contagious via direct contact between birds
diagnosis: Clinical presentation of severe paralysis and neurological signs in turkeys during mosquito season
Histopathology revealing non-suppurative meningoencephalitis with perivascular cuffing in the brain and spinal cord
Virus isolation from brain tissue or RT-PCR detection of the flavivirus
Serology (HI or ELISA) for flock confirmation
treatment: No specific antiviral treatment is available.
Supportive care is generally ineffective due to the progressive nature of the neurological damage.
control: Rigorous vector control programs aimed at reducing mosquito populations around turkey farms
Eliminating standing water and applying appropriate larvicides and adulticides
Screening of houses where feasible
vaccineRecommendations: Live attenuated vaccines are highly effective and are routinely administered in endemic regions to prevent outbreaks.

diseases.name: Avian Spirochetosis
diseases.description: Avian Spirochetosis is an acute, tick-borne septicemic disease of poultry caused by the bacterium Borrelia anserina. It causes severe fever, destruction of red blood cells, and extensive tissue damage, leading to high morbidity and mortality, particularly in regions where the soft tick vector (Argas persicus) is endemic.
clinicalSigns: Severe depression, lethargy, and huddling
Initial high fever followed by subnormal temperatures prior to death
Comb and wattles may initially be pale (anemia) and subsequently turn purple/dark red (cyanosis) due to septic shock
Greenish, mucoid diarrhea
Paresis or paralysis in the terminal stages of the disease
transmission: Transmitted exclusively through the bite of infected soft ticks (Argas spp.) or occasionally red mites (Dermanyssus gallinae)
Ticks harbor the bacteria for years and pass it to their offspring (transovarial transmission)
Infection can also occur if birds ingest infected ticks
diagnosis: Identification of the highly motile, spiral-shaped bacteria in wet mounts of fresh blood examined under dark-field microscopy during the febrile stage
Giemsa-stained blood smears revealing the spirochetes among erythrocytes
Gross necropsy showing a massively enlarged, mottled spleen and pale, friable liver
treatment: Antibiotics such as penicillins, tetracyclines, and tylosin are highly effective if administered early in the course of the disease.
Treatment must be paired with immediate vector control to prevent reinfection.
control: Aggressive eradication of the Argas persicus tick from the poultry environment
Ticks hide in deep cracks and crevices of wooden structures; thorough application of approved acaricides under high pressure is required
Replacing old, cracked wooden roosts and housing materials
vaccineRecommendations: Inactivated bacterins are available in highly endemic regions and provide solid protection, though vector eradication remains the primary control method.

diseases.name: Streptococcosis
diseases.description: Streptococcosis in poultry refers to acute or chronic systemic infections caused predominantly by Streptococcus gallolyticus, Streptococcus zooepidemicus, or Enterococcus faecalis. It often presents as acute septicemia causing sudden death, or as a chronic localized infection causing endocarditis or arthritis.
clinicalSigns: In acute septicemia: sudden death with few premonitory signs, severe lethargy, and ruffled feathers
Pale comb and wattles, with general weakness and depression
Yellowish, watery diarrhea
In chronic forms: lameness due to arthritis, or emaciation and sudden heart failure due to vegetative endocarditis
Decreased egg production in layers
transmission: Horizontal transmission via ingestion of contaminated feed, water, or feces
Entry through skin abrasions, wounds, or compromised mucosal surfaces (often secondary to viral challenges or poor litter conditions)
Some strains have zoonotic potential, requiring careful handling of infected flocks
diagnosis: Gross necropsy revealing generalized septicemia: enlarged spleen, hepatomegaly, and fibrin deposition on the liver and heart
Vegetative endocarditis (cauliflower-like growths on heart valves) is a classic sign of chronic infection
Bacterial culture and isolation of Gram-positive cocci in chains from heart blood or affected organs
treatment: Antibiotics such as penicillins, erythromycin, or amoxicillin administered via drinking water.
Antimicrobial susceptibility testing (AST) is highly recommended as resistance patterns vary widely.
control: Maintaining excellent environmental sanitation and dry litter to prevent skin abrasions
Rigorous cleaning and disinfection of water lines
Minimizing stress and controlling primary immunosuppressive diseases
vaccineRecommendations: Commercial vaccines are not generally available; autogenous bacterins may be used in specific, recurring breeder flock cases.

diseases.name: Campylobacteriosis
diseases.description: Campylobacteriosis in poultry is primarily an asymptomatic colonization of the gastrointestinal tract by Campylobacter jejuni and C. coli. While it rarely causes clinical disease in the birds themselves, poultry are the most significant reservoir for human Campylobacteriosis, representing a massive public health and food safety challenge.
clinicalSigns: Typically completely asymptomatic; birds grow and perform normally despite heavy intestinal colonization
In rare instances involving young or highly stressed birds, it may cause mild, transient watery diarrhea
Slight reductions in weight gain or feed efficiency in heavily infected broiler flocks
transmission: Rapid horizontal spread via the fecal-oral route within a flock
Introduction to the house via contaminated water, insects (flies, beetles), rodents, and personnel footwear
Colonization typically occurs rapidly around 2-3 weeks of age in broilers
diagnosis: Diagnostic efforts are primarily focused on food safety screening rather than clinical diagnosis
Isolation of the bacterium from cloacal swabs, cecal contents, or environmental drag swabs
Requires specialized microaerophilic culture conditions and selective media (e.g., Campy-Cefex agar), or rapid PCR screening
treatment: Antibiotic treatment is not recommended or performed to clear Campylobacter from poultry, as it is largely ineffective at eliminating colonization and promotes severe antimicrobial resistance.
control: Strict implementation of rigorous biosecurity measures: dedicated footwear/clothing, rodent control, and fly screening
Water sanitation using organic acids or chlorination
Pre-slaughter feed withdrawal and meticulous processing plant hygiene to minimize carcass cross-contamination
Use of competitive exclusion products (probiotics) to reduce cecal colonization
vaccineRecommendations: No effective commercial vaccines are currently available, though development is a major area of active research for food safety purposes.

diseases.name: Water Deprivation Syndrome
diseases.description: Water Deprivation Syndrome occurs when flocks are deprived of drinking water due to mechanical failure, freezing, or mismanagement, followed by sudden reintroduction. This causes severe dehydration, increased blood sodium levels, and upon re-drinking, catastrophic osmotic shock leading to brain edema and acute mortality.
clinicalSigns: Extreme restlessness and frantic searching for water initially
Progressing to severe lethargy, cyanosis (darkening) of the comb, and dry, shriveled skin
Impacted, doughy crop contents due to lack of fluid for digestion
Upon reintroduction of water: convulsions, ataxia, and sudden massive mortality (water intoxication)
transmission: Non-infectious. Entirely an environmental and management-related crisis.
diagnosis: History of mechanical failure in water lines or sudden changes in water pressure
Necropsy reveals severe dehydration (sticky subcutaneous tissues, dark musculature) or, if water was reintroduced, massive cerebral edema
Kidneys may be pale and swollen with urate deposits
treatment: If caught during the deprivation phase: Water MUST be reintroduced gradually. Provide water for 15 minutes, then turn it off for an hour, repeating this cycle to prevent osmotic shock and brain edema.
Electrolytes can be added once the birds are stabilized.
control: Routine, daily inspection of all water lines, regulators, and nipple drinkers
Installation of automated water consumption monitoring systems with built-in alarms for flow interruption
Ensuring backup water systems are functional
vaccineRecommendations: Not applicable.

diseases.name: Ionophore Toxicity
diseases.description: Ionophore Toxicity occurs when poultry ingest toxic levels of ionophore anticoccidials (e.g., monensin, salinomycin, narasin), either through feed mixing errors or concurrent administration with incompatible drugs like tiamulin. It causes severe, acute muscle degeneration (myopathy) and fatal cardiovascular collapse.
clinicalSigns: Sudden onset of severe leg weakness, ataxia, and inability to stand
Birds resting on their breasts with legs extended backward or forward
Drooping wings and tremors
Reluctance or inability to access feed and water
Sudden mortality due to respiratory or cardiac muscle failure
transmission: Non-infectious. Caused strictly by dietary intoxication or adverse drug interactions.
diagnosis: Clinical history of recent feed delivery or administration of tiamulin/erythromycin while on ionophores
Gross necropsy revealing pale streaks in the skeletal muscles (thigh, breast) and pale, flabby heart muscle
Histopathology confirms severe myonecrosis
Chemical analysis of the feed identifying toxic levels of the specific ionophore
treatment: Immediate removal of the toxic feed
No specific antidote exists; survivors may have permanent muscle damage and poor subsequent growth rates.
control: Rigorous quality control and equipment flushing at the feed mill to prevent cross-contamination
Strict veterinary oversight to prevent prescribing incompatible macrolide or pleuromutilin antibiotics to flocks receiving ionophores
vaccineRecommendations: Not applicable.

diseases.name: Copper Toxicity
diseases.description: Copper Toxicity typically arises from over-supplementation or prolonged use of copper sulfate in drinking water (often used erroneously as a treatment for Candidiasis or as an algae controller). It causes severe caustic damage to the upper gastrointestinal tract, gizzard erosion, and massive liver accumulation.
clinicalSigns: Severe reduction in feed and water intake due to painful oral and digestive lesions
Dark, sticky, or black droppings (melena) resulting from upper GI tract bleeding
Oral lesions and ulcerations
Progressive weight loss, unthriftiness, and stunting
Increased mortality in prolonged exposures
transmission: Non-infectious. Iatrogenic (caused by medical/management intervention) due to overdosing in water lines.
diagnosis: History of adding copper sulfate to the water system
Gross necropsy showing a distinctly blue-green tint to the crop contents and severe erosion/ulceration of the gizzard lining
Liver may be enlarged and pale
Toxicological analysis showing extremely high copper levels in liver tissue
treatment: Immediate cessation of copper sulfate administration
Flushing water lines thoroughly with clean, fresh water
Providing supportive vitamins (especially Vitamin K for bleeding) and soft, easily digestible feed
control: Strict adherence to dosage guidelines if copper sulfate is used therapeutically, restricting use to a maximum of 3-5 days
Using safer alternatives for water sanitation and antifungal treatments
vaccineRecommendations: Not applicable.

diseases.name: Deep Pectoral Myopathy (Green Muscle Disease)
diseases.description: Deep Pectoral Myopathy, commonly known as Green Muscle Disease, is an ischemic necrosis of the supracoracoideus (minor pectoral) muscle. It occurs in heavy, fast-growing broilers and breeders when vigorous wing flapping causes the muscle to swell, cutting off its own blood supply within its tight fascial compartment, leading to tissue death and a green discoloration.
clinicalSigns: Completely subclinical in the live bird; birds show no obvious signs of illness, pain, or altered gait
May occasionally present as slight unilateral swelling of the breast muscle if palpated immediately after the inciting event
Becomes a massive economic issue at the processing plant due to downgrading and trimming of affected breast fillets
transmission: Non-infectious. It is a physiological condition driven by modern broiler genetics (large breast muscles) combined with sudden exertion (wing flapping).
diagnosis: Diagnosed almost exclusively post-mortem at the processing plant
The inner breast fillet (tender) appears swollen and pale (acute phase), progressing to a striking bright green, firm, and necrotic lesion (chronic phase)
treatment: No treatment is possible or necessary for the live bird, as the condition is painless once the muscle tissue dies, but the meat is condemned.
control: Minimizing flock disturbances, loud noises, and sudden light changes to prevent panic and excessive wing flapping
Gentle handling during catching and loading
Genetic selection by primary breeding companies to improve the vascularization and fascial elasticity of the breast muscles
vaccineRecommendations: Not applicable.

diseases.name: Tibial Dyschondroplasia (TD)

symptomsEnhanced.name: Gait, Reluctance to stand or walk

diseases.description: Tibial Dyschondroplasia (TD) is a prevalent metabolic bone disease in rapidly growing broilers and turkeys. It is characterized by an avascular plug of unmineralized cartilage in the growth plate of the proximal tibiotarsus, leading to severe bone bowing, lameness, and susceptibility to fractures.
clinicalSigns: Bowing of the legs, either outward or inward, usually visible by 3 to 5 weeks of age
Reluctance to move, resting on hocks, and an uneven, swaying gait
Swelling around the upper hock joint
Poor weight gain due to inability to compete for feed
High incidence of leg condemnations at processing
transmission: Non-infectious. It is a complex metabolic disorder influenced by rapid growth genetics, calcium/phosphorus imbalances, acid-base imbalances (excess chloride), and mycotoxins (fusarochromanone).
diagnosis: Gross necropsy is definitive: longitudinal splitting of the proximal tibiotarsus reveals a massive, opaque, white plug of unvascularized cartilage extending down into the metaphysis
Can also be diagnosed in live birds using specialized portable Lixiscope (low-intensity X-ray) technology
treatment: Treatment is generally ineffective once the cartilaginous plug has formed and the bone has bowed.
Severely lame birds must be humanely culled.
control: Formulating diets to maintain optimal Calcium to Phosphorus ratios and careful management of dietary electrolytes (Sodium, Potassium, Chloride) to prevent metabolic acidosis
Ensuring adequate Vitamin D3 supplementation
Slowing early growth rates using modified lighting programs
vaccineRecommendations: Not applicable.

diseases.name: Spiking Mortality Syndrome
diseases.description: Spiking Mortality Syndrome (SMS), often linked to Hypoglycemia-Spiking Mortality Syndrome, is a condition in broiler chickens characterized by sudden, sharp spikes in mortality (typically between 12 and 18 days of age) associated with severe hypoglycemia (low blood sugar). Its exact etiology is complex, likely involving emerging viruses (arenaviruses/astroviruses) triggering metabolic collapse.
clinicalSigns: Sudden, sharp increase in mortality lasting 3 to 5 days, then rapidly subsiding
Affected chicks show severe tremors, ataxia, and blindness just prior to death
Chicks frequently found lying on their breasts with legs extended, showing neurological spasms (hypoglycemic coma)
Loud, high-pitched vocalizations (distress chirping)
Survivors often become runts
transmission: The exact infectious trigger is debated, but it acts horizontally and is heavily linked to the buildup of viral loads in reused litter, exacerbated by darkling beetles.
diagnosis: Clinical history of a sharp, unexplained mortality spike around day 14
Blood biochemistry of dying chicks reveals profound, fatal hypoglycemia
Absence of gross lesions at necropsy, though the liver may be slightly pale or small
treatment: Immediate administration of sugar (glucose or sucrose) in the drinking water for 2-3 days to reverse hypoglycemia and halt mortality
Ensuring uninterrupted access to feed and extending lighting hours temporarily to encourage continuous eating
control: Aggressive control of darkling beetles (Alphitobius diaperinus) between flocks
Complete clean-out of reused litter if the farm has a history of SMS outbreaks
Avoiding prolonged periods of dark or feed withdrawal in young chicks
vaccineRecommendations: No vaccines available.

diseases.name: Fatty Liver and Kidney Syndrome (FLKS)
diseases.description: Fatty Liver and Kidney Syndrome (FLKS) is a fatal metabolic disorder affecting fast-growing broiler chickens, primarily caused by a marginal deficiency of Biotin combined with stress (such as brief fasting). It leads to severe hypoglycemia and massive lipid accumulation in the liver, kidneys, and myocardium.
clinicalSigns: Often affects the best-growing birds in the flock between 2 to 4 weeks of age
Sudden onset of severe lethargy; birds sit quietly and are reluctant to move
Progresses rapidly to coma and death within a few hours
No specific premonitory signs other than mild stunting in some affected birds
transmission: Non-infectious. It is a nutritional and metabolic failure triggered by environmental stress (like temporary feed or water deprivation).
diagnosis: Gross necropsy reveals excessively pale, swollen, and fatty liver and kidneys
The heart often shows distinct pale streaking due to lipid infiltration
Blood tests on dying birds reveal profound hypoglycemia
Diagnosis is confirmed by a rapid cessation of mortality following biotin supplementation
treatment: Immediate administration of Biotin via drinking water
Providing continuous access to feed and avoiding any fasting stress
control: Ensuring broiler diets are well fortified with bioavailable Biotin, especially when using high levels of wheat or barley (which have low biotin bioavailability)
Strict management to prevent any accidental feed or water outages
vaccineRecommendations: Not applicable.

diseases.name: Spondylolisthesis (Kinky Back)

symptomsEnhanced.name: Gait, Reluctance to stand or walk

diseases.description: Spondylolisthesis is a developmental skeletal deformity primarily affecting fast-growing broilers. It involves the ventral displacement (subluxation) of the 4th thoracic vertebra over the 5th. This pinches the spinal cord, leading to severe posterior paralysis. It presents clinically identical to bacterial Spondylitis but is non-infectious.
clinicalSigns: Classic posture: sitting on the hocks and tail, with the feet pointed straight out in front or slightly raised off the ground
Progressive inability to walk, progressing to complete hind-limb paralysis
Birds remain alert and will eat and drink if feed is placed directly within reach, but otherwise die of dehydration
Typically manifests between 3 and 6 weeks of age
transmission: Non-infectious. It is a developmental defect linked to rapid growth genetics and mechanical stress on the spine.
diagnosis: Clinical posture is highly indicative

Gross necropsy requires careful longitudinal splitting of the spinal column; observation of the physical deformation and pinching of the spinal cord at T4-T5, without the caseous abscesses characteristic of Enterococcus cecorum infection
treatment: No treatment is possible. The spinal cord damage is physical and permanent.
Humane culling of affected birds is required immediately.
control: Implementing early growth restriction programs (e.g., lighting manipulation or feed restriction) to allow skeletal strength to develop before heavy muscle mass accumulation
Genetic selection by primary breeders to improve spinal integrity
vaccineRecommendations: Not applicable.

diseases.name: Twisted Leg (Valgus-Varus Deformity)

symptomsEnhanced.name: Gait, Reluctance to stand or walk

diseases.description: Valgus-Varus Deformity (VVD), commonly called Twisted Leg, is the most common developmental bone deformity in heavy broilers and turkeys. It involves the lateral (valgus) or medial (varus) bending of the distal tibiotarsus and metatarsus, causing severe gait abnormalities and lameness.
clinicalSigns: Visible outward bowing (valgus, "knock-kneed") or inward bowing (varus, "bow-legged") of the legs
Awkward, waddling gait and reluctance to walk
Birds spend excessive time resting, leading to breast blisters and hock burns from wet litter
Reduced feed intake leading to poor growth and high culling rates
transmission: Non-infectious. It is a biomechanical failure linked to extremely rapid early growth, lack of exercise, and genetic predisposition.
diagnosis: Visual assessment of the live bird's stance and gait
Unlike perosis, the gastrocnemius tendon remains firmly in its groove, but the bone shaft itself is visibly bent and twisted
treatment: No medical or surgical treatment is practical in commercial poultry.
Severely affected birds must be culled.
control: Slowing the early growth curve using specific lighting programs (e.g., providing dark periods) to encourage activity and allow bone mineralization to catch up with weight gain
Ensuring optimal incubation conditions, as overheating during incubation can significantly increase the incidence of VVD
vaccineRecommendations: Not applicable.

diseases.name: Keel Bone Cyst (Breast Blister)
diseases.description: A Keel Bone Cyst, or Breast Blister, is an inflammatory, fluid-filled bursa that develops over the keel (breast) bone. It is an environmental management issue resulting from prolonged, continuous pressure and friction against hard surfaces or wet, crusted litter. It causes significant carcass downgrading at the processing plant.
clinicalSigns: Visible swelling, thickening, and redness of the skin directly over the keel bone
Loss of feathers over the affected breast area
Development of a soft, fluid-filled sac (blister) that may become firm, caseous, or ulcerated over time
Generally does not cause systemic illness or mortality, but indicates poor housing conditions or concurrent leg issues causing the bird to rest excessively
transmission: Non-infectious. Caused by mechanical trauma, though secondary bacterial infection (like Staphylococcus) can occur if the blister ulcerates.
diagnosis: Visual inspection and palpation of the breast area revealing the characteristic bursa
Detected frequently during processing plant inspections, leading to trimming and downgrading of the breast meat
treatment: No practical treatment for commercial flocks. The focus is entirely on prevention and environmental remediation.
In valuable breeding stock, severe infected blisters could theoretically be surgically drained, but this is rare.
control: Maintaining deep, dry, and friable litter to provide a soft resting surface
Preventing severe leg problems (like VVD or TD) which force birds to sit continuously
Ensuring proper design of roosts and equipment to prevent mechanical injury
vaccineRecommendations: Not applicable.

diseases.name: Round Heart Disease
diseases.description: Round Heart Disease, or Spontaneous Cardiomyopathy, is a condition primarily affecting young turkeys and occasionally broilers. It is characterized by profound dilation and hypertrophy of the heart muscle (especially the right ventricle), leading to congestive heart failure and sudden mortality.
clinicalSigns: Sudden death in apparently healthy, rapidly growing young birds
Enlarged, fluid-filled abdomen (ascites) secondary to heart failure
Severe lethargy, ruffled feathers, and drooping wings
Labored breathing or gasping due to pulmonary congestion
transmission: Non-infectious. It is considered a metabolic and potentially genetic condition, though environmental stressors (hypoxia, cold stress, or certain mycotoxins) can precipitate outbreaks.
diagnosis: Gross necropsy is definitive: the heart is massively enlarged, flabby, and distinctively round rather than its normal conical shape
The right ventricle is severely dilated and the myocardium is pale
Liver may be swollen and congested
treatment: No medical treatment is available or effective for the damaged heart.
Humane culling of birds showing severe ascites or lethargy.
control: Optimizing brooding temperatures to prevent cold stress
Ensuring excellent ventilation to provide maximum oxygen availability during the first 3 weeks of life
Genetic selection by primary breeders to eliminate susceptible lines
vaccineRecommendations: Not applicable.

diseases.name: Aortic Rupture
diseases.description: Aortic Rupture is a fatal cardiovascular event primarily affecting rapidly growing, heavy male turkeys (toms) between 8 and 20 weeks of age. It occurs due to the dissecting aneurysm and subsequent rupture of the abdominal aorta, leading to massive and immediate internal exsanguination.
clinicalSigns: Absolutely no premonitory signs; birds are typically the largest, healthiest-looking in the flock
Sudden death, often following a brief period of wing flapping or convulsions
Profound paleness of the head, comb, and wattles immediately upon death due to total blood loss
transmission: Non-infectious. It is a physiological structural failure linked to high blood pressure (hypertension) inherent in heavy turkey genetics.
Can be exacerbated by marginal dietary copper deficiency (which weakens the arterial elastin) or high-fat diets.
diagnosis: Necropsy reveals a massive, clotted pool of blood in the abdominal cavity
Careful cleaning of the blood will reveal a longitudinal tear in the abdominal aorta, usually near the kidneys
treatment: Treatment is impossible as the condition is instantly fatal.
control: Implementing restricted feeding or lighting programs to slightly slow the growth rate of male turkeys
Ensuring diets are well-fortified with bioavailable Copper, which is essential for the cross-linking of elastin and collagen in blood vessels
Minimizing flock panic or sudden loud noises which spike blood pressure
vaccineRecommendations: Not applicable.

diseases.name: Crop Impaction
diseases.description: Crop Impaction, sometimes presenting as Pendulous Crop, is a physical obstruction or neuromuscular failure of the crop. It prevents feed from passing into the proventriculus. It can be caused by gorging on tough fibrous material (like long grass or litter), severe water deprivation, or neural damage (such as from Marek's disease).
clinicalSigns: Grossly enlarged, firm, or doughy crop that fails to empty overnight
In pendulous crop, the crop hangs low, swinging heavily against the breast
Foul, sour odor from the beak due to feed fermenting inside the impacted crop
Progressive weight loss and emaciation despite a full-looking breast area
Occasional regurgitation or choking
transmission: Non-infectious physical condition, though it may be a secondary symptom of an infectious viral disease targeting nerves (Marek's).
diagnosis: Palpation of the crop reveals a hard, impacted mass of feed/fibers or a liquid-filled, flaccid sac
Observation that the crop does not empty after a 12-hour period of feed withdrawal
treatment: For mild impactions: massaging the crop gently with warm water or mineral oil to break up the mass
For severe impactions in valuable birds: surgical opening of the crop (ingluviotomy) to physically remove the blockage and flush the mucosa
If pendulous crop is due to nerve damage, it is incurable and requires culling
control: Preventing access to long, tough, fibrous grasses or poor-quality shavings
Ensuring constant access to fresh water to facilitate normal crop emptying
Providing appropriate insoluble grit to aid mechanical digestion
vaccineRecommendations: Not applicable.

diseases.name: Vent Gleet (Cloacitis)
diseases.description: Vent Gleet, or Cloacitis, is a chronic inflammatory condition of the cloaca and surrounding tissues. It is frequently associated with fungal overgrowth (Candida albicans) or secondary bacterial infections following digestive upset. It causes severe irritation and a foul-smelling discharge.
clinicalSigns: Feathers below the vent are matted, crusted, and soiled with a whitish, foul-smelling discharge
The vent itself appears red, swollen, ulcerated, and highly irritated
Frequent passage of loose, watery droppings
Loss of feathers around the vent due to constant pecking or chemical burns from the discharge
Decreased egg production and reluctance to mate
transmission: Can be transmitted venereally from hen to hen via the rooster in breeder flocks
Often arises spontaneously due to dysbiosis (imbalance of gut flora) caused by stress or prolonged antibiotic use
diagnosis: Visual examination of the characteristic pasty, smelly vent and inflamed cloacal lips
Swabbing the vent for fungal or bacterial culture can confirm the specific opportunistic pathogen
treatment: Washing the vent area thoroughly with mild soapy water or chlorhexidine to remove the crust
Applying topical antifungal/antibacterial creams directly to the cloaca
Adding probiotics or apple cider vinegar to the drinking water to restore a healthy, acidic gut microbiome
control: Maintaining excellent gut health through balanced diets and the judicious use of antibiotics
Temporarily removing roosters from affected breeder pens to prevent venereal spread
Providing clean, dry litter to prevent environmental contamination of the vent
vaccineRecommendations: Not applicable.

diseases.name: Egg Bound (Dystocia)

symptomsEnhanced.name: Gait, Reluctance to stand or walk

diseases.description: Being 'Egg Bound' (Dystocia) is a critical, life-threatening reproductive emergency where a hen is unable to pass an egg through the oviduct and cloaca. It is commonly caused by abnormally large or malformed eggs, calcium deficiency leading to weak uterine muscle contractions, obesity, or premature onset of lay.
clinicalSigns: Bird repeatedly visiting the nest box but failing to lay
Straining, pumping the tail, and adopting a wide, penguin-like stance
Severe lethargy, closed eyes, and sitting on the floor
The vent may appear swollen, red, and the egg may sometimes be visible or palpable just inside the cloaca
Rapid progression to shock and death if the egg compresses the kidneys or major blood vessels
transmission: Non-infectious physical and metabolic emergency.
diagnosis: Clinical observation of straining and characteristic posture
Gentle digital palpation of the abdomen just below the vent will reveal a hard, retained egg in the reproductive tract
treatment: Immediate isolation to a warm, quiet, dark environment to relax the muscles
Oral administration of liquid calcium (calcium gluconate) to stimulate uterine contractions
Gentle lubrication of the vent with water-based lubricant
As a last resort, a veterinarian may aspirate the egg contents with a needle and carefully collapse the shell, ensuring all fragments are removed
control: Ensuring proper pre-lay calcium loading in pullet diets
Managing body weight to prevent obesity, which restricts the pelvic opening
Using appropriate lighting programs to prevent pullets from coming into lay too early before their skeletal frames are fully mature
vaccineRecommendations: Not applicable.

diseases.name: Egg Yolk Peritonitis
diseases.description: Egg Yolk Peritonitis is a severe, often fatal inflammation and bacterial infection of the abdominal cavity (coelom). It occurs when a developing yolk misses the infundibulum (internal ovulation) or ruptures inside the body. The nutrient-rich yolk provides a perfect medium for opportunistic bacteria (like E. coli) to proliferate, causing massive peritonitis.
clinicalSigns: Cessation of egg production in an actively laying hen
Noticeably enlarged, heavy, and hot abdomen (often fluid-filled, feeling like a water balloon)
Severe lethargy, depression, and loss of appetite
Penguin-like stance due to abdominal pain and weight
Respiratory distress due to fluid pressure on the air sacs
transmission: Non-infectious primarily, though secondary E. coli infections (Avian Pathogenic E. coli - APEC) rapidly turn the condition fatal.
Can be triggered by rough handling of laying hens, high stress, or diseases causing erratic ovulation (like Avian Influenza or ND).
diagnosis: Clinical presentation of a swollen abdomen in a non-laying adult hen
Gross necropsy revealing the abdominal cavity filled with yellow, foul-smelling, cooked-egg-like caseous material or murky yellow fluid covering the intestines
Bacterial culture of the fluid (typically yielding E. coli)
treatment: Treatment is highly challenging and often unrewarding in commercial settings.
Broad-spectrum antibiotics can be administered, but they struggle to penetrate the thick caseous yolk masses in the abdomen.
Humane culling is often the most appropriate action.
control: Gentle handling of hens during peak lay to prevent physical rupture of developing follicles
Controlling viral respiratory diseases (IB, ND) that disrupt the normal function of the reproductive tract
Preventing obesity in breeder flocks
vaccineRecommendations: Autogenous E. coli vaccines can reduce the severity of secondary bacterial peritonitis in breeder flocks.

diseases.name: Vent Prolapse (Prolapse of the Oviduct)
diseases.description: Vent Prolapse is a management-related reproductive disorder where the lower part of the oviduct and cloaca fails to retract after oviposition (laying an egg) and remains exposed outside the body. This exposed, red tissue acts as a rapid trigger for other birds to attack, leading to fatal cannibalism and evisceration.
clinicalSigns: A red, swollen mass of tissue protruding from the vent
Presence of blood on the vent or surrounding feathers
Other birds actively pecking at the affected hen's rear
If undetected, the bird will be found dead with its lower intestines completely pecked out (eviscerated)
transmission: Non-infectious physical disorder.
Strongly associated with overweight hens, producing overly large/double-yolk eggs, insufficient calcium (muscle weakness), and high light intensity.
diagnosis: Direct visual observation of the everted cloacal tissue
In mortality cases, investigating whether death was primarily due to cannibalism triggered by a prolapse
treatment: Immediate removal and isolation of the affected bird to prevent cannibalism
Gently washing the prolapsed tissue, applying an anti-inflammatory ointment (like Preparation H or honey), and physically pushing the tissue back inside
Keeping the bird in a dark area to halt egg production temporarily and allow muscles to heal
If tissue is severely pecked or necrotic, humane culling is required
control: Controlling pullet body weight and lighting programs to ensure they do not start laying too early before physical maturity
Reducing light intensity in the laying house to minimize aggressive pecking behavior
Ensuring proper beak trimming protocols are followed
vaccineRecommendations: Not applicable.

diseases.name: Salpingitis

symptomsEnhanced.name: Egg shell(s), Misshapen (abnormal size or shape)

diseases.description: Salpingitis is a severe bacterial inflammation and infection of the oviduct, most commonly caused by ascending E. coli infections or descending Mycoplasma/IBV infections. It leads to the permanent destruction of the reproductive tract and the formation of large, caseous pus masses (commonly misidentified by farmers as 'lash eggs').
clinicalSigns: Permanent cessation of egg production or a severe drop in the flock
The hen may occasionally pass a rubbery, layered, yellowish-brown mass of pus and necrotic tissue (a 'lash egg')
Swollen, hard abdomen that feels firm upon palpation (unlike the fluid feel of peritonitis)
Progressive weight loss (loss of breast muscle) despite an enlarged abdomen
General unthriftiness and pale combs
transmission: Ascending infection from the cloaca (often due to vent pecking or poor sanitation)
Descending infection from the respiratory tract via the abdominal air sacs (common with E. coli and Mycoplasma gallisepticum)
diagnosis: Finding 'lash eggs' on the egg belt or in nesting boxes
Gross necropsy revealing a massively distended oviduct impacted with concentric layers of solid, foul-smelling, caseous exudate
Bacterial culture of the oviduct contents to identify the specific pathogen
treatment: Treatment is generally ineffective once the oviduct is impacted with caseous material, as antibiotics cannot penetrate the mass and the tissue damage is permanent.
Humane culling of affected, non-productive birds is economically and medically necessary.
control: Maintaining excellent respiratory health to prevent descending E. coli infections
Good nest box sanitation to prevent ascending infections from contaminated litter
Control of Mycoplasma and Infectious Bronchitis via vaccination programs
vaccineRecommendations: Vaccinating against primary respiratory triggers (IBV, NDV, MG) and using E. coli bacterins in breeder flocks to reduce systemic susceptibility.
